[[ja:葦津珍彦]]

'''Ashizu Ujihiko''' (葦津 珍彦, July 17, 1909 – June 10, 1992) was a Japanese {{ill|Shinto|ja|神道}} scholar. He was also renowned as a commentator of the {{ill|Nationalist|ja|民族派}} movement.<ref>Yukio Hori, ''Postwar Right-Wing Forces'', Keiso Shobō, 1993, Expanded Edition.</ref>
== Career ==
He was born as the eldest son of {{ill|Ashizu Kojiro|ja|葦津耕次郎}} in Hakozaki, Fukuoka Prefecture (now Hakozaki, {{ill|Higashi-ku, Fukuoka|ja|福岡市東区}}). The Ashizu family is a descendant of the Ogami family, one of the shrine families of {{ill|Hakozaki Shrine|ja|筥崎宮}}, and his uncle, {{ill|Ashizu Seizō|ja|葦津洗造}}, was the chief priest of the same shrine.<ref name="#1">Foreword by {{ill|Ashizu Yasukuni|ja|葦津泰國}}, from ''Living the Shōwa Era: The Claims of Ujihiko Ashizu Series IV''</ref>
In 1922 (Taishō 11), he entered {{ill|Tokyo Metropolitan Koishikawa High School|ja|東京都立小石川高等学校}} (then Tokyo Prefectural Fifth Junior High School). Around this time, he yearned for socialism, participated in the Social Science Study Group, and avidly read related books.<ref name="#1"/>
Those around him were concerned, and after graduation, he went on to {{ill|Kokugakuin University|ja|國學院大學}}.{{Citation needed|date=July 5, 2017}} He also enrolled in the {{ill|Tokyo School of Foreign Languages|ja|東京外国語学校 (旧制)}}, but dropped out of both.{{Citation needed|date=July 5, 2017}} In 1928 (Shōwa 3), he entered {{ill|Fukushima University|ja|福島大学}} (then Fukushima Higher Commercial School), but dropped out the following year.
In 1931, at the recommendation of a friend who belonged to the {{ill|Japanese Communist Party|ja|日本共産党}}, he wrote a leading article for the newspaper {{ill|Akahata|ja|しんぶん赤旗}}.<ref name=ashidu>{{Cite journal|author=Konno Nobuyuki|title=The Thought of Ashizu Ujihiko during and after the War—Focusing on His View of Shinto|journal=Interdisciplinary Research on 'Narratives' in Japan from 1890 to the 1950s|page=63|year=2012}}</ref> However, in February of the following year, under the influence of his father, he is said to have converted to traditional conservative imperial {{ill|Shinto|ja|神道}}, which aimed for the realization of "Japan, the Emperor's Nation with Unity of Rites and Government," like his father.<ref name=ashidu />
In 1932 (Shōwa 7), he succeeded his father's business and engaged in the construction of shrines and temples as the representative of a shrine and temple construction office.<ref>"Fifty Years of the Association of Shinto Shrines" (Non-commercial publication), May 1996, Publisher: {{ill|Association of Shinto Shrines|ja|神社本庁}}, p.74—Obituary of Ashizu Ujihiko, associate of {{ill|Jinja Shinpo Sha|ja|神社新報社}} (June 10)</ref> During this period, he interacted with {{ill|Toyama Mitsuru|ja|頭山満}}, {{ill|Teisuke Imaizumi|ja|今泉定助}}, {{ill|Takayama Noboru|ja|高山昇}}, {{ill|Ogata Taketora|ja|緒方竹虎}}, {{ill|Ryōhei Uchida|ja|内田良平}}, {{ill|Inoue Tadamaro|ja|井上孚麿}}, and others. Before the outbreak of the {{ill|Pacific War|ja|太平洋戦争}}, he opposed the war, viewing it as an "unwinnable battle," and after the outbreak, he engaged in unique political activities, such as insisting on adhering only to the war objectives shown in the Imperial Rescript on the outbreak of war, which was "to secure the survival of an independent nation as unavoidable."{{Citation needed|date=July 5, 2017}}
During the war, some of his critical articles on {{ill|Nazi Germany|ja|ナチス・ドイツ}}, Japan's ally, and the policies of the {{ill|Hideki Tōjō|ja|東條英機}} Cabinet were banned, and he was arrested at times.{{Citation needed|date=July 5, 2017}}
Immediately after the war, he dissolved his company and assisted {{ill|Shigeru Yoshida (Home Ministry official)|ja|吉田茂 (内務官僚)}}, {{ill|Munenori Miyagawa|ja|宮川宗徳}} (later Secretary-General of the main office), and {{ill|Tokugawa Munetake|ja|徳川宗敬}} (later Chief of the main office), dedicating himself to the establishment of the {{ill|Association of Shinto Shrines|ja|神社本庁}}. Furthermore, when the '{{ill|Jinja Shinpo|ja|神社新報}}' was launched as part of the main office's educational public relations, he took charge of the management of {{ill|Jinja Shinpo Sha|ja|神社新報社}}, the publishing body, along with being the chief editor.
Even after his retirement in 1968 (Shōwa 43), he continued his writing activities and the guidance of future generations, being at the forefront of movements to protect the {{ill|Kokutai|ja|国体}} and {{ill|Shinto shrines|ja|神社}}. He continued to have a significant influence on the Shinto world (through the {{ill|National Council to Defend Japan|ja|日本を守る国民会議}}, now {{ill|Nippon Kaigi|ja|日本会議}}) and {{ill|Nationalist student movements|ja|民族派学生組織}} ({{ill|Japan Youth Council|ja|日本青年協議会}}), among others.
On the other hand, he questioned the enshrinement of {{ill|Class A war criminals|ja|A級戦犯}} like Tōjō, whom he had criticized during the war, due to their responsibility for the defeat.<ref>"Yasukuni: Where to Go from 'Postwar' 3—Article by Eminent Scholar of Shinto History Raises Doubts about 'Class A Enshrinement'," ''Mainichi Shimbun'' Tokyo edition morning edition: August 8, 2006</ref> (Note: {{ill|Yasukuni Shrine|ja|靖国神社}} is not a {{ill|Bekkaku Kanpeisha|ja|別表神社}} of the Association of Shinto Shrines.)
In 1992 (Heisei 4), he passed away at his home in {{ill|Kamakura|ja|鎌倉市}} at the age of 82.
{{ill|Bunzō Hashikawa|ja|橋川文三}}, who had experiences of debates with Ashizu, praised him as "an advanced polemicist among conservatives," noting that his theory of the Emperor system "should be noted as surpassing the level of traditional right-wingers." He pointed out that Ashizu tried to grasp the problem of the Emperor system from a comparative institutional perspective, based on research in European political thought history, pursuing an open form of "Kokutai theory" as his characteristic.<ref>From ''Postwar Japanese Thought Series 7: Conservative Thought,'' edited by Bunzō Hashikawa, Chikuma Shobō, 1968</ref>
{{ill|Yoshio Ōishi|ja|大石義雄}}, in ''Twenty Years of the Constitution'', praised Ashizu's ''Words of a Native'' and described Ashizu as "a modern-day Kitabatake Chikafusa."
After the war, he had friendships that transcended ideological positions with {{ill|Saburō Ichi'i|ja|市井三郎}} and {{ill|Shunsuke Tsurumi|ja|鶴見俊輔}}, who were editors of ''{{ill|Shisō no Kagaku|ja|思想の科学}}'', and the critic {{ill|Yoshihiko Takeuchi|ja|竹内好}}, and he often contributed to the same magazine.
He was also well-versed in Korean culture and had interactions with {{ill|Lyuh Woon-hyung|ja|呂運亨}}, an independence activist in Korea during the Japanese occupation. It is said that when Ashizu visited the {{ill|Korea University (Japan)|ja|朝鮮大学校}} (in Japan), he was moved upon seeing a portrait of {{ill|Kim Il-sung|ja|金日成}} in the middle of the reception room, and remarked, "This is what current Japanese people lack. In the past, the 'Imperial Portrait' of the Emperor was enshrined in every school; we need to restore that situation."<ref>{{Cite book |author=Hideki Kajimura |title=Overcoming Exclusionism: A History of Korea |publisher=Heibonsha Library |year=2014 |page=25}}</ref>
== Family ==
[[File:Iwasaki Seishichi and his friends.jpg|thumb|Nara Tarō Ōmiwa (center of back row)]]
* Father: '''{{ill|Ashizu Kojiro|ja|葦津耕次郎}}''' (1878–1940) – Shintoist.<ref name=nishiya>{{Cite journal|author=Takafumi Nishiya |title=Ashizu Kojiro: The Trajectory of a Shinto Practitioner during the 'State Shinto' Period |volume=Kyoto University Doctoral Thesis |issue=No.14958 |year=2009 |naid=500000489926 |url=https://hdl.handle.net/2433/126522}}</ref> He served at {{ill|Hakozaki Shrine|ja|筥崎宮}}, devoted himself to Shinto service, and later became active as a businessman running mining and shrine construction businesses, while also engaging as a commentator expressing views on political and social issues from the perspective of Shinto and shrines, such as the {{ill|Korean Shrine Deity Controversy|ja|朝鮮神宮御祭神論争}}.<ref>[http://kaeizyuku.com/2020/01/14/visit-graves/ January 14, 2020 'Visit to the Ashizu Family Grave'] Kaei-juku</ref>
* Grandfather: '''Ashizu Isoo''' (–1903) – Chief priest of Hakozaki Shrine.<ref name=asia>Uraube Noboru, [http://asiarestoration.com/wp-content/uploads/2016/06/daiajia1st.pdf 'Daijia wo Tsuranuita Ōmiwa Asahei'], ''Dai Ajia'' inaugural issue, June 30, 2016, Dai Ajia Kenkyūkai</ref> For a time, he also concurrently served as chief priest of the {{ill|Kashii Shrine|ja|香椎宮}}.<ref>[https://dl.ndl.go.jp/info:ndljp/pid/2947481/9 Official Gazette. June 26, 1897]</ref>
* Great-uncle: '''{{ill|Chōbei Ōmiwa|ja|大三輪長兵衛}}''' (1835–1908) – Brother of his grandfather. After working at an oil shop and rice shop in Osaka, he opened a Matsumae merchant house,<ref>[https://dl.ndl.go.jp/info:ndljp/pid/778835/97 'Chōbei Ōmiwa', ''Meiji Fugo Chifujidai'', Sumitei Inshi (Daigakukan, 1902)]</ref> and later made a fortune in cotton trade, serving as president of the 58th National Bank, chairman of the Osaka Clearing House, Osaka Prefectural Assembly chairman, and Osaka City Council chairman, among other positions. In 1898, he became a member of the {{ill|House of Representatives|ja|衆議院}}.<ref name=sangyo/><ref>[https://www.ndl.go.jp/portrait/datas/36/ 'Chōbei Ōmiwa'], Portraits of Modern Japanese Historical Figures, National Diet Library</ref><ref>[https://kotobank.jp/word/大三輪長兵衛-1061443 'Chōbei Ōmiwa'], Kotobank</ref><ref>[http://www.pref.osaka.lg.jp/attach/3059/00006976/seifuku-itiran_20200522.pdf 'List of Former Chairmen and Vice-Chairmen of Osaka Prefectural Assembly'], Osaka Prefecture, 2020</ref>
* Son: '''{{ill|Ashizu Yasukuni|ja|葦津泰國}}''' (1937–2024) – Graduate of Tokyo Metropolitan University. Former president of Jinja Shinpo Sha. Author of ''The Life of Chōbei Ōmiwa'' (Ashizu Office, 2008), among others.
== Works ==
[Full list of his publications, including major works and collected editions.]
=== Collected Works ===
* ''Ashizu Ujihiko Selected Works'' – Edited by the same Editorial Committee<ref>Each with commentary by {{ill|Mitsuo Suzuki|ja|鈴木満男}} (a disciple)</ref>, Jinja Shinpo Sha, 1996 1. ''Emperor, Shinto, Constitution'' 2. ''As Successors of the Restoration'' 3. ''Current Affairs and Character Critiques''
* ''"Let's Read the Shōwa Era": Ashizu Ujihiko's Claims Series'' – Ashizu Office, 2005–2007 1. ''Japan's Monarchy: Studies on the Emperor System'' 2. ''Eternal Restorationist'' 3. ''The End of Modern Democracy: Revival of Japanese Thought'' 4. ''Words of a Native: The Logic of Trust and Loyalty'' 5. ''Greater Asianism and Toyama Mitsuru'' 6. Those around him were concerned, and after graduation, he went on to {{ill|Kokugakuin University|ja|國學院大學}}.{{Citation needed|date=July 5, 2017}} He also enrolled in the {{ill|Tokyo School of Foreign Languages|ja|東京外国語学校 (旧制)}}, but dropped out of both.{{Citation needed|date=July 5, 2017}} In 1928 (Shōwa 3), he entered {{ill|Fukushima University|ja|福島大学}} (then Fukushima Higher Commercial School), but dropped out the following year.
In 1931, at the recommendation of a friend who belonged to the {{ill|Japanese Communist Party|ja|日本共産党}}, he wrote a leading article for the newspaper {{ill|Akahata|ja|しんぶん赤旗}}.<ref name=ashidu>{{Cite journal|author=Konno Nobuyuki|title=The Thought of Ashizu Ujihiko during and after the War—Focusing on His View of Shinto|journal=Interdisciplinary Research on 'Narratives' in Japan from 1890 to the 1950s|page=63|year=2012}}</ref> However, in February of the following year, under the influence of his father, he is said to have converted to traditional conservative imperial {{ill|Shinto|ja|神道}}, which aimed for the realization of "Japan, the Emperor's Nation with Unity of Rites and Government," like his father.<ref name=ashidu />
In 1932 (Shōwa 7), he succeeded his father's business and engaged in the construction of shrines and temples as the representative of a shrine and temple construction office.<ref>"Fifty Years of the Association of Shinto Shrines" (Non-commercial publication), May 1996, Publisher: {{ill|Association of Shinto Shrines|ja|神社本庁}}, p.74—Obituary of Ashizu Ujihiko, associate of {{ill|Jinja Shinpo Sha|ja|神社新報社}} (June 10)</ref> During this period, he interacted with {{ill|Toyama Mitsuru|ja|頭山満}}, {{ill|Teisuke Imaizumi|ja|今泉定助}}, {{ill|Takayama Noboru|ja|高山昇}}, {{ill|Ogata Taketora|ja|緒方竹虎}}, {{ill|Ryōhei Uchida|ja|内田良平}}, {{ill|Inoue Tadamaro|ja|井上孚麿}}, and others. Before the outbreak of the {{ill|Pacific War|ja|太平洋戦争}}, he opposed the war, viewing it as an "unwinnable battle," and after the outbreak, he engaged in unique political activities, such as insisting on adhering only to the war objectives shown in the Imperial Rescript on the outbreak of war, which was "to secure the survival of an independent nation as unavoidable."{{Citation needed|date=July 5, 2017}}
During the war, some of his critical articles on {{ill|Nazi Germany|ja|ナチス・ドイツ}}, Japan's ally, and the policies of the {{ill|Hideki Tōjō|ja|東條英機}} Cabinet were banned, and he was arrested at times.{{Citation needed|date=July 5, 2017}}
Immediately after the war, he dissolved his company and assisted {{ill|Shigeru Yoshida (Home Ministry official)|ja|吉田茂 (内務官僚)}}, {{ill|Munenori Miyagawa|ja|宮川宗徳}} (later Secretary-General of the main office), and {{ill|Tokugawa Munetake|ja|徳川宗敬}} (later Chief of the main office), dedicating himself to the establishment of the {{ill|Association of Shinto Shrines|ja|神社本庁}}. Furthermore, when the '{{ill|Jinja Shinpo|ja|神社新報}}' was launched as part of the main office's educational public relations, he took charge of the management of {{ill|Jinja Shinpo Sha|ja|神社新報社}}, the publishing body, along with being the chief editor.
Even after his retirement in 1968 (Shōwa 43), he continued his writing activities and the guidance of future generations, being at the forefront of movements to protect the {{ill|Kokutai|ja|国体}} and {{ill|Shinto shrines|ja|神社}}. He continued to have a significant influence on the Shinto world (through the {{ill|National Council to Defend Japan|ja|日本を守る国民会議}}, now {{ill|Nippon Kaigi|ja|日本会議}}) and {{ill|Nationalist student movements|ja|民族派学生組織}} ({{ill|Japan Youth Council|ja|日本青年協議会}}), among others.
On the other hand, he questioned the enshrinement of {{ill|Class A war criminals|ja|A級戦犯}} like Tōjō, whom he had criticized during the war, due to their responsibility for the defeat.<ref>"Yasukuni: Where to Go from 'Postwar' 3—Article by Eminent Scholar of Shinto History Raises Doubts about 'Class A Enshrinement'," ''Mainichi Shimbun'' Tokyo edition morning edition: August 8, 2006</ref> (Note: {{ill|Yasukuni Shrine|ja|靖国神社}} is not a {{ill|Bekkaku Kanpeisha|ja|別表神社}} of the Association of Shinto Shrines.)
In 1992 (Heisei 4), he passed away at his home in {{ill|Kamakura|ja|鎌倉市}} at the age of 82.
{{ill|Bunzō Hashikawa|ja|橋川文三}}, who had experiences of debates with Ashizu, praised him as "an advanced polemicist among conservatives," noting that his theory of the Emperor system "should be noted as surpassing the level of traditional right-wingers." He pointed out that Ashizu tried to grasp the problem of the Emperor system from a comparative institutional perspective, based on research in European political thought history, pursuing an open form of "Kokutai theory" as his characteristic.<ref>From ''Postwar Japanese Thought Series 7: Conservative Thought,'' edited by Bunzō Hashikawa, Chikuma Shobō, 1968</ref>
{{ill|Yoshio Ōishi|ja|大石義雄}}, in ''Twenty Years of the Constitution'', praised Ashizu's ''Words of a Native'' and described Ashizu as "a modern-day Kitabatake Chikafusa."
After the war, he had friendships that transcended ideological positions with {{ill|Saburō Ichi'i|ja|市井三郎}} and {{ill|Shunsuke Tsurumi|ja|鶴見俊輔}}, who were editors of ''{{ill|Shisō no Kagaku|ja|思想の科学}}'', and the critic {{ill|Yoshihiko Takeuchi|ja|竹内好}}, and he often contributed to the same magazine.
He was also well-versed in Korean culture and had interactions with {{ill|Lyuh Woon-hyung|ja|呂運亨}}, an independence activist in Korea during the Japanese occupation. It is said that when Ashizu visited the {{ill|Korea University (Japan)|ja|朝鮮大学校}} (in Japan), he was moved upon seeing a portrait of {{ill|Kim Il-sung|ja|金日成}} in the middle of the reception room, and remarked, "This is what current Japanese people lack. In the past, the 'Imperial Portrait' of the Emperor was enshrined in every school; we need to restore that situation."<ref>{{Cite book |author=Hideki Kajimura |title=Overcoming Exclusionism: A History of Korea |publisher=Heibonsha Library |year=2014 |page=25}}</ref>
== Family ==
[[File:Iwasaki Seishichi and his friends.jpg|thumb|Nara Tarō Ōmiwa (center of back row)]]
* Father: '''{{ill|Ashizu Kojiro|ja|葦津耕次郎}}''' (1878–1940) – Shintoist.<ref name=nishiya>{{Cite journal|author=Takafumi Nishiya |title=Ashizu Kojiro: The Trajectory of a Shinto Practitioner during the 'State Shinto' Period |volume=Kyoto University Doctoral Thesis |issue=No.14958 |year=2009 |naid=500000489926 |url=https://hdl.handle.net/2433/126522}}</ref> He served at {{ill|Hakozaki Shrine|ja|筥崎宮}}, devoted himself to Shinto service, and later became active as a businessman running mining and shrine construction businesses, while also engaging as a commentator expressing views on political and social issues from the perspective of Shinto and shrines, such as the {{ill|Korean Shrine Deity Controversy|ja|朝鮮神宮御祭神論争}}.<ref>[http://kaeizyuku.com/2020/01/14/visit-graves/ January 14, 2020 'Visit to the Ashizu Family Grave'] Kaei-juku</ref>
* Grandfather: '''Ashizu Isoo''' (–1903) – Chief priest of Hakozaki Shrine.<ref name=asia>Uraube Noboru, [http://asiarestoration.com/wp-content/uploads/2016/06/daiajia1st.pdf 'Daijia wo Tsuranuita Ōmiwa Asahei'], ''Dai Ajia'' inaugural issue, June 30, 2016, Dai Ajia Kenkyūkai</ref> For a time, he also concurrently served as chief priest of the {{ill|Kashii Shrine|ja|香椎宮}}.<ref>[https://dl.ndl.go.jp/info:ndljp/pid/2947481/9 Official Gazette. June 26, 1897]</ref>
* Great-uncle: '''{{ill|Chōbei Ōmiwa|ja|大三輪長兵衛}}''' (1835–1908) – Brother of his grandfather. After working at an oil shop and rice shop in Osaka, he opened a Matsumae merchant house,<ref>[https://dl.ndl.go.jp/info:ndljp/pid/778835/97 'Chōbei Ōmiwa', ''Meiji Fugo Chifujidai'', Sumitei Inshi (Daigakukan, 1902)]</ref> and later made a fortune in cotton trade, serving as president of the 58th National Bank, chairman of the Osaka Clearing House, Osaka Prefectural Assembly chairman, and Osaka City Council chairman, among other positions. In 1898, he became a member of the {{ill|House of Representatives|ja|衆議院}}.<ref name=sangyo/><ref>[https://www.ndl.go.jp/portrait/datas/36/ 'Chōbei Ōmiwa'], Portraits of Modern Japanese Historical Figures, National Diet Library</ref><ref>[https://kotobank.jp/word/大三輪長兵衛-1061443 'Chōbei Ōmiwa'], Kotobank</ref><ref>[http://www.pref.osaka.lg.jp/attach/3059/00006976/seifuku-itiran_20200522.pdf 'List of Former Chairmen and Vice-Chairmen of Osaka Prefectural Assembly'], Osaka Prefecture, 2020</ref>
* Son: '''{{ill|Ashizu Yasukuni|ja|葦津泰國}}''' (1937–2024) – Graduate of Tokyo Metropolitan University. Former president of Jinja Shinpo Sha. Author of ''The Life of Chōbei Ōmiwa'' (Ashizu Office, 2008), among others.
== Works ==
[Full list of his publications, including major works and collected editions.]
=== Collected Works ===
* ''Ashizu Ujihiko Selected Works'' – Edited by the same Editorial Committee<ref>Each with commentary by {{ill|Mitsuo Suzuki|ja|鈴木満男}} (a disciple)</ref>, Jinja Shinpo Sha, 1996 1. ''Emperor, Shinto, Constitution'' 2. ''As Successors of the Restoration'' 3. ''Current Affairs and Character Critiques''
* ''"Let's Read the Shōwa Era": Ashizu Ujihiko's Claims Series'' – Ashizu Office, 2005–2007 1. ''Japan's Monarchy: Studies on the Emperor System'' 2. ''Eternal Restorationist'' 3. ''The End of Modern Democracy: Revival of Japanese Thought'' 4. ''Words of a Native: The Logic of Trust and Loyalty'' 5. ''Greater Asianism and Toyama Mitsuru'' 6. ''Living the Shōwa Era: The Spirit and Thoughts of the People of the Divine Land''
== Footnotes ==
{{Reflist|30em}}
== References ==
* ''Meiji Restoration and the Liberation of the East'', "Preface (Kazuma Shindō)", Shin Seiryoku Sha, 1964 * ''Ashizu Ujihiko Memorial Record'', Konihon Sha, 1993 * ''Ashizu Ujihiko Selected Works Volume 3'', Jinja Shinpo Sha, 1996, commentary and chronology at the end * Yukio Hori, ''Postwar Right-Wing Forces'', "Chapter 3: Ideology of the Postwar Right Wing, Section 2: Thoughts of Ashizu Ujihiko—Ideology of the Association of Shinto Shrines", Keiso Shobō, 1993, Expanded Edition

[[ja:葦津珍彦]]

revision.text:
'''Ashizu Ujihiko''' (葦津 珍彦, July 17, 1909 – June 10, 1992) was a Japanese {{ill|Shinto|ja|神道}} scholar. He was also renowned as a commentator of the {{ill|Nationalist|ja|民族派}} movement.<ref>Yukio Hori, ''Postwar Right-Wing Forces'', Keiso Shobō, 1993, Expanded Edition.</ref>
== Career ==
He was born as the eldest son of {{ill|Ashizu Kojiro|ja|葦津耕次郎}} in Hakozaki, Fukuoka Prefecture (now Hakozaki, {{ill|Higashi-ku, Fukuoka|ja|福岡市東区}}). The Ashizu family is a descendant of the Ogami family, one of the shrine families of {{ill|Hakozaki Shrine|ja|筥崎宮}}, and his uncle, {{ill|Ashizu Seizō|ja|葦津洗造}}, was the chief priest of the same shrine.<ref name="#1">Foreword by {{ill|Ashizu Yasukuni|ja|葦津泰國}}, from ''Living the Shōwa Era: The Claims of Ujihiko Ashizu Series IV''</ref>
In 1922 (Taishō 11), he entered {{ill|Tokyo Metropolitan Koishikawa High School|ja|東京都立小石川高等学校}} (then Tokyo Prefectural Fifth Junior High School). Around this time, he yearned for socialism, participated in the Social Science Study Group, and avidly read related books.<ref name="#1"/>
Those around him were concerned, and after graduation, he went on to {{ill|Kokugakuin University|ja|國學院大學}}.{{Citation needed|date=July 5, 2017}} He also enrolled in the {{ill|Tokyo School of Foreign Languages|ja|東京外国語学校 (旧制)}}, but dropped out of both.{{Citation needed|date=July 5, 2017}} In 1928 (Shōwa 3), he entered {{ill|Fukushima University|ja|福島大学}} (then Fukushima Higher Commercial School), but dropped out the following year.
In 1931, at the recommendation of a friend who belonged to the {{ill|Japanese Communist Party|ja|日本共産党}}, he wrote a leading article for the newspaper {{ill|Akahata|ja|しんぶん赤旗}}.<ref name=ashidu>{{Cite journal|author=Konno Nobuyuki|title=The Thought of Ashizu Ujihiko during and after the War—Focusing on His View of Shinto|journal=Interdisciplinary Research on 'Narratives' in Japan from 1890 to the 1950s|page=63|year=2012}}</ref> However, in February of the following year, under the influence of his father, he is said to have converted to traditional conservative imperial {{ill|Shinto|ja|神道}}, which aimed for the realization of "Japan, the Emperor's Nation with Unity of Rites and Government," like his father.<ref name=ashidu />
In 1932 (Shōwa 7), he succeeded his father's business and engaged in the construction of shrines and temples as the representative of a shrine and temple construction office.<ref>"Fifty Years of the Association of Shinto Shrines" (Non-commercial publication), May 1996, Publisher: {{ill|Association of Shinto Shrines|ja|神社本庁}}, p.74—Obituary of Ashizu Ujihiko, associate of {{ill|Jinja Shinpo Sha|ja|神社新報社}} (June 10)</ref> During this period, he interacted with {{ill|Toyama Mitsuru|ja|頭山満}}, {{ill|Teisuke Imaizumi|ja|今泉定助}}, {{ill|Takayama Noboru|ja|高山昇}}, {{ill|Ogata Taketora|ja|緒方竹虎}}, {{ill|Ryōhei Uchida|ja|内田良平}}, {{ill|Inoue Tadamaro|ja|井上孚麿}}, and others. Before the outbreak of the {{ill|Pacific War|ja|太平洋戦争}}, he opposed the war, viewing it as an "unwinnable battle," and after the outbreak, he engaged in unique political activities, such as insisting on adhering only to the war objectives shown in the Imperial Rescript on the outbreak of war, which was "to secure the survival of an independent nation as unavoidable."{{Citation needed|date=July 5, 2017}}
During the war, some of his critical articles on {{ill|Nazi Germany|ja|ナチス・ドイツ}}, Japan's ally, and the policies of the {{ill|Hideki Tōjō|ja|東條英機}} Cabinet were banned, and he was arrested at times.{{Citation needed|date=July 5, 2017}}
Immediately after the war, he dissolved his company and assisted {{ill|Shigeru Yoshida (Home Ministry official)|ja|吉田茂 (内務官僚)}}, {{ill|Munenori Miyagawa|ja|宮川宗徳}} (later Secretary-General of the main office), and {{ill|Tokugawa Munetake|ja|徳川宗敬}} (later Chief of the main office), dedicating himself to the establishment of the {{ill|Association of Shinto Shrines|ja|神社本庁}}. Furthermore, when the '{{ill|Jinja Shinpo|ja|神社新報}}' was launched as part of the main office's educational public relations, he took charge of the management of {{ill|Jinja Shinpo Sha|ja|神社新報社}}, the publishing body, along with being the chief editor.
Even after his retirement in 1968 (Shōwa 43), he continued his writing activities and the guidance of future generations, being at the forefront of movements to protect the {{ill|Kokutai|ja|国体}} and {{ill|Shinto shrines|ja|神社}}. He continued to have a significant influence on the Shinto world (through the {{ill|National Council to Defend Japan|ja|日本を守る国民会議}}, now {{ill|Nippon Kaigi|ja|日本会議}}) and {{ill|Nationalist student movements|ja|民族派学生組織}} ({{ill|Japan Youth Council|ja|日本青年協議会}}), among others.
On the other hand, he questioned the enshrinement of {{ill|Class A war criminals|ja|A級戦犯}} like Tōjō, whom he had criticized during the war, due to their responsibility for the defeat.<ref>"Yasukuni: Where to Go from 'Postwar' 3—Article by Eminent Scholar of Shinto History Raises Doubts about 'Class A Enshrinement'," ''Mainichi Shimbun'' Tokyo edition morning edition: August 8, 2006</ref> (Note: {{ill|Yasukuni Shrine|ja|靖国神社}} is not a {{ill|Bekkaku Kanpeisha|ja|別表神社}} of the Association of Shinto Shrines.)
In 1992 (Heisei 4), he passed away at his home in {{ill|Kamakura|ja|鎌倉市}} at the age of 82.
{{ill|Bunzō Hashikawa|ja|橋川文三}}, who had experiences of debates with Ashizu, praised him as "an advanced polemicist among conservatives," noting that his theory of the Emperor system "should be noted as surpassing the level of traditional right-wingers." He pointed out that Ashizu tried to grasp the problem of the Emperor system from a comparative institutional perspective, based on research in European political thought history, pursuing an open form of "Kokutai theory" as his characteristic.<ref>From ''Postwar Japanese Thought Series 7: Conservative Thought,'' edited by Bunzō Hashikawa, Chikuma Shobō, 1968</ref>
{{ill|Yoshio Ōishi|ja|大石義雄}}, in ''Twenty Years of the Constitution'', praised Ashizu's ''Words of a Native'' and described Ashizu as "a modern-day Kitabatake Chikafusa."
After the war, he had friendships that transcended ideological positions with {{ill|Saburō Ichi'i|ja|市井三郎}} and {{ill|Shunsuke Tsurumi|ja|鶴見俊輔}}, who were editors of ''{{ill|Shisō no Kagaku|ja|思想の科学}}'', and the critic {{ill|Yoshihiko Takeuchi|ja|竹内好}}, and he often contributed to the same magazine.
He was also well-versed in Korean culture and had interactions with {{ill|Lyuh Woon-hyung|ja|呂運亨}}, an independence activist in Korea during the Japanese occupation. It is said that when Ashizu visited the {{ill|Korea University (Japan)|ja|朝鮮大学校}} (in Japan), he was moved upon seeing a portrait of {{ill|Kim Il-sung|ja|金日成}} in the middle of the reception room, and remarked, "This is what current Japanese people lack. In the past, the 'Imperial Portrait' of the Emperor was enshrined in every school; we need to restore that situation."<ref>{{Cite book |author=Hideki Kajimura |title=Overcoming Exclusionism: A History of Korea |publisher=Heibonsha Library |year=2014 |page=25}}</ref>
== Family ==
[[File:Iwasaki Seishichi and his friends.jpg|thumb|Nara Tarō Ōmiwa (center of back row)]]
* Father: '''{{ill|Ashizu Kojiro|ja|葦津耕次郎}}''' (1878–1940) – Shintoist.<ref name=nishiya>{{Cite journal|author=Takafumi Nishiya |title=Ashizu Kojiro: The Trajectory of a Shinto Practitioner during the 'State Shinto' Period |volume=Kyoto University Doctoral Thesis |issue=No.14958 |year=2009 |naid=500000489926 |url=https://hdl.handle.net/2433/126522}}</ref> He served at {{ill|Hakozaki Shrine|ja|筥崎宮}}, devoted himself to Shinto service, and later became active as a businessman running mining and shrine construction businesses, while also engaging as a commentator expressing views on political and social issues from the perspective of Shinto and shrines, such as the {{ill|Korean Shrine Deity Controversy|ja|朝鮮神宮御祭神論争}}.<ref>[http://kaeizyuku.com/2020/01/14/visit-graves/ January 14, 2020 'Visit to the Ashizu Family Grave'] Kaei-juku</ref>
* Grandfather: '''Ashizu Isoo''' (–1903) – Chief priest of Hakozaki Shrine.<ref name=asia>Uraube Noboru, [http://asiarestoration.com/wp-content/uploads/2016/06/daiajia1st.pdf 'Daijia wo Tsuranuita Ōmiwa Asahei'], ''Dai Ajia'' inaugural issue, June 30, 2016, Dai Ajia Kenkyūkai</ref> For a time, he also concurrently served as chief priest of the {{ill|Kashii Shrine|ja|香椎宮}}.<ref>[https://dl.ndl.go.jp/info:ndljp/pid/2947481/9 Official Gazette. June 26, 1897]</ref>
* Great-uncle: '''{{ill|Chōbei Ōmiwa|ja|大三輪長兵衛}}''' (1835–1908) – Brother of his grandfather. After working at an oil shop and rice shop in Osaka, he opened a Matsumae merchant house,<ref>[https://dl.ndl.go.jp/info:ndljp/pid/778835/97 'Chōbei Ōmiwa', ''Meiji Fugo Chifujidai'', Sumitei Inshi (Daigakukan, 1902)]</ref> and later made a fortune in cotton trade, serving as president of the 58th National Bank, chairman of the Osaka Clearing House, Osaka Prefectural Assembly chairman, and Osaka City Council chairman, among other positions. In 1898, he became a member of the {{ill|House of Representatives|ja|衆議院}}.<ref name=sangyo/><ref>[https://www.ndl.go.jp/portrait/datas/36/ 'Chōbei Ōmiwa'], Portraits of Modern Japanese Historical Figures, National Diet Library</ref><ref>[https://kotobank.jp/word/大三輪長兵衛-1061443 'Chōbei Ōmiwa'], Kotobank</ref><ref>[http://www.pref.osaka.lg.jp/attach/3059/00006976/seifuku-itiran_20200522.pdf 'List of Former Chairmen and Vice-Chairmen of Osaka Prefectural Assembly'], Osaka Prefecture, 2020</ref>
* Son: '''{{ill|Ashizu Yasukuni|ja|葦津泰國}}''' (1937–2024) – Graduate of Tokyo Metropolitan University. Former president of Jinja Shinpo Sha. Author of ''The Life of Chōbei Ōmiwa'' (Ashizu Office, 2008), among others.
== Works ==
[Full list of his publications, including major works and collected editions.]
=== Collected Works ===
* ''Ashizu Ujihiko Selected Works'' – Edited by the same Editorial Committee<ref>Each with commentary by {{ill|Mitsuo Suzuki|ja|鈴木満男}} (a disciple)</ref>, Jinja Shinpo Sha, 1996 1. ''Emperor, Shinto, Constitution'' 2. ''As Successors of the Restoration'' 3. ''Current Affairs and Character Critiques''

[[ja:葦津珍彦]]

revision.comment: removed [[Category:Unsorted link categories]]; added [[Category:Link incomplete]] using [[Help:Gadget-HotCat|HotCat]]
revision.text:
'''Ashizu Ujihiko''' (葦津 珍彦, July 17, 1909 – June 10, 1992) was a Japanese {{ill|Shinto|ja|神道}} scholar. He was also renowned as a commentator of the {{ill|Nationalist|ja|民族派}} movement.<ref>Yukio Hori, ''Postwar Right-Wing Forces'', Keiso Shobō, 1993, Expanded Edition.</ref>
== Career ==
He was born as the eldest son of {{ill|Ashizu Kojiro|ja|葦津耕次郎}} in Hakozaki, Fukuoka Prefecture (now Hakozaki, {{ill|Higashi-ku, Fukuoka|ja|福岡市東区}}). The Ashizu family is a descendant of the Ogami family, one of the shrine families of {{ill|Hakozaki Shrine|ja|筥崎宮}}, and his uncle, {{ill|Ashizu Seizō|ja|葦津洗造}}, was the chief priest of the same shrine.<ref name="#1">Foreword by {{ill|Ashizu Yasukuni|ja|葦津泰國}}, from ''Living the Shōwa Era: The Claims of Ujihiko Ashizu Series IV''</ref>
In 1922 (Taishō 11), he entered {{ill|Tokyo Metropolitan Koishikawa High School|ja|東京都立小石川高等学校}} (then Tokyo Prefectural Fifth Junior High School). Around this time, he yearned for socialism, participated in the Social Science Study Group, and avidly read related books.<ref name="#1"/>
Those around him were concerned, and after graduation, he went on to {{ill|Kokugakuin University|ja|國學院大學}}.{{Citation needed|date=July 5, 2017}} He also enrolled in the {{ill|Tokyo School of Foreign Languages|ja|東京外国語学校 (旧制)}}, but dropped out of both.{{Citation needed|date=July 5, 2017}} In 1928 (Shōwa 3), he entered {{ill|Fukushima University|ja|福島大学}} (then Fukushima Higher Commercial School), but dropped out the following year.
In 1931, at the recommendation of a friend who belonged to the {{ill|Japanese Communist Party|ja|日本共産党}}, he wrote a leading article for the newspaper {{ill|Akahata|ja|しんぶん赤旗}}.<ref name=ashidu>{{Cite journal|author=Konno Nobuyuki|title=The Thought of Ashizu Ujihiko during and after the War—Focusing on His View of Shinto|journal=Interdisciplinary Research on 'Narratives' in Japan from 1890 to the 1950s|page=63|year=2012}}</ref> However, in February of the following year, under the influence of his father, he is said to have converted to traditional conservative imperial {{ill|Shinto|ja|神道}}, which aimed for the realization of "Japan, the Emperor's Nation with Unity of Rites and Government," like his father.<ref name=ashidu />
In 1932 (Shōwa 7), he succeeded his father's business and engaged in the construction of shrines and temples as the representative of a shrine and temple construction office.<ref>"Fifty Years of the Association of Shinto Shrines" (Non-commercial publication), May 1996, Publisher: {{ill|Association of Shinto Shrines|ja|神社本庁}}, p.74—Obituary of Ashizu Ujihiko, associate of {{ill|Jinja Shinpo Sha|ja|神社新報社}} (June 10)</ref> During this period, he interacted with {{ill|Toyama Mitsuru|ja|頭山満}}, {{ill|Teisuke Imaizumi|ja|今泉定助}}, {{ill|Takayama Noboru|ja|高山昇}}, {{ill|Ogata Taketora|ja|緒方竹虎}}, {{ill|Ryōhei Uchida|ja|内田良平}}, {{ill|Inoue Tadamaro|ja|井上孚麿}}, and others. Before the outbreak of the {{ill|Pacific War|ja|太平洋戦争}}, he opposed the war, viewing it as an "unwinnable battle," and after the outbreak, he engaged in unique political activities, such as insisting on adhering only to the war objectives shown in the Imperial Rescript on the outbreak of war, which was "to secure the survival of an independent nation as unavoidable."{{Citation needed|date=July 5, 2017}}
During the war, some of his critical articles on {{ill|Nazi Germany|ja|ナチス・ドイツ}}, Japan's ally, and the policies of the {{ill|Hideki Tōjō|ja|東條英機}} Cabinet were banned, and he was arrested at times.{{Citation needed|date=July 5, 2017}}
Immediately after the war, he dissolved his company and assisted {{ill|Shigeru Yoshida (Home Ministry official)|ja|吉田茂 (内務官僚)}}, {{ill|Munenori Miyagawa|ja|宮川宗徳}} (later Secretary-General of the main office), and {{ill|Tokugawa Munetake|ja|徳川宗敬}} (later Chief of the main office), dedicating himself to the establishment of the {{ill|Association of Shinto Shrines|ja|神社本庁}}. Furthermore, when the '{{ill|Jinja Shinpo|ja|神社新報}}' was launched as part of the main office's educational public relations, he took charge of the management of {{ill|Jinja Shinpo Sha|ja|神社新報社}}, the publishing body, along with being the chief editor.
Even after his retirement in 1968 (Shōwa 43), he continued his writing activities and the guidance of future generations, being at the forefront of movements to protect the {{ill|Kokutai|ja|国体}} and {{ill|Shinto shrines|ja|神社}}. He continued to have a significant influence on the Shinto world (through the {{ill|National Council to Defend Japan|ja|日本を守る国民会議}}, now {{ill|Nippon Kaigi|ja|日本会議}}) and {{ill|Nationalist student movements|ja|民族派学生組織}} ({{ill|Japan Youth Council|ja|日本青年協議会}}), among others.
On the other hand, he questioned the enshrinement of {{ill|Class A war criminals|ja|A級戦犯}} like Tōjō, whom he had criticized during the war, due to their responsibility for the defeat.<ref>"Yasukuni: Where to Go from 'Postwar' 3—Article by Eminent Scholar of Shinto History Raises Doubts about 'Class A Enshrinement'," ''Mainichi Shimbun'' Tokyo edition morning edition: August 8, 2006</ref> (Note: {{ill|Yasukuni Shrine|ja|靖国神社}} is not a {{ill|Bekkaku Kanpeisha|ja|別表神社}} of the Association of Shinto Shrines.)
In 1992 (Heisei 4), he passed away at his home in {{ill|Kamakura|ja|鎌倉市}} at the age of 82.
{{ill|Bunzō Hashikawa|ja|橋川文三}}, who had experiences of debates with Ashizu, praised him as "an advanced polemicist among conservatives," noting that his theory of the Emperor system "should be noted as surpassing the level of traditional right-wingers." He pointed out that Ashizu tried to grasp the problem of the Emperor system from a comparative institutional perspective, based on research in European political thought history, pursuing an open form of "Kokutai theory" as his characteristic.<ref>From ''Postwar Japanese Thought Series 7: Conservative Thought,'' edited by Bunzō Hashikawa, Chikuma Shobō, 1968</ref>
{{ill|Yoshio Ōishi|ja|大石義雄}}, in ''Twenty Years of the Constitution'', praised Ashizu's ''Words of a Native'' and described Ashizu as "a modern-day Kitabatake Chikafusa."
After the war, he had friendships that transcended ideological positions with {{ill|Saburō Ichi'i|ja|市井三郎}} and {{ill|Shunsuke Tsurumi|ja|鶴見俊輔}}, who were editors of ''{{ill|Shisō no Kagaku|ja|思想の科学}}'', and the critic {{ill|Yoshihiko Takeuchi|ja|竹内好}}, and he often contributed to the same magazine.
He was also well-versed in Korean culture and had interactions with {{ill|Lyuh Woon-hyung|ja|呂運亨}}, an independence activist in Korea during the Japanese occupation. It is said that when Ashizu visited the {{ill|Korea University (Japan)|ja|朝鮮大学校}} (in Japan), he was moved upon seeing a portrait of {{ill|Kim Il-sung|ja|金日成}} in the middle of the reception room, and remarked, "This is what current Japanese people lack. In the past, the 'Imperial Portrait' of the Emperor was enshrined in every school; we need to restore that situation."<ref>{{Cite book |author=Hideki Kajimura |title=Overcoming Exclusionism: A History of Korea |publisher=Heibonsha Library |year=2014 |page=25}}</ref>
== Family ==
[[File:Iwasaki Seishichi and his friends.jpg|thumb|Nara Tarō Ōmiwa (center of back row)]]
* Father: '''{{ill|Ashizu Kojiro|ja|葦津耕次郎}}''' (1878–1940) – Shintoist.<ref name=nishiya>{{Cite journal|author=Takafumi Nishiya |title=Ashizu Kojiro: The Trajectory of a Shinto Practitioner during the 'State Shinto' Period |volume=Kyoto University Doctoral Thesis |issue=No.14958 |year=2009 |naid=500000489926 |url=https://hdl.handle.net/2433/126522}}</ref> He served at {{ill|Hakozaki Shrine|ja|筥崎宮}}, devoted himself to Shinto service, and later became active as a businessman running mining and shrine construction businesses, while also engaging as a commentator expressing views on political and social issues from the perspective of Shinto and shrines, such as the {{ill|Korean Shrine Deity Controversy|ja|朝鮮神宮御祭神論争}}.<ref>[http://kaeizyuku.com/2020/01/14/visit-graves/ January 14, 2020 'Visit to the Ashizu Family Grave'] Kaei-juku</ref>
* Grandfather: '''Ashizu Isoo''' (–1903) – Chief priest of Hakozaki Shrine.<ref name=asia>Uraube Noboru, [http://asiarestoration.com/wp-content/uploads/2016/06/daiajia1st.pdf 'Daijia wo Tsuranuita Ōmiwa Asahei'], ''Dai Ajia'' inaugural issue, June 30, 2016, Dai Ajia Kenkyūkai</ref> For a time, he also concurrently served as chief priest of the {{ill|Kashii Shrine|ja|香椎宮}}.<ref>[https://dl.ndl.go.jp/info:ndljp/pid/2947481/9 Official Gazette. June 26, 1897]</ref>
* Great-uncle: '''{{ill|Chōbei Ōmiwa|ja|大三輪長兵衛}}''' (1835–1908) – Brother of his grandfather. After working at an oil shop and rice shop in Osaka, he opened a Matsumae merchant house,<ref>[https://dl.ndl.go.jp/info:ndljp/pid/778835/97 'Chōbei Ōmiwa', ''Meiji Fugo Chifujidai'', Sumitei Inshi (Daigakukan, 1902)]</ref> and later made a fortune in cotton trade, serving as president of the 58th National Bank, chairman of the Osaka Clearing House, Osaka Prefectural Assembly chairman, and Osaka City Council chairman, among other positions. In 1898, he became a member of the {{ill|House of Representatives|ja|衆議院}}.<ref name=sangyo/><ref>[https://www.ndl.go.jp/portrait/datas/36/ 'Chōbei Ōmiwa'], Portraits of Modern Japanese Historical Figures, National Diet Library</ref><ref>[https://kotobank.jp/word/大三輪長兵衛-1061443 'Chōbei Ōmiwa'], Kotobank</ref><ref>[http://www.pref.osaka.lg.jp/attach/3059/00006976/seifuku-itiran_20200522.pdf 'List of Former Chairmen and Vice-Chairmen of Osaka Prefectural Assembly'], Osaka Prefecture, 2020</ref>
* Son: '''{{ill|Ashizu Yasukuni|ja|葦津泰國}}''' (1937–2024) – Graduate of Tokyo Metropolitan University. Former president of Jinja Shinpo Sha. Author of ''The Life of Chōbei Ōmiwa'' (Ashizu Office, 2008), among others.
== Works ==
[Full list of his publications, including major works and collected editions.]
=== Collected Works ===
* ''Ashizu Ujihiko Selected Works'' – Edited by the same Editorial Committee<ref>Each with commentary by {{ill|Mitsuo Suzuki|ja|鈴木満男}} (a disciple)</ref>, Jinja Shinpo Sha, 1996 1. ''Emperor, Shinto, Constitution'' 2. ''As Successors of the Restoration'' 3. ''Current Affairs and Character Critiques''
* ''"Let's Read the Shōwa Era": Ashizu Ujihiko's Claims Series'' – Ashizu Office, 2005–2007 1. ''Japan's Monarchy: Studies on the Emperor System'' 2. ''Eternal Restorationist'' 3. ''The End of Modern Democracy: Revival of Japanese Thought'' 4. ''Words of a Native: The Logic of Trust and Loyalty'' 5. ''Greater Asianism and Toyama Mitsuru'' 6. ''Living the Shōwa Era: The Spirit and Thoughts of the People of the Divine Land''
== Footnotes ==
{{Reflist|30em}}
== References ==
* ''Meiji Restoration and the Liberation of the East'', "Preface (Kazuma Shindō)", Shin Seiryoku Sha, 1964 * ''Ashizu Ujihiko Memorial Record'', Konihon Sha, 1993 * ''Ashizu Ujihiko Selected Works Volume 3'', Jinja Shinpo Sha, 1996, commentary and chronology at the end * Yukio Hori, ''Postwar Right-Wing Forces'', "Chapter 3: Ideology of the Postwar Right Wing, Section 2: Thoughts of Ashizu Ujihiko—Ideology of the Association of Shinto Shrines", Keiso Shobō, 1993, Expanded Edition
== External Links ==
* [https://dl.ndl.go.jp/info:ndljp/pid/1271056 'Words of Japanese People: Enlightening the Nazi'], Ashizu Ujihiko (Kyōdai Kai, 1940) * [https://web.archive.org/web/20201125060116/https://www.chugainippoh.co.jp/info/ruikotu/ruikotu015-02-001.html 'On the Thought of Ashizu Ujihiko—Focusing on His Postwar Theories on the Emperor and Shinto'], Hiroyuki Imanishi, ''Chūgai Nippo'', 15th 'Ruikotsu Award' Winning Essay, Encouragement Prize * [http://ymatsuda.kill.jp/Ashizu-mokuroku.pdf 'List of Works by Ashizu Ujihiko']

[[ja:葦津珍彦]]

== Family ==
[[File:Iwasaki Seishichi and his friends.jpg|thumb|Nara Tarō Ōmiwa (center of back row)]]
* Father: '''{{ill|Ashizu Kojiro|ja|葦津耕次郎}}''' (1878–1940) – Shintoist.<ref name=nishiya>{{Cite journal|author=Takafumi Nishiya |title=Ashizu Kojiro: The Trajectory of a Shinto Practitioner during the 'State Shinto' Period |volume=Kyoto University Doctoral Thesis |issue=No.14958 |year=2009 |naid=500000489926 |url=https://hdl.handle.net/2433/126522}}</ref> He served at {{ill|Hakozaki Shrine|ja|筥崎宮}}, devoted himself to Shinto service, and later became active as a businessman running mining and shrine construction businesses, while also engaging as a commentator expressing views on political and social issues from the perspective of Shinto and shrines, such as the {{ill|Korean Shrine Deity Controversy|ja|朝鮮神宮御祭神論争}}.<ref>[http://kaeizyuku.com/2020/01/14/visit-graves/ January 14, 2020 'Visit to the Ashizu Family Grave'] Kaei-juku</ref>

* ''Meiji Restoration and the Liberation of the East'', "Preface (Kazuma Shindō)", Shin Seiryoku Sha, 1964 * ''Ashizu Ujihiko Memorial Record'', Konihon Sha, 1993 * ''Ashizu Ujihiko Selected Works Volume 3'', Jinja Shinpo Sha, 1996, commentary and chronology at the end * Yukio Hori, ''Postwar Right-Wing Forces'', "Chapter 3: Ideology of the Postwar Right Wing, Section 2: Thoughts of Ashizu Ujihiko—Ideology of the Association of Shinto Shrines", Keiso Shobō, 1993, Expanded Edition

[[ja:葦津珍彦]]

revision.comment: 1 revision imported: making drafts attribute to me
revision.text:
'''Ashizu Ujihiko''' (葦津 珍彦, July 17, 1909 – June 10, 1992) was a Japanese {{ill|Shinto|ja|神道}} scholar. He was also renowned as a commentator of the {{ill|Nationalist|ja|民族派}} movement.<ref>Yukio Hori, ''Postwar Right-Wing Forces'', Keiso Shobō, 1993, Expanded Edition.</ref>
== Career ==
He was born as the eldest son of {{ill|Ashizu Kojiro|ja|葦津耕次郎}} in Hakozaki, Fukuoka Prefecture (now Hakozaki, {{ill|Higashi-ku, Fukuoka|ja|福岡市東区}}). The Ashizu family is a descendant of the Ogami family, one of the shrine families of {{ill|Hakozaki Shrine|ja|筥崎宮}}, and his uncle, {{ill|Ashizu Seizō|ja|葦津洗造}}, was the chief priest of the same shrine.<ref name="#1">Foreword by {{ill|Ashizu Yasukuni|ja|葦津泰國}}, from ''Living the Shōwa Era: The Claims of Ujihiko Ashizu Series IV''</ref>
In 1922 (Taishō 11), he entered {{ill|Tokyo Metropolitan Koishikawa High School|ja|東京都立小石川高等学校}} (then Tokyo Prefectural Fifth Junior High School). Around this time, he yearned for socialism, participated in the Social Science Study Group, and avidly read related books.<ref name="#1"/>
Those around him were concerned, and after graduation, he went on to {{ill|Kokugakuin University|ja|國學院大學}}.{{Citation needed|date=July 5, 2017}} He also enrolled in the {{ill|Tokyo School of Foreign Languages|ja|東京外国語学校 (旧制)}}, but dropped out of both.{{Citation needed|date=July 5, 2017}} In 1928 (Shōwa 3), he entered {{ill|Fukushima University|ja|福島大学}} (then Fukushima Higher Commercial School), but dropped out the following year.
In 1931, at the recommendation of a friend who belonged to the {{ill|Japanese Communist Party|ja|日本共産党}}, he wrote a leading article for the newspaper {{ill|Akahata|ja|しんぶん赤旗}}.<ref name=ashidu>{{Cite journal|author=Konno Nobuyuki|title=The Thought of Ashizu Ujihiko during and after the War—Focusing on His View of Shinto|journal=Interdisciplinary Research on 'Narratives' in Japan from 1890 to the 1950s|page=63|year=2012}}</ref> However, in February of the following year, under the influence of his father, he is said to have converted to traditional conservative imperial {{ill|Shinto|ja|神道}}, which aimed for the realization of "Japan, the Emperor's Nation with Unity of Rites and Government," like his father.<ref name=ashidu />
In 1932 (Shōwa 7), he succeeded his father's business and engaged in the construction of shrines and temples as the representative of a shrine and temple construction office.<ref>"Fifty Years of the Association of Shinto Shrines" (Non-commercial publication), May 1996, Publisher: {{ill|Association of Shinto Shrines|ja|神社本庁}}, p.74—Obituary of Ashizu Ujihiko, associate of {{ill|Jinja Shinpo Sha|ja|神社新報社}} (June 10)</ref> During this period, he interacted with {{ill|Toyama Mitsuru|ja|頭山満}}, {{ill|Teisuke Imaizumi|ja|今泉定助}}, {{ill|Takayama Noboru|ja|高山昇}}, {{ill|Ogata Taketora|ja|緒方竹虎}}, {{ill|Ryōhei Uchida|ja|内田良平}}, {{ill|Inoue Tadamaro|ja|井上孚麿}}, and others. Before the outbreak of the {{ill|Pacific War|ja|太平洋戦争}}, he opposed the war, viewing it as an "unwinnable battle," and after the outbreak, he engaged in unique political activities, such as insisting on adhering only to the war objectives shown in the Imperial Rescript on the outbreak of war, which was "to secure the survival of an independent nation as unavoidable."{{Citation needed|date=July 5, 2017}}
During the war, some of his critical articles on {{ill|Nazi Germany|ja|ナチス・ドイツ}}, Japan's ally, and the policies of the {{ill|Hideki Tōjō|ja|東條英機}} Cabinet were banned, and he was arrested at times.{{Citation needed|date=July 5, 2017}}
Immediately after the war, he dissolved his company and assisted {{ill|Shigeru Yoshida (Home Ministry official)|ja|吉田茂 (内務官僚)}}, {{ill|Munenori Miyagawa|ja|宮川宗徳}} (later Secretary-General of the main office), and {{ill|Tokugawa Munetake|ja|徳川宗敬}} (later Chief of the main office), dedicating himself to the establishment of the {{ill|Association of Shinto Shrines|ja|神社本庁}}. Furthermore, when the '{{ill|Jinja Shinpo|ja|神社新報}}' was launched as part of the main office's educational public relations, he took charge of the management of {{ill|Jinja Shinpo Sha|ja|神社新報社}}, the publishing body, along with being the chief editor.
Even after his retirement in 1968 (Shōwa 43), he continued his writing activities and the guidance of future generations, being at the forefront of movements to protect the {{ill|Kokutai|ja|国体}} and {{ill|Shinto shrines|ja|神社}}. He continued to have a significant influence on the Shinto world (through the {{ill|National Council to Defend Japan|ja|日本を守る国民会議}}, now {{ill|Nippon Kaigi|ja|日本会議}}) and {{ill|Nationalist student movements|ja|民族派学生組織}} ({{ill|Japan Youth Council|ja|日本青年協議会}}), among others.
On the other hand, he questioned the enshrinement of {{ill|Class A war criminals|ja|A級戦犯}} like Tōjō, whom he had criticized during the war, due to their responsibility for the defeat.<ref>"Yasukuni: Where to Go from 'Postwar' 3—Article by Eminent Scholar of Shinto History Raises Doubts about 'Class A Enshrinement'," ''Mainichi Shimbun'' Tokyo edition morning edition: August 8, 2006</ref> (Note: {{ill|Yasukuni Shrine|ja|靖国神社}} is not a {{ill|Bekkaku Kanpeisha|ja|別表神社}} of the Association of Shinto Shrines.)
In 1992 (Heisei 4), he passed away at his home in {{ill|Kamakura|ja|鎌倉市}} at the age of 82.
{{ill|Bunzō Hashikawa|ja|橋川文三}}, who had experiences of debates with Ashizu, praised him as "an advanced polemicist among conservatives," noting that his theory of the Emperor system "should be noted as surpassing the level of traditional right-wingers." He pointed out that Ashizu tried to grasp the problem of the Emperor system from a comparative institutional perspective, based on research in European political thought history, pursuing an open form of "Kokutai theory" as his characteristic.<ref>From ''Postwar Japanese Thought Series 7: Conservative Thought,'' edited by Bunzō Hashikawa, Chikuma Shobō, 1968</ref>
{{ill|Yoshio Ōishi|ja|大石義雄}}, in ''Twenty Years of the Constitution'', praised Ashizu's ''Words of a Native'' and described Ashizu as "a modern-day Kitabatake Chikafusa."
After the war, he had friendships that transcended ideological positions with {{ill|Saburō Ichi'i|ja|市井三郎}} and {{ill|Shunsuke Tsurumi|ja|鶴見俊輔}}, who were editors of ''{{ill|Shisō no Kagaku|ja|思想の科学}}'', and the critic {{ill|Yoshihiko Takeuchi|ja|竹内好}}, and he often contributed to the same magazine.
He was also well-versed in Korean culture and had interactions with {{ill|Lyuh Woon-hyung|ja|呂運亨}}, an independence activist in Korea during the Japanese occupation. It is said that when Ashizu visited the {{ill|Korea University (Japan)|ja|朝鮮大学校}} (in Japan), he was moved upon seeing a portrait of {{ill|Kim Il-sung|ja|金日成}} in the middle of the reception room, and remarked, "This is what current Japanese people lack. In the past, the 'Imperial Portrait' of the Emperor was enshrined in every school; we need to restore that situation."<ref>{{Cite book |author=Hideki Kajimura |title=Overcoming Exclusionism: A History of Korea |publisher=Heibonsha Library |year=2014 |page=25}}</ref>
== Family ==
[[File:Iwasaki Seishichi and his friends.jpg|thumb|Nara Tarō Ōmiwa (center of back row)]]
* Father: '''{{ill|Ashizu Kojiro|ja|葦津耕次郎}}''' (1878–1940) – Shintoist.<ref name=nishiya>{{Cite journal|author=Takafumi Nishiya |title=Ashizu Kojiro: The Trajectory of a Shinto Practitioner during the 'State Shinto' Period |volume=Kyoto University Doctoral Thesis |issue=No.14958 |year=2009 |naid=500000489926 |url=https://hdl.handle.net/2433/126522}}</ref> He served at {{ill|Hakozaki Shrine|ja|筥崎宮}}, devoted himself to Shinto service, and later became active as a businessman running mining and shrine construction businesses, while also engaging as a commentator expressing views on political and social issues from the perspective of Shinto and shrines, such as the {{ill|Korean Shrine Deity Controversy|ja|朝鮮神宮御祭神論争}}.<ref>[http://kaeizyuku.com/2020/01/14/visit-graves/ January 14, 2020 'Visit to the Ashizu Family Grave'] Kaei-juku</ref>
* Grandfather: '''Ashizu Isoo''' (–1903) – Chief priest of Hakozaki Shrine.<ref name=asia>Uraube Noboru, [http://asiarestoration.com/wp-content/uploads/2016/06/daiajia1st.pdf 'Daijia wo Tsuranuita Ōmiwa Asahei'], ''Dai Ajia'' inaugural issue, June 30, 2016, Dai Ajia Kenkyūkai</ref> For a time, he also concurrently served as chief priest of the {{ill|Kashii Shrine|ja|香椎宮}}.<ref>[https://dl.ndl.go.jp/info:ndljp/pid/2947481/9 Official Gazette. June 26, 1897]</ref>
* Great-uncle: '''{{ill|Chōbei Ōmiwa|ja|大三輪長兵衛}}''' (1835–1908) – Brother of his grandfather. After working at an oil shop and rice shop in Osaka, he opened a Matsumae merchant house,<ref>[https://dl.ndl.go.jp/info:ndljp/pid/778835/97 'Chōbei Ōmiwa', ''Meiji Fugo Chifujidai'', Sumitei Inshi (Daigakukan, 1902)]</ref> and later made a fortune in cotton trade, serving as president of the 58th National Bank, chairman of the Osaka Clearing House, Osaka Prefectural Assembly chairman, and Osaka City Council chairman, among other positions. In 1898, he became a member of the {{ill|House of Representatives|ja|衆議院}}.<ref name=sangyo/><ref>[https://www.ndl.go.jp/portrait/datas/36/ 'Chōbei Ōmiwa'], Portraits of Modern Japanese Historical Figures, National Diet Library</ref><ref>[https://kotobank.jp/word/大三輪長兵衛-1061443 'Chōbei Ōmiwa'], Kotobank</ref><ref>[http://www.pref.osaka.lg.jp/attach/3059/00006976/seifuku-itiran_20200522.pdf 'List of Former Chairmen and Vice-Chairmen of Osaka Prefectural Assembly'], Osaka Prefecture, 2020</ref>
* Son: '''{{ill|Ashizu Yasukuni|ja|葦津泰國}}''' (1937–2024) – Graduate of Tokyo Metropolitan University. Former president of Jinja Shinpo Sha. Author of ''The Life of Chōbei Ōmiwa'' (Ashizu Office, 2008), among others.
== Works ==
[Full list of his publications, including major works and collected editions.]
=== Collected Works ===
* ''Ashizu Ujihiko Selected Works'' – Edited by the same Editorial Committee<ref>Each with commentary by {{ill|Mitsuo Suzuki|ja|鈴木満男}} (a disciple)</ref>, Jinja Shinpo Sha, 1996 1. ''Emperor, Shinto, Constitution'' 2. ''As Successors of the Restoration'' 3. ''Current Affairs and Character Critiques''
* ''"Let's Read the Shōwa Era": Ashizu Ujihiko's Claims Series'' – Ashizu Office, 2005–2007 1. ''Japan's Monarchy: Studies on the Emperor System'' 2. ''Eternal Restorationist'' 3. ''The End of Modern Democracy: Revival of Japanese Thought'' 4. ''Words of a Native: The Logic of Trust and Loyalty'' 5. ''Greater Asianism and Toyama Mitsuru'' 6. ''Living the Shōwa Era: The Spirit and Thoughts of the People of the Divine Land''
== Footnotes ==
{{Reflist|30em}}
== References ==
* ''Meiji Restoration and the Liberation of the East'', "Preface (Kazuma Shindō)", Shin Seiryoku Sha, 1964 * ''Ashizu Ujihiko Memorial Record'', Konihon Sha, 1993 * ''Ashizu Ujihiko Selected Works Volume 3'', Jinja Shinpo Sha, 1996, commentary and chronology at the end * Yukio Hori, ''Postwar Right-Wing Forces'', "Chapter 3: Ideology of the Postwar Right Wing, Section 2: Thoughts of Ashizu Ujihiko—Ideology of the Association of Shinto Shrines", Keiso Shobō, 1993, Expanded Edition
== External Links ==
* [https://dl.ndl.go.jp/info:ndljp/pid/1271056 'Words of Japanese People: Enlightening the Nazi'], Ashizu Ujihiko (Kyōdai Kai, 1940) * [https://web.archive.org/web/20201125060116/https://www.chugainippoh.co.jp/info/ruikotu/ruikotu015-02-001.html 'On the Thought of Ashizu Ujihiko—Focusing on His Postwar Theories on the Emperor and Shinto'], Hiroyuki Imanishi, ''Chūgai Nippo'', 15th 'Ruikotsu Award' Winning Essay, Encouragement Prize * [http://ymatsuda.kill.jp/Ashizu-mokuroku.pdf 'List of Works by Ashizu Ujihiko']
[[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[category:unsorted completion categories]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Uzuhiko Ashizu}}[[category:kk]] [[Category:Shintoists]] [[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[category:unsorted completion categories]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Uzuhiko Ashizu}}[[category:kk]] [[Category:20th-century Japanese critics]] [[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[category:unsorted completion categories]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Uzuhiko Ashizu}}[[category:kk]] [[Category:Japanese nationalists]] [[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[category:unsorted completion categories]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Uzuhiko Ashizu}}[[category:kk]] [[Category:Japanese conservative thinkers]] [[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[category:unsorted completion categories]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Uzuhiko Ashizu}}[[category:kk]] [[Category:People associated with Tokyo Metropolitan Koishikawa High School]] [[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[category:unsorted completion categories]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Uzuhiko Ashizu}}[[category:kk]] [[Category:People from Fukuoka City]] [[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[category:unsorted completion categories]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Uzuhiko Ashizu}}[[category:kk]] [[Category:1909 births]] [[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[category:unsorted completion categories]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Uzuhiko Ashizu}}[[category:kk]] [[Category:1992 deaths]] [[Category:kk]][[category:kk]] [[Category:mass bump]]
[[ja:葦津珍彦]]

== Family ==
[[File:Iwasaki Seishichi and his friends.jpg|thumb|Nara Tarō Ōmiwa (center of back row)]]
* Father: '''{{ill|Ashizu Kojiro|ja|葦津耕次郎}}''' (1878–1940) – Shintoist.<ref name=nishiya>{{Cite journal|author=Takafumi Nishiya |title=Ashizu Kojiro: The Trajectory of a Shinto Practitioner during the 'State Shinto' Period |volume=Kyoto University Doctoral Thesis |issue=No.14958 |year=2009 |naid=500000489926 |url=https://hdl.handle.net/2433/126522}}</ref> He served at {{ill|Hakozaki Shrine|ja|筥崎宮}}, devoted himself to Shinto service, and later became active as a businessman running mining and shrine construction businesses, while also engaging as a commentator expressing views on political and social issues from the perspective of Shinto and shrines, such as the {{ill|Korean Shrine Deity Controversy|ja|朝鮮神宮御祭神論争}}.<ref>[http://kaeizyuku.com/2020/01/14/visit-graves/ January 14, 2020 'Visit to the Ashizu Family Grave'] Kaei-juku</ref>

June 26, 1897]</ref>
* Great-uncle: '''{{ill|Chōbei Ōmiwa|ja|大三輪長兵衛}}''' (1835–1908) – Brother of his grandfather. After working at an oil shop and rice shop in Osaka, he opened a Matsumae merchant house,<ref>[https://dl.ndl.go.jp/info:ndljp/pid/778835/97 'Chōbei Ōmiwa', ''Meiji Fugo Chifujidai'', Sumitei Inshi (Daigakukan, 1902)]</ref> and later made a fortune in cotton trade, serving as president of the 58th National Bank, chairman of the Osaka Clearing House, Osaka Prefectural Assembly chairman, and Osaka City Council chairman, among other positions. In 1898, he became a member of the {{ill|House of Representatives|ja|衆議院}}.<ref name=sangyo/><ref>[https://www.ndl.go.jp/portrait/datas/36/ 'Chōbei Ōmiwa'], Portraits of Modern Japanese Historical Figures, National Diet Library</ref><ref>[https://kotobank.jp/word/大三輪長兵衛-1061443 'Chōbei Ōmiwa'], Kotobank</ref><ref>[http://www.pref.osaka.lg.jp/attach/3059/00006976/seifuku-itiran_20200522.pdf 'List of Former Chairmen and Vice-Chairmen of Osaka Prefectural Assembly'], Osaka Prefecture, 2020</ref>
* Son: '''{{ill|Ashizu Yasukuni|ja|葦津泰國}}''' (1937–2024) – Graduate of Tokyo Metropolitan University. Former president of Jinja Shinpo Sha. Author of ''The Life of Chōbei Ōmiwa'' (Ashizu Office, 2008), among others.
== Works ==
[Full list of his publications, including major works and collected editions.]
=== Collected Works ===
* ''Ashizu Ujihiko Selected Works'' – Edited by the same Editorial Committee<ref>Each with commentary by {{ill|Mitsuo Suzuki|ja|鈴木満男}} (a disciple)</ref>, Jinja Shinpo Sha, 1996 1. ''Emperor, Shinto, Constitution'' 2. ''As Successors of the Restoration'' 3. ''Current Affairs and Character Critiques''
* ''"Let's Read the Shōwa Era": Ashizu Ujihiko's Claims Series'' – Ashizu Office, 2005–2007 1. ''Japan's Monarchy: Studies on the Emperor System'' 2. ''Eternal Restorationist'' 3. ''The End of Modern Democracy: Revival of Japanese Thought'' 4. ''Words of a Native: The Logic of Trust and Loyalty'' 5. ''Greater Asianism and Toyama Mitsuru'' 6. ''Living the Shōwa Era: The Spirit and Thoughts of the People of the Divine Land''
== Footnotes ==
{{Reflist|30em}}
== References ==
* ''Meiji Restoration and the Liberation of the East'', "Preface (Kazuma Shindō)", Shin Seiryoku Sha, 1964 * ''Ashizu Ujihiko Memorial Record'', Konihon Sha, 1993 * ''Ashizu Ujihiko Selected Works Volume 3'', Jinja Shinpo Sha, 1996, commentary and chronology at the end * Yukio Hori, ''Postwar Right-Wing Forces'', "Chapter 3: Ideology of the Postwar Right Wing, Section 2: Thoughts of Ashizu Ujihiko—Ideology of the Association of Shinto Shrines", Keiso Shobō, 1993, Expanded Edition
== External Links ==
* [https://dl.ndl.go.jp/info:ndljp/pid/1271056 'Words of Japanese People: Enlightening the Nazi'], Ashizu Ujihiko (Kyōdai Kai, 1940) * [https://web.archive.org/web/20201125060116/https://www.chugainippoh.co.jp/info/ruikotu/ruikotu015-02-001.html 'On the Thought of Ashizu Ujihiko—Focusing on His Postwar Theories on the Emperor and Shinto'], Hiroyuki Imanishi, ''Chūgai Nippo'', 15th 'Ruikotsu Award' Winning Essay, Encouragement Prize * [http://ymatsuda.kill.jp/Ashizu-mokuroku.pdf 'List of Works by Ashizu Ujihiko']

revision.comment: 1 revision imported: import for reasons specified in the description
revision.text:
'''Ashizu Ujihiko''' (葦津 珍彦, July 17, 1909 – June 10, 1992) was a Japanese {{ill|Shinto|ja|神道}} scholar. He was also renowned as a commentator of the {{ill|Nationalist|ja|民族派}} movement.<ref>Yukio Hori, ''Postwar Right-Wing Forces'', Keiso Shobō, 1993, Expanded Edition.</ref>
== Career ==
He was born as the eldest son of {{ill|Ashizu Kojiro|ja|葦津耕次郎}} in Hakozaki, Fukuoka Prefecture (now Hakozaki, {{ill|Higashi-ku, Fukuoka|ja|福岡市東区}}). The Ashizu family is a descendant of the Ogami family, one of the shrine families of {{ill|Hakozaki Shrine|ja|筥崎宮}}, and his uncle, {{ill|Ashizu Seizō|ja|葦津洗造}}, was the chief priest of the same shrine.<ref name="#1">Foreword by {{ill|Ashizu Yasukuni|ja|葦津泰國}}, from ''Living the Shōwa Era: The Claims of Ujihiko Ashizu Series IV''</ref>
In 1922 (Taishō 11), he entered {{ill|Tokyo Metropolitan Koishikawa High School|ja|東京都立小石川高等学校}} (then Tokyo Prefectural Fifth Junior High School). Around this time, he yearned for socialism, participated in the Social Science Study Group, and avidly read related books.<ref name="#1"/>
Those around him were concerned, and after graduation, he went on to {{ill|Kokugakuin University|ja|國學院大學}}.{{Citation needed|date=July 5, 2017}} He also enrolled in the {{ill|Tokyo School of Foreign Languages|ja|東京外国語学校 (旧制)}}, but dropped out of both.{{Citation needed|date=July 5, 2017}} In 1928 (Shōwa 3), he entered {{ill|Fukushima University|ja|福島大学}} (then Fukushima Higher Commercial School), but dropped out the following year.
In 1931, at the recommendation of a friend who belonged to the {{ill|Japanese Communist Party|ja|日本共産党}}, he wrote a leading article for the newspaper {{ill|Akahata|ja|しんぶん赤旗}}.<ref name=ashidu>{{Cite journal|author=Konno Nobuyuki|title=The Thought of Ashizu Ujihiko during and after the War—Focusing on His View of Shinto|journal=Interdisciplinary Research on 'Narratives' in Japan from 1890 to the 1950s|page=63|year=2012}}</ref> However, in February of the following year, under the influence of his father, he is said to have converted to traditional conservative imperial {{ill|Shinto|ja|神道}}, which aimed for the realization of "Japan, the Emperor's Nation with Unity of Rites and Government," like his father.<ref name=ashidu />
In 1932 (Shōwa 7), he succeeded his father's business and engaged in the construction of shrines and temples as the representative of a shrine and temple construction office.<ref>"Fifty Years of the Association of Shinto Shrines" (Non-commercial publication), May 1996, Publisher: {{ill|Association of Shinto Shrines|ja|神社本庁}}, p.74—Obituary of Ashizu Ujihiko, associate of {{ill|Jinja Shinpo Sha|ja|神社新報社}} (June 10)</ref> During this period, he interacted with {{ill|Toyama Mitsuru|ja|頭山満}}, {{ill|Teisuke Imaizumi|ja|今泉定助}}, {{ill|Takayama Noboru|ja|高山昇}}, {{ill|Ogata Taketora|ja|緒方竹虎}}, {{ill|Ryōhei Uchida|ja|内田良平}}, {{ill|Inoue Tadamaro|ja|井上孚麿}}, and others. Before the outbreak of the {{ill|Pacific War|ja|太平洋戦争}}, he opposed the war, viewing it as an "unwinnable battle," and after the outbreak, he engaged in unique political activities, such as insisting on adhering only to the war objectives shown in the Imperial Rescript on the outbreak of war, which was "to secure the survival of an independent nation as unavoidable."{{Citation needed|date=July 5, 2017}}
During the war, some of his critical articles on {{ill|Nazi Germany|ja|ナチス・ドイツ}}, Japan's ally, and the policies of the {{ill|Hideki Tōjō|ja|東條英機}} Cabinet were banned, and he was arrested at times.{{Citation needed|date=July 5, 2017}}
Immediately after the war, he dissolved his company and assisted {{ill|Shigeru Yoshida (Home Ministry official)|ja|吉田茂 (内務官僚)}}, {{ill|Munenori Miyagawa|ja|宮川宗徳}} (later Secretary-General of the main office), and {{ill|Tokugawa Munetake|ja|徳川宗敬}} (later Chief of the main office), dedicating himself to the establishment of the {{ill|Association of Shinto Shrines|ja|神社本庁}}. Furthermore, when the '{{ill|Jinja Shinpo|ja|神社新報}}' was launched as part of the main office's educational public relations, he took charge of the management of {{ill|Jinja Shinpo Sha|ja|神社新報社}}, the publishing body, along with being the chief editor.
Even after his retirement in 1968 (Shōwa 43), he continued his writing activities and the guidance of future generations, being at the forefront of movements to protect the {{ill|Kokutai|ja|国体}} and {{ill|Shinto shrines|ja|神社}}. He continued to have a significant influence on the Shinto world (through the {{ill|National Council to Defend Japan|ja|日本を守る国民会議}}, now {{ill|Nippon Kaigi|ja|日本会議}}) and {{ill|Nationalist student movements|ja|民族派学生組織}} ({{ill|Japan Youth Council|ja|日本青年協議会}}), among others.
On the other hand, he questioned the enshrinement of {{ill|Class A war criminals|ja|A級戦犯}} like Tōjō, whom he had criticized during the war, due to their responsibility for the defeat.<ref>"Yasukuni: Where to Go from 'Postwar' 3—Article by Eminent Scholar of Shinto History Raises Doubts about 'Class A Enshrinement'," ''Mainichi Shimbun'' Tokyo edition morning edition: August 8, 2006</ref> (Note: {{ill|Yasukuni Shrine|ja|靖国神社}} is not a {{ill|Bekkaku Kanpeisha|ja|別表神社}} of the Association of Shinto Shrines.)
In 1992 (Heisei 4), he passed away at his home in {{ill|Kamakura|ja|鎌倉市}} at the age of 82.
{{ill|Bunzō Hashikawa|ja|橋川文三}}, who had experiences of debates with Ashizu, praised him as "an advanced polemicist among conservatives," noting that his theory of the Emperor system "should be noted as surpassing the level of traditional right-wingers." He pointed out that Ashizu tried to grasp the problem of the Emperor system from a comparative institutional perspective, based on research in European political thought history, pursuing an open form of "Kokutai theory" as his characteristic.<ref>From ''Postwar Japanese Thought Series 7: Conservative Thought,'' edited by Bunzō Hashikawa, Chikuma Shobō, 1968</ref>
{{ill|Yoshio Ōishi|ja|大石義雄}}, in ''Twenty Years of the Constitution'', praised Ashizu's ''Words of a Native'' and described Ashizu as "a modern-day Kitabatake Chikafusa."
After the war, he had friendships that transcended ideological positions with {{ill|Saburō Ichi'i|ja|市井三郎}} and {{ill|Shunsuke Tsurumi|ja|鶴見俊輔}}, who were editors of ''{{ill|Shisō no Kagaku|ja|思想の科学}}'', and the critic {{ill|Yoshihiko Takeuchi|ja|竹内好}}, and he often contributed to the same magazine.
He was also well-versed in Korean culture and had interactions with {{ill|Lyuh Woon-hyung|ja|呂運亨}}, an independence activist in Korea during the Japanese occupation. It is said that when Ashizu visited the {{ill|Korea University (Japan)|ja|朝鮮大学校}} (in Japan), he was moved upon seeing a portrait of {{ill|Kim Il-sung|ja|金日成}} in the middle of the reception room, and remarked, "This is what current Japanese people lack. In the past, the 'Imperial Portrait' of the Emperor was enshrined in every school; we need to restore that situation."<ref>{{Cite book |author=Hideki Kajimura |title=Overcoming Exclusionism: A History of Korea |publisher=Heibonsha Library |year=2014 |page=25}}</ref>
== Family ==
[[File:Iwasaki Seishichi and his friends.jpg|thumb|Nara Tarō Ōmiwa (center of back row)]]
* Father: '''{{ill|Ashizu Kojiro|ja|葦津耕次郎}}''' (1878–1940) – Shintoist.<ref name=nishiya>{{Cite journal|author=Takafumi Nishiya |title=Ashizu Kojiro: The Trajectory of a Shinto Practitioner during the 'State Shinto' Period |volume=Kyoto University Doctoral Thesis |issue=No.14958 |year=2009 |naid=500000489926 |url=https://hdl.handle.net/2433/126522}}</ref> He served at {{ill|Hakozaki Shrine|ja|筥崎宮}}, devoted himself to Shinto service, and later became active as a businessman running mining and shrine construction businesses, while also engaging as a commentator expressing views on political and social issues from the perspective of Shinto and shrines, such as the {{ill|Korean Shrine Deity Controversy|ja|朝鮮神宮御祭神論争}}.<ref>[http://kaeizyuku.com/2020/01/14/visit-graves/ January 14, 2020 'Visit to the Ashizu Family Grave'] Kaei-juku</ref>
* Grandfather: '''Ashizu Isoo''' (–1903) – Chief priest of Hakozaki Shrine.<ref name=asia>Uraube Noboru, [http://asiarestoration.com/wp-content/uploads/2016/06/daiajia1st.pdf 'Daijia wo Tsuranuita Ōmiwa Asahei'], ''Dai Ajia'' inaugural issue, June 30, 2016, Dai Ajia Kenkyūkai</ref> For a time, he also concurrently served as chief priest of the {{ill|Kashii Shrine|ja|香椎宮}}.<ref>[https://dl.ndl.go.jp/info:ndljp/pid/2947481/9 Official Gazette. June 26, 1897]</ref>
* Great-uncle: '''{{ill|Chōbei Ōmiwa|ja|大三輪長兵衛}}''' (1835–1908) – Brother of his grandfather. After working at an oil shop and rice shop in Osaka, he opened a Matsumae merchant house,<ref>[https://dl.ndl.go.jp/info:ndljp/pid/778835/97 'Chōbei Ōmiwa', ''Meiji Fugo Chifujidai'', Sumitei Inshi (Daigakukan, 1902)]</ref> and later made a fortune in cotton trade, serving as president of the 58th National Bank, chairman of the Osaka Clearing House, Osaka Prefectural Assembly chairman, and Osaka City Council chairman, among other positions. In 1898, he became a member of the {{ill|House of Representatives|ja|衆議院}}.<ref name=sangyo/><ref>[https://www.ndl.go.jp/portrait/datas/36/ 'Chōbei Ōmiwa'], Portraits of Modern Japanese Historical Figures, National Diet Library</ref><ref>[https://kotobank.jp/word/大三輪長兵衛-1061443 'Chōbei Ōmiwa'], Kotobank</ref><ref>[http://www.pref.osaka.lg.jp/attach/3059/00006976/seifuku-itiran_20200522.pdf 'List of Former Chairmen and Vice-Chairmen of Osaka Prefectural Assembly'], Osaka Prefecture, 2020</ref>
* Son: '''{{ill|Ashizu Yasukuni|ja|葦津泰國}}''' (1937–2024) – Graduate of Tokyo Metropolitan University. Former president of Jinja Shinpo Sha. Author of ''The Life of Chōbei Ōmiwa'' (Ashizu Office, 2008), among others.
== Works ==
[Full list of his publications, including major works and collected editions.]
=== Collected Works ===
* ''Ashizu Ujihiko Selected Works'' – Edited by the same Editorial Committee<ref>Each with commentary by {{ill|Mitsuo Suzuki|ja|鈴木満男}} (a disciple)</ref>, Jinja Shinpo Sha, 1996 1. ''Emperor, Shinto, Constitution'' 2. ''As Successors of the Restoration'' 3. ''Current Affairs and Character Critiques''
* ''"Let's Read the Shōwa Era": Ashizu Ujihiko's Claims Series'' – Ashizu Office, 2005–2007 1. ''Japan's Monarchy: Studies on the Emperor System'' 2. ''Eternal Restorationist'' 3. ''The End of Modern Democracy: Revival of Japanese Thought'' 4. ''Words of a Native: The Logic of Trust and Loyalty'' 5. ''Greater Asianism and Toyama Mitsuru'' 6. ''Living the Shōwa Era: The Spirit and Thoughts of the People of the Divine Land''
== Footnotes ==
{{Reflist|30em}}
== References ==
* ''Meiji Restoration and the Liberation of the East'', "Preface (Kazuma Shindō)", Shin Seiryoku Sha, 1964 * ''Ashizu Ujihiko Memorial Record'', Konihon Sha, 1993 * ''Ashizu Ujihiko Selected Works Volume 3'', Jinja Shinpo Sha, 1996, commentary and chronology at the end * Yukio Hori, ''Postwar Right-Wing Forces'', "Chapter 3: Ideology of the Postwar Right Wing, Section 2: Thoughts of Ashizu Ujihiko—Ideology of the Association of Shinto Shrines", Keiso Shobō, 1993, Expanded Edition
== External Links ==
* [https://dl.ndl.go.jp/info:ndljp/pid/1271056 'Words of Japanese People: Enlightening the Nazi'], Ashizu Ujihiko (Kyōdai Kai, 1940) * [https://web.archive.org/web/20201125060116/https://www.chugainippoh.co.jp/info/ruikotu/ruikotu015-02-001.html 'On the Thought of Ashizu Ujihiko—Focusing on His Postwar Theories on the Emperor and Shinto'], Hiroyuki Imanishi, ''Chūgai Nippo'', 15th 'Ruikotsu Award' Winning Essay, Encouragement Prize * [http://ymatsuda.kill.jp/Ashizu-mokuroku.pdf 'List of Works by Ashizu Ujihiko']

In the past, the 'Imperial Portrait' of the Emperor was enshrined in every school; we need to restore that situation."<ref>{{Cite book |author=Hideki Kajimura |title=Overcoming Exclusionism: A History of Korea |publisher=Heibonsha Library |year=2014 |page=25}}</ref>
== Family ==
[[File:Iwasaki Seishichi and his friends.jpg|thumb|Nara Tarō Ōmiwa (center of back row)]]
* Father: '''{{ill|Ashizu Kojiro|ja|葦津耕次郎}}''' (1878–1940) – Shintoist.<ref name=nishiya>{{Cite journal|author=Takafumi Nishiya |title=Ashizu Kojiro: The Trajectory of a Shinto Practitioner during the 'State Shinto' Period |volume=Kyoto University Doctoral Thesis |issue=No.14958 |year=2009 |naid=500000489926 |url=https://hdl.handle.net/2433/126522}}</ref> He served at {{ill|Hakozaki Shrine|ja|筥崎宮}}, devoted himself to Shinto service, and later became active as a businessman running mining and shrine construction businesses, while also engaging as a commentator expressing views on political and social issues from the perspective of Shinto and shrines, such as the {{ill|Korean Shrine Deity Controversy|ja|朝鮮神宮御祭神論争}}.<ref>[http://kaeizyuku.com/2020/01/14/visit-graves/ January 14, 2020 'Visit to the Ashizu Family Grave'] Kaei-juku</ref>
* Grandfather: '''Ashizu Isoo''' (–1903) – Chief priest of Hakozaki Shrine.<ref name=asia>Uraube Noboru, [http://asiarestoration.com/wp-content/uploads/2016/06/daiajia1st.pdf 'Daijia wo Tsuranuita Ōmiwa Asahei'], ''Dai Ajia'' inaugural issue, June 30, 2016, Dai Ajia Kenkyūkai</ref> For a time, he also concurrently served as chief priest of the {{ill|Kashii Shrine|ja|香椎宮}}.<ref>[https://dl.ndl.go.jp/info:ndljp/pid/2947481/9 Official Gazette. June 26, 1897]</ref>
* Great-uncle: '''{{ill|Chōbei Ōmiwa|ja|大三輪長兵衛}}''' (1835–1908) – Brother of his grandfather. After working at an oil shop and rice shop in Osaka, he opened a Matsumae merchant house,<ref>[https://dl.ndl.go.jp/info:ndljp/pid/778835/97 'Chōbei Ōmiwa', ''Meiji Fugo Chifujidai'', Sumitei Inshi (Daigakukan, 1902)]</ref> and later made a fortune in cotton trade, serving as president of the 58th National Bank, chairman of the Osaka Clearing House, Osaka Prefectural Assembly chairman, and Osaka City Council chairman, among other positions. In 1898, he became a member of the {{ill|House of Representatives|ja|衆議院}}.<ref name=sangyo/><ref>[https://www.ndl.go.jp/portrait/datas/36/ 'Chōbei Ōmiwa'], Portraits of Modern Japanese Historical Figures, National Diet Library</ref><ref>[https://kotobank.jp/word/大三輪長兵衛-1061443 'Chōbei Ōmiwa'], Kotobank</ref><ref>[http://www.pref.osaka.lg.jp/attach/3059/00006976/seifuku-itiran_20200522.pdf 'List of Former Chairmen and Vice-Chairmen of Osaka Prefectural Assembly'], Osaka Prefecture, 2020</ref>
* Son: '''{{ill|Ashizu Yasukuni|ja|葦津泰國}}''' (1937–2024) – Graduate of Tokyo Metropolitan University. Former president of Jinja Shinpo Sha. Author of ''The Life of Chōbei Ōmiwa'' (Ashizu Office, 2008), among others.
== Works ==
[Full list of his publications, including major works and collected editions.]
=== Collected Works ===
* ''Ashizu Ujihiko Selected Works'' – Edited by the same Editorial Committee<ref>Each with commentary by {{ill|Mitsuo Suzuki|ja|鈴木満男}} (a disciple)</ref>, Jinja Shinpo Sha, 1996 1. ''Emperor, Shinto, Constitution'' 2. ''As Successors of the Restoration'' 3. ''Current Affairs and Character Critiques''
* ''"Let's Read the Shōwa Era": Ashizu Ujihiko's Claims Series'' – Ashizu Office, 2005–2007 1. ''Japan's Monarchy: Studies on the Emperor System'' 2. ''Eternal Restorationist'' 3. ''The End of Modern Democracy: Revival of Japanese Thought'' 4. ''Words of a Native: The Logic of Trust and Loyalty'' 5. ''Greater Asianism and Toyama Mitsuru'' 6. ''Living the Shōwa Era: The Spirit and Thoughts of the People of the Divine Land''
== Footnotes ==

== References ==
