Before the outbreak of the {{ill|Pacific War|ja|太平洋戦争|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, he opposed the war, viewing it as an "unwinnable battle," and after the outbreak, he engaged in unique political activities, such as insisting on adhering only to the war objectives shown in the Imperial Rescript on the outbreak of war, which was "to secure the survival of an independent nation as unavoidable."{{Citation needed|date=July 5, 2017}}
During the war, some of his critical articles on {{ill|Nazi Germany|ja|ナチス・ドイツ|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, Japan's ally, and the policies of the {{ill|Hideki Tōjō|ja|東條英機|qq=draft|qq=draft}} Cabinet were banned, and he was arrested at times.{{Citation needed|date=July 5, 2017}}
Immediately after the war, he dissolved his company and assisted {{ill|Shigeru Yoshida (Home Ministry official)|ja|吉田茂 (内務官僚)|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Munenori Miyagawa|ja|宮川宗徳|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (later Secretary-General of the main office), and {{ill|Tokugawa Munetake|ja|徳川宗敬|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (later Chief of the main office), dedicating himself to the establishment of the {{ill|Association of Shinto Shrines|ja|神社本庁|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. Furthermore, when the '{{ill|Jinja Shinpo|ja|神社新報|qq=draft|qq=draft}}' was launched as part of the main office's educational public relations, he took charge of the management of {{ill|Jinja Shinpo Sha|ja|神社新報社|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, the publishing body, along with being the chief editor.
Even after his retirement in 1968 (Shōwa 43), he continued his writing activities and the guidance of future generations, being at the forefront of movements to protect the {{ill|Kokutai|ja|国体|qq=draft|qq=draft}} and {{ill|Shinto shrines|ja|神社|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. He continued to have a significant influence on the Shinto world (through the {{ill|National Council to Defend Japan|ja|日本を守る国民会議|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, now {{ill|Nippon Kaigi|ja|日本会議|qq=draft|qq=draft}}) and {{ill|Nationalist student movements|ja|民族派学生組織|qq=draft|qq=draft}} ({{ill|Japan Youth Council|ja|日本青年協議会|qq=draft|qq=draft}}), among others.
On the other hand, he questioned the enshrinement of {{ill|Class A war criminals|ja|A級戦犯|qq=draft|qq=draft}} like Tōjō, whom he had criticized during the war, due to their responsibility for the defeat.<ref>"Yasukuni: Where to Go from 'Postwar' 3—Article by Eminent Scholar of Shinto History Raises Doubts about 'Class A Enshrinement'," ''Mainichi Shimbun'' Tokyo edition morning edition: August 8, 2006</ref> (Note: {{ill|Yasukuni Shrine|ja|靖国神社|qq=draft|qq=draft}} is not a {{ill|Bekkaku Kanpeisha|ja|別表神社|qq=draft|qq=draft}} of the Association of Shinto Shrines.)
In 1992 (Heisei 4), he passed away at his home in {{ill|Kamakura|ja|鎌倉市|qq=draft|qq=draft}} at the age of 82.
{{ill|Bunzō Hashikawa|ja|橋川文三|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, who had experiences of debates with Ashizu, praised him as "an advanced polemicist among conservatives," noting that his theory of the Emperor system "should be noted as surpassing the level of traditional right-wingers." He pointed out that Ashizu tried to grasp the problem of the Emperor system from a comparative institutional perspective, based on research in European political thought history, pursuing an open form of "Kokutai theory" as his characteristic.<ref>From ''Postwar Japanese Thought Series 7: Conservative Thought,'' edited by Bunzō Hashikawa, Chikuma Shobō, 1968</ref>
{{ill|Yoshio Ōishi|ja|大石義雄|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, in ''Twenty Years of the Constitution'', praised Ashizu's ''Words of a Native'' and described Ashizu as "a modern-day Kitabatake Chikafusa."
After the war, he had friendships that transcended ideological positions with {{ill|Saburō Ichi'i|ja|市井三郎|qq=draft|qq=draft}} and {{ill|Shunsuke Tsurumi|ja|鶴見俊輔|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, who were editors of ''{{ill|Shisō no Kagaku|ja|思想の科学|qq=draft|qq=draft}}'', and the critic {{ill|Yoshihiko Takeuchi|ja|竹内好|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, and he often contributed to the same magazine.
He was also well-versed in Korean culture and had interactions with {{ill|Lyuh Woon-hyung|ja|呂運亨|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, an independence activist in Korea during the Japanese occupation. It is said that when Ashizu visited the {{ill|Korea University (Japan)|ja|朝鮮大学校|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (in Japan), he was moved upon seeing a portrait of {{ill|Kim Il-sung|ja|金日成|qq=draft|qq=draft}} in the middle of the reception room, and remarked, "This is what current Japanese people lack. In the past, the 'Imperial Portrait' of the Emperor was enshrined in every school; we need to restore that situation."<ref>{{Cite book |author=Hideki Kajimura |title=Overcoming Exclusionism: A History of Korea |publisher=Heibonsha Library |year=2014 |page=25}}</ref>
== Family ==
[[File:Iwasaki Seishichi and his friends.jpg|thumb|Nara Tarō Ōmiwa (center of back row)]]
* Father: '''{{ill|Ashizu Kojiro|ja|葦津耕次郎|qq=draft|qq=draft}}''' (1878–1940) – Shintoist.<ref name=nishiya>{{Cite journal|author=Takafumi Nishiya |title=Ashizu Kojiro: The Trajectory of a Shinto Practitioner during the 'State Shinto' Period |volume=Kyoto University Doctoral Thesis |issue=No.14958 |year=2009 |naid=500000489926 |url=https://hdl.handle.net/2433/126522}}</ref> He served at {{ill|Hakozaki Shrine|ja|筥崎宮|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, devoted himself to Shinto service, and later became active as a businessman running mining and shrine construction businesses, while also engaging as a commentator expressing views on political and social issues from the perspective of Shinto and shrines, such as the {{ill|Korean Shrine Deity Controversy|ja|朝鮮神宮御祭神論争|qq=draft|qq=draft}}.<ref>[http://kaeizyuku.com/2020/01/14/visit-graves/ January 14, 2020 'Visit to the Ashizu Family Grave'] Kaei-juku</ref>
* Grandfather: '''Ashizu Isoo''' (–1903) – Chief priest of Hakozaki Shrine.<ref name=asia>Uraube Noboru, [http://asiarestoration.com/wp-content/uploads/2016/06/daiajia1st.pdf 'Daijia wo Tsuranuita Ōmiwa Asahei'], ''Dai Ajia'' inaugural issue, June 30, 2016, Dai Ajia Kenkyūkai</ref> For a time, he also concurrently served as chief priest of the {{ill|Kashii Shrine|ja|香椎宮|qq=draft|qq=draft}}.<ref>[https://dl.ndl.go.jp/info:ndljp/pid/2947481/9 Official Gazette. June 26, 1897]</ref>
* Great-uncle: '''{{ill|Chōbei Ōmiwa|ja|大三輪長兵衛|qq=draft|qq=draft}}''' (1835–1908) – Brother of his grandfather. After working at an oil shop and rice shop in Osaka, he opened a Matsumae merchant house,<ref>[https://dl.ndl.go.jp/info:ndljp/pid/778835/97 'Chōbei Ōmiwa', ''Meiji Fugo Chifujidai'', Sumitei Inshi (Daigakukan, 1902)]</ref> and later made a fortune in cotton trade, serving as president of the 58th National Bank, chairman of the Osaka Clearing House, Osaka Prefectural Assembly chairman, and Osaka City Council chairman, among other positions. In 1898, he became a member of the {{ill|House of Representatives|ja|衆議院|qq=draft|qq=draft}}.<ref name=sangyo/><ref>[https://www.ndl.go.jp/portrait/datas/36/ 'Chōbei Ōmiwa'], Portraits of Modern Japanese Historical Figures, National Diet Library</ref><ref>[https://kotobank.jp/word/大三輪長兵衛-1061443 'Chōbei Ōmiwa'], Kotobank</ref><ref>[http://www.pref.osaka.lg.jp/attach/3059/00006976/seifuku-itiran_20200522.pdf 'List of Former Chairmen and Vice-Chairmen of Osaka Prefectural Assembly'], Osaka Prefecture, 2020</ref>
* Son: '''{{ill|Ashizu Yasukuni|ja|葦津泰國|qq=draft|qq=draft}}''' (1937–2024) – Graduate of Tokyo Metropolitan University. Former president of Jinja Shinpo Sha. Author of ''The Life of Chōbei Ōmiwa'' (Ashizu Office, 2008), among others.
== Works ==
[Full list of his publications, including major works and collected editions.]
=== Collected Works ===
* ''Ashizu Ujihiko Selected Works'' – Edited by the same Editorial Committee<ref>Each with commentary by {{ill|Mitsuo Suzuki|ja|鈴木満男|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (a disciple)</ref>, Jinja Shinpo Sha, 1996 1. ''Emperor, Shinto, Constitution'' 2. ''As Successors of the Restoration'' 3. ''Current Affairs and Character Critiques''
* ''"Let's Read the Shōwa Era": Ashizu Ujihiko's Claims Series'' – Ashizu Office, 2005–2007 1. ''Japan's Monarchy: Studies on the Emperor System'' 2. ''Eternal Restorationist'' 3. ''The End of Modern Democracy: Revival of Japanese Thought'' 4. ''Words of a Native: The Logic of Trust and Loyalty'' 5. ''Greater Asianism and Toyama Mitsuru'' 6. ''Living the Shōwa Era: The Spirit and Thoughts of the People of the Divine Land''

revision.comment: 102 revisions imported: Bot: import full history from ja:葦津珍彦 up to rev 102315627
revision.text:
'''葦津 珍彦'''（あしづ うずひこ、[[1909年]]〈[[明治]]42年〉[[7月17日]] - [[1992年]]〈[[平成]]4年〉[[6月10日]]）は、日本の[[神道]]家。[[民族派]]の論客としても著名であった<ref>堀幸雄『戦後の右翼勢力』勁草書房・1993年・増補版。</ref>。
== 経歴 == [[葦津耕次郎]]の長男として福岡県筥崎（現・[[福岡市]]東区[[箱崎 (福岡市)|箱崎]]）に生れる。葦津家は[[筥崎宮]]の[[社家]]の一つである大神家の末裔であり、伯父である[[葦津洗造]]は同宮の宮司であった<ref name="#1">葦津泰國による序文、『昭和史を生きて 葦津珍彦の主張シリーズⅣ』より</ref>。1922年（[[大正]]11年）[[東京都立小石川高等学校|東京府立第五中学校]]に入学。この頃から社会主義に憧れ、[[社会科学研究会]]に参加し、関連する書物を読み漁ったという<ref name="#1"/>。周囲はこれを心配し、卒業後は[[國學院大學]]に進んだ{{要出典|date=2017年7月5日 (水) 08:33 (UTC)}}。別に[[東京外国語学校 (旧制)|東京外国語学校]]（現：[[東京外国語大学]]）にも入学するが、いずれも退学した{{要出典|date=2017年7月5日 (水) 08:33 (UTC)}}。1928年（[[昭和]]3年）には[[福島高等商業学校]]（現・[[福島大学]]）に入学するも、翌年退学。1931年、[[日本共産党]]に所属する友人の勧めで[[しんぶん赤旗|アカハタ]]に巻頭論文を執筆した<ref name=ashidu>昆野伸幸「戦中・戦後における葦津珍彦の思想―神道観を中心に」『「1890 ∼ 1950年代日本における《語り》についての学際的研究」成果論集』p.63 2012年</ref>。しかし、翌年2月には父の影響を受けて父と同じ「[[祭政一致]]の天皇国日本」実現を理想とする伝統保守の尊皇神道へと[[転向]]したとされる<ref name=ashidu />。
1932年（昭和7年）、父の営んでいた事業を継いで社寺工務所の代表として[[宮大工|神社建築業]]に従事する<ref>神社本廳五十年誌(非売品)-平成八年五月発行-発行者:神社本庁 P74-神社新報社社友葦津珍彦氏帰幽(六月十日)より</ref>。この時期、[[頭山満]]や[[今泉定助]]、[[高山昇]]、[[緒方竹虎]]、[[内田良平 (政治運動家)|内田良平]]、[[井上孚麿]]らと交流をもち、[[太平洋戦争]]が始まる前は、この戦争を「必敗の戦い」であるとして反対運動を行い、開戦後は開戦の[[詔勅]]に示された「やむにやまれぬ独立国としての存続を確保する」戦争目的のみを守り続けることを主張するなど、独自の政治的活動を展開した{{要出典|date=2017年7月5日 (水) 08:33 (UTC)}}。戦時中には日本の同盟国であった[[ナチス・ドイツ]]や、[[東條英機]]内閣の政策を批判する論文が発禁となり、逮捕された事もあった{{要出典|date=2017年7月5日 (水) 08:33 (UTC)}}。
戦後すぐ会社を解散し[[吉田茂 (内務官僚)|吉田茂]]（[[内務省 (日本)|内務省]]出身）、宮川宗徳（のち本庁事務総長）や、[[徳川宗敬]]（のち本庁統理）らを補佐し、[[神社本庁]]の設立に尽力し、さらに本庁の[[教学]]広報の一環として『[[神社新報]]』が創刊されると、主筆と共に発行母体の神社新報社<ref>2012年（平成24年）に神社新報社編で『次代へつなぐ葦津珍彦の精神と思想 生誕百年・歿後二十年を記念して』（神社新報社〈神社新報ブックス〉16）を刊行した。</ref>の経営も引き受けた。
1968年（昭和43年）の退職後も執筆活動や後学の指導にあたり、[[国体]]護持・[[神社]]護持運動の最前線にあって、神道界（[[日本を守る国民会議]]を経て、現：[[日本会議]]）や[[民族派学生組織|民族派運動]]（[[日本青年協議会]]）、などに大きな影響を与え続けた。一方で戦時中自らが批判していた東條らA級戦犯の合祀には敗戦の責任から疑義を呈していた<ref>「靖国 「戦後」からどこへ3 神道史大御所の論文 「A級合祀」に疑義」‐ 毎日新聞東京版朝刊：2006年8月8日付</ref>（なお、[[靖国神社]]は神社本庁の[[別表神社]]ではない）。[[1992年]]（平成4年）、[[鎌倉市]]の自宅にて死去。享年82。
葦津との論争の経験もある[[橋川文三]]は、葦津を「保守派中の先鋭なポレミスト」と評した上で、その天皇制論を「伝統的右翼者流の水準をこえたものとして注目される」とし、天皇制の問題を欧州政治思想史の研究を背景とする比較制度論的見地からとらえようとする、いわば「国体論」の開かれた形態を追求する点が特徴である<ref>橋川文三責任編集『[[戦後日本思想大系]]7 保守の思想』（[[筑摩書房]]、1968年）より</ref>としている。
[[大石義雄]]は、『憲法二十年』<ref>大石義雄『憲法二十年』、246頁（[[有信堂]]、1966年） </ref>で葦津の『土民のことば』を称賛し、葦津を「今様北畠親房」と表現した。
戦後は『[[思想の科学]]』編集者でもあった[[市井三郎]]や[[鶴見俊輔]]、論客[[竹内好]]などとも思想的立場を超え交友があり、同誌にもしばしば寄稿していた。
また、[[朝鮮の文化]]にも造詣が深く、[[日本統治時代の朝鮮]]における独立運動家だった[[呂運亨]]とも交流があった。葦津は、[[朝鮮大学校 (日本)|朝鮮大学校]]を訪問した際、応接室の真ん中に[[金日成]]の肖像が飾られているのを見て感激し、「今の日本人にはこれがないからだめなんだ。かつては天皇陛下の『御真影』がどこの学校にも奉られていた、あの状況をまた取り戻さなきゃあ」と述べたという<ref>{{Cite book |和書 |author=[[梶村秀樹]] |title=排外主義克服のための朝鮮史 |publisher=平凡社ライブラリー |year=2014 |page=25}}</ref>。
== 親族 == [[ファイル:Iwasaki Seishichi and his friends.jpg|サムネイル|大三輪奈良太郎（後列中央）]] * 父・'''葦津耕次郎'''（1878-1940） - 神道家<ref name=nishiya>{{Cite journal|和書|author=西矢貴文 |title=葦津耕次郎 : 「国家神道」期における一神道人の軌跡 |volume=京都大学 博士論文 |issue=甲第14958号 |year=2009 |naid=500000489926 |url=https://hdl.handle.net/2433/126522}}</ref>。[[筥崎宮]]に奉職し神明奉仕に励み、後には鉱山業や社寺建築業を営む実業家として活躍する一方、[[朝鮮神宮]]御祭神論争など神道・神社の見地から時の政治・社会問題に発言する言論人としても活躍<ref>[http://kaeizyuku.com/2020/01/14/visit-graves/ 令和2年1月14日 「葦津家之墓」墓参]花瑛塾</ref>。民間の神道人として活動しつつ、[[玄洋社]]の[[頭山満]]と親交を結ぶなど独自の活動をした<ref>[https://www.chugainippoh.co.jp/info/ruikotu/ruikotu015-02-001.html 第15回「涙骨賞」受賞論文 奨励賞 葦津珍彦の思想について―戦後における天皇論・神道論を中心に 今西宏之氏]中外日報</ref>。著書に『日支事変の解決法』<ref>[https://dl.ndl.go.jp/info:ndljp/pid/1270782日支事変の解決法] (葦津耕次郎, 1938)、国立国会図書館デジタルコレクション</ref>。耕次郎の兄・葦津洗造は官幣中社筥崎宮宮司<ref>[https://dl.ndl.go.jp/info:ndljp/pid/2951432/17 官報. 1910年05月31日]</ref> * 祖父・'''葦津磯夫'''（-1903） - 筥崎宮宮司<ref name=asia>[http://asiarestoration.com/wp-content/uploads/2016/06/daiajia1st.pdf 王道を貫いた大三輪朝兵衛]浦辺登、『大亜細亜』創刊号、平成28年6月30日、大アジア研究会</ref>。一時、官幣大社[[香椎宮]]宮司も兼任<ref>[https://dl.ndl.go.jp/info:ndljp/pid/2947481/9 官報. 1897年06月26日]</ref>。[[福岡県立福岡農業高等学校|福岡農学校]]校長として[[横井時敬]]を招いて日本の農業改革に関わった<ref>『大三輪長兵衛の生涯』p208</ref>。 *大伯父・'''[[大三輪長兵衛]]'''（1835-1908） -祖父の兄。大阪の油屋、米屋の手代を経て、松前問屋を開き<ref>[https://dl.ndl.go.jp/info:ndljp/pid/778835/97 大三輪長兵衛]『明治富豪致富時代 : 付・日本全国五十万円以上の資産家』墨堤隠士 著 (大学館, 1902) </ref>、のちに綿貿易で財を成し、第五十八国立銀行頭取、大阪手形交換所会頭、大阪府会議長、同市会議長等を歴任、1898年に衆議院議員<ref name=sangyo/><ref>[https://www.ndl.go.jp/portrait/datas/36/ 大三輪長兵衛 おおみわ ちょうべえ]近代日本人の肖像、国立国会図書館</ref><ref>[https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%A4%A7%E4%B8%89%E8%BC%AA%E9%95%B7%E5%85%B5%E8%A1%9B-1061443 大三輪長兵衛]コトバンク</ref><ref>[http://www.pref.osaka.lg.jp/attach/3059/00006976/seifuku-itiran_20200522.pdf 大阪府議会歴代議長・副議長一覧]大阪府、[[令和]]2年5月</ref>。[[五代友厚]]とも親しかった<ref>{{Cite journal|和書|author=毛利敏彦 |title=大阪商業講習所の誕生と福澤諭吉 : 大阪市立大学事始め |journal=近代日本研究 |issn=0911-4181 |publisher=慶應義塾福澤研究センター |year=1985 |volume=2 |pages=207-236 |naid=120005349221 |url=https://koara.lib.keio.ac.jp/xoonips/modules/xoonips/detail.php?koara_id=AN10005325-19850000-0207}}</ref>。朝鮮国王・[[高宗 (朝鮮)|高宗]]とも通じ、朝鮮政府[[典圜局]]の新貨幣発行事業の顧問となり、交換局会弁なる総裁職を任じられた<ref name=sangyo>『近代日本の伸銅業: 水車から生まれた金属加工』産業新聞社、2008/12/12 p226-234</ref>。長兵衛の長男・'''大三輪奈良太郎''' (1867年生)は、米国遊学後、[[日本銀行]]を経て名古屋明治銀行頭取<ref>[http://jahis.law.nagoya-u.ac.jp/who/docs/who4-3189 大三輪奈良太郎]『人事興信録』第4版 [大正4(1915)年1月]</ref><ref>[https://dl.ndl.go.jp/info:ndljp/pid/913714/12 大三輪奈良太郎君]『名古屋百紳士』馬場籍生 著 (名古屋百紳士発行所, 1917) </ref>。その前妻きよは[[岩村高俊]]の娘、後妻の美弥は[[山内豊盈]]([[山内容堂]]弟)の娘<ref name=asia/>。 * 曽祖父・'''大神嘉納''' - 神職。筥崎宮の権大宮司・大神一貞の次男<ref>『大三輪長兵衛の生涯』葦津泰国（葦津事務所、2008年）p36</ref>。妻は[[福岡藩]]士・井上弥七（弥八）の娘<ref name=asia/><ref>『大三輪長兵衛の生涯』p40</ref>。兄（一貞の長男）の大神多門は、[[神仏習合]]で仏教の下に置かれていた神道の復活のため、[[神仏分離]]を求めて京の[[一条家]]を頼りにしたところ、福岡藩[[寺社奉行]]の怒りを買い、[[玄海島]]に遠島され、その地で没した<ref name=asia/><ref name=nishiya/>。その影響で大神家は筥崎宮を追われ、大三輪長兵衛が商人として成功するまで極貧生活を強いられたという<ref name=asia/>。 * 長男・'''葦津泰国'''（1937 - 2024） - [[東京都立大学 (1949-2011)|東京都立大学]]卒。神社新報社・元社長ほか。著書に『大三輪長兵衛の生涯』（葦津事務所、2008年）などがある。
== 著書 == * 『日本民族の世界政策私見』 日の丸組、昭和9年（1934年） * 『論集』 兄弟社、昭和17年（1942年） * 『臣民の権利』 報國新報社、昭和17年（1942年） * 『終戦始末記』 神社新報非公開文書、昭和22年（1947年） * 『日本国憲法は如何にして作られたか』 神社新報非公開文書、昭和23年（1948年） * 『共産思想の追放』 神道青年全国協議会、昭和24年（1949年） * 『天皇意思と一般意思』 神社新報社、昭和27年（1952年） * 『天皇・神道・憲法』 神社新報社政教研究室、昭和29年（1954年） * 『憲法はこのまヽでよいか』 神社新報社政教研究室、昭和30年（1955年） * 『現代神社の諸問題』 神社新報社、昭和30年（1955年） * 『近代政治と良心問題』 神社新報社、昭和30年（1955年） * 『神社新報編集室記録』 神社新報社、昭和31年（1956年） * 『宗教法人法改正の前提』 神社本庁、昭和32年（1957年） * 『皇位と神宮』 神社本庁調査部、昭和32年（1957年） * 『神宮と国家』 神社本庁調査部、昭和32年（1957年） * 『中華革命とロシア革命』 内外維新研究所、昭和33年（1958年） * 『国法と宗教』 神社本庁調査部、昭和33年（1958年） * 『三笠宮殿下へ御忠告』 [[国民の祝日]]研究会、昭和34年（1959年） * 『神宮制度改正要綱』 財団法人[[伊勢神宮]]奉賛会、昭和34年（1959年） * 『伊勢神宮の制度改正について』 神社本庁、昭和34年（1959年） * 『神社と皇位と』 神社本庁、昭和35年（1960年） * 『土民のことば <small>信頼と忠誠との情理</small>』 神社新報社、昭和36年（1961年） * 『神宮と皇位と』 神社本庁、昭和36年（1961年） * 『天皇・祭祀・憲法』 神社本庁、昭和37年（1962年） **増訂版・同、昭和43年（1968年） * 『皇位継承と祖宗の神器』 兄弟文庫、昭和37年（1962年） * 『神宮と憲法』 神社新報社、昭和38年（1963年） * 『明治維新と東洋の解放』 新勢力社、昭和39年（1964年） **新版・皇學館大学出版部、平成7年（1995年） * 『維新問答』 神社新報社、昭和40年（1965年） * 『大アジア主義と頭山満』 日本教文社、昭和40年（1965年）、改訂版1972年 * 『日本の君主制 天皇制研究』 神社新報社・新勢力社、昭和41年（1966年） * 『明治維新と現代日本』 神社本庁明治維新百年記念事業委員会、昭和41年（1966年） * 『青春の日忘れがたし』 無題私家版論文集、昭和42年（1967年） * 『ロシヤ革命史話』 新勢力社、昭和42年（1967年） * 『神社と宗教について』 神社新報社政教研究室、昭和43年（1968年） * 『神道的日本民族論』 神社新報社、昭和44年（1969年） * 『武士道 戦闘者の精神』 徳間書店、昭和44年（1969年） **新版・神社新報社、平成14年（2002年） * 『維新への展望』 神社本庁及び新日本協議会東京都支部連合会、昭和44年（1969年） * 『[[今泉定助]]先生を語る』（正・続） 日本大学今泉研究所、昭和44年（1969年） * 『日本人の忠誠心について』 [[日本大学]]今泉研究所、昭和44年（1969年） * 『暴力革命より恐るべき「平和革命」』 神社本庁時局対策本部、昭和44年（1969年） * 『公明党糾弾論とその批判』 神社本庁時局対策本部、昭和45年（1970年） * 『葦津耕次郎追想録』、昭和45年（1970年）、私家版 * 『天皇 日本のいのち』 日本教文社、昭和46年（1971年）、編著<ref>他は小出英経、[[田中忠雄 (評論家)|田中忠雄]]、[[土屋道雄]]、[[原敬吾]]、夜久正雄</ref> * 『国体問答』 神社新報社、昭和46年（1971年） * 『祭祀と統治の間』 神道政治連盟、昭和46年（1971年） * 『忠誠の心理と論理』 神道政治連盟、昭和47年（1972年） * 『近代民主主義の終末 日本思想の復活』 日本教文社、昭和47年（1972年） * 『天皇 日本人の精神史』 神社新報社、昭和48年（1973年） * 『異郷の同胞を祖国へ』 神道政治連盟、昭和48年（1973年） * 『革命と反革命・ロシア革命史話（抄）』 神道政治連盟、昭和48年（1973年） * 『皇室の高貴なる精神の伝統』 [[皇學館大學]]出版部、昭和49年（1974年） * 『新勢力創立二十周年記念 葦津先生御講演』 大昭会・新勢力クラブ、昭和50年（1975年） * 『永遠の維新者』 二月社、昭和50年（1975年）。[[西郷隆盛|西郷南州]]伝ほか **新版・[[葦書房]]、昭和56年（1981年） * 『現代社会思潮と日本文明』 [[神社本庁]]時局対策本部、昭和51年（1976年） * 『[[山田顕義]]と日本法学』 日本大学今泉研究所、昭和53年（1978年） * 『天皇制への疑問と回答』 [[大東塾]]出版部、昭和54年（1979年） * 『[[大日本帝国憲法]]制定史』 サンケイ新聞社、昭和54年（1979年） * 『みやびと覇権 類纂天皇論』 [[日本教文社]]、昭和55年（1980年） * 『時の流れ 戦後三十有余年時評集』 [[神社新報社]]、昭和56年（1981年） * 『宗教法人法とその税制』 神社新報社法律研究会、昭和60年（1985年） * 『神国の民の心』 島津書房、昭和61年（1986年） * 『[[国家神道]]とは何だったのか』 神社新報社、昭和62年（1987年） **新版・[[阪本是丸]]註 [[神社新報]]社、平成18年（2006年） * 『天皇 昭和から平成へ』 神社新報社「神社新報ブックス」、平成元年（1989年） * 『一神道人の生涯 [[高山昇]]先生を回想して』 [[東伏見稲荷神社]]、平成4年（1992年）。非売品 * 『明治憲法の制定史話』神社新報社、平成30年（2018年）
=== 著作集 === * 『葦津珍彦選集』 同・編集委員会編<ref>各・解題は[[鈴木満男 (人類学者)|鈴木満男]]（門下生）</ref>／神社新報社、平成8年（1996年） # 天皇・神道・憲法 #維新の継承者として #時局・人物論 * 『「昭和を読もう」葦津珍彦の主張シリーズ』 葦津事務所、平成17年（2005年）- 平成19年（2007年） # 日本の君主制 天皇制の研究 # 永遠の維新者 # 近代民主主義の終末 日本思想の復活 # 土民のことば 信頼と忠誠との情理 # 大アジア主義と頭山満 # 昭和史を生きて 神国の民の心
== 回想 == *葦津大成『父、兄、私と大東亜戦争 次代への伝言』（神社新報ブックス、1999年）、親族の回想 *『次代へつなぐ葦津珍彦の精神と思想 生誕百年・歿後二十年を記念して』（神社新報ブックス、2012年）、追想集
== 脚注 == {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{Reflist|30em}}
== 参考文献 == <!-- 実際に参考にした文献一覧 --> * 『明治維新と東洋の解放』「序（[[進藤一馬]]）」 新勢力社、昭和39年（1964年） * 『葦津珍彦先生追悼録』 小日本社、平成5年（1993年） * 『葦津珍彦選集 第三巻』 神社新報社、平成8年（1996年）巻末の解説・年譜 *堀幸雄『戦後の右翼勢力』「第三章・戦後右翼のイデオロギー、第二節・葦津珍彦の思想―神社本庁のイデオロギー」（勁草書房・1993年・増補版）。
== 外部リンク == * [https://dl.ndl.go.jp/info:ndljp/pid/1271056 日本人の言葉 : ナチスの蒙を啓く]葦津珍彦 著 (兄弟会, 1940) * [https://web.archive.org/web/20201125060116/https://www.chugainippoh.co.jp/info/ruikotu/ruikotu015-02-001.html 葦津珍彦の思想について―戦後における天皇論・神道論を中心に]今西宏之 中外日報 第15回「涙骨賞」受賞論文 奨励賞 * [https://web.archive.org/web/20090928173400/http://www.geocities.jp/showahistory/book/note02.html 葦津珍彦を読む] * [http://ymatsuda.kill.jp/Ashizu-mokuroku.pdf 葦津珍彦著作一覧]
{{Normdaten}} {{Writer-stub}} {{DEFAULTSORT:あしつ うすひこ}} [[Category:神道に関連する人物]] [[Category:20世紀日本の評論家]] [[Category:日本のナショナリスト]] [[Category:日本の保守思想家]] [[Category:東京都立小石川中等教育学校出身の人物]] [[Category:福岡市出身の人物]] [[Category:1909年生]] [[Category:1992年没]]

revision.comment: Immanuelle moved page [[葦津珍彦]] to [[Uzuhiko Ashizu]] without leaving a redirect: Bot: merge import
revision.text:
'''葦津 珍彦'''（あしづ うずひこ、[[1909年]]〈[[明治]]42年〉[[7月17日]] - [[1992年]]〈[[平成]]4年〉[[6月10日]]）は、日本の[[神道]]家。[[民族派]]の論客としても著名であった<ref>堀幸雄『戦後の右翼勢力』勁草書房・1993年・増補版。</ref>。
== 経歴 == [[葦津耕次郎]]の長男として福岡県筥崎（現・[[福岡市]]東区[[箱崎 (福岡市)|箱崎]]）に生れる。葦津家は[[筥崎宮]]の[[社家]]の一つである大神家の末裔であり、伯父である[[葦津洗造]]は同宮の宮司であった<ref name="#1">葦津泰國による序文、『昭和史を生きて 葦津珍彦の主張シリーズⅣ』より</ref>。1922年（[[大正]]11年）[[東京都立小石川高等学校|東京府立第五中学校]]に入学。この頃から社会主義に憧れ、[[社会科学研究会]]に参加し、関連する書物を読み漁ったという<ref name="#1"/>。周囲はこれを心配し、卒業後は[[國學院大學]]に進んだ{{要出典|date=2017年7月5日 (水) 08:33 (UTC)}}。別に[[東京外国語学校 (旧制)|東京外国語学校]]（現：[[東京外国語大学]]）にも入学するが、いずれも退学した{{要出典|date=2017年7月5日 (水) 08:33 (UTC)}}。1928年（[[昭和]]3年）には[[福島高等商業学校]]（現・[[福島大学]]）に入学するも、翌年退学。1931年、[[日本共産党]]に所属する友人の勧めで[[しんぶん赤旗|アカハタ]]に巻頭論文を執筆した<ref name=ashidu>昆野伸幸「戦中・戦後における葦津珍彦の思想―神道観を中心に」『「1890 ∼ 1950年代日本における《語り》についての学際的研究」成果論集』p.63 2012年</ref>。しかし、翌年2月には父の影響を受けて父と同じ「[[祭政一致]]の天皇国日本」実現を理想とする伝統保守の尊皇神道へと[[転向]]したとされる<ref name=ashidu />。
1932年（昭和7年）、父の営んでいた事業を継いで社寺工務所の代表として[[宮大工|神社建築業]]に従事する<ref>神社本廳五十年誌(非売品)-平成八年五月発行-発行者:神社本庁 P74-神社新報社社友葦津珍彦氏帰幽(六月十日)より</ref>。この時期、[[頭山満]]や[[今泉定助]]、[[高山昇]]、[[緒方竹虎]]、[[内田良平 (政治運動家)|内田良平]]、[[井上孚麿]]らと交流をもち、[[太平洋戦争]]が始まる前は、この戦争を「必敗の戦い」であるとして反対運動を行い、開戦後は開戦の[[詔勅]]に示された「やむにやまれぬ独立国としての存続を確保する」戦争目的のみを守り続けることを主張するなど、独自の政治的活動を展開した{{要出典|date=2017年7月5日 (水) 08:33 (UTC)}}。戦時中には日本の同盟国であった[[ナチス・ドイツ]]や、[[東條英機]]内閣の政策を批判する論文が発禁となり、逮捕された事もあった{{要出典|date=2017年7月5日 (水) 08:33 (UTC)}}。
戦後すぐ会社を解散し[[吉田茂 (内務官僚)|吉田茂]]（[[内務省 (日本)|内務省]]出身）、宮川宗徳（のち本庁事務総長）や、[[徳川宗敬]]（のち本庁統理）らを補佐し、[[神社本庁]]の設立に尽力し、さらに本庁の[[教学]]広報の一環として『[[神社新報]]』が創刊されると、主筆と共に発行母体の神社新報社<ref>2012年（平成24年）に神社新報社編で『次代へつなぐ葦津珍彦の精神と思想 生誕百年・歿後二十年を記念して』（神社新報社〈神社新報ブックス〉16）を刊行した。</ref>の経営も引き受けた。
1968年（昭和43年）の退職後も執筆活動や後学の指導にあたり、[[国体]]護持・[[神社]]護持運動の最前線にあって、神道界（[[日本を守る国民会議]]を経て、現：[[日本会議]]）や[[民族派学生組織|民族派運動]]（[[日本青年協議会]]）、などに大きな影響を与え続けた。一方で戦時中自らが批判していた東條らA級戦犯の合祀には敗戦の責任から疑義を呈していた<ref>「靖国 「戦後」からどこへ3 神道史大御所の論文 「A級合祀」に疑義」‐ 毎日新聞東京版朝刊：2006年8月8日付</ref>（なお、[[靖国神社]]は神社本庁の[[別表神社]]ではない）。[[1992年]]（平成4年）、[[鎌倉市]]の自宅にて死去。享年82。
葦津との論争の経験もある[[橋川文三]]は、葦津を「保守派中の先鋭なポレミスト」と評した上で、その天皇制論を「伝統的右翼者流の水準をこえたものとして注目される」とし、天皇制の問題を欧州政治思想史の研究を背景とする比較制度論的見地からとらえようとする、いわば「国体論」の開かれた形態を追求する点が特徴である<ref>橋川文三責任編集『[[戦後日本思想大系]]7 保守の思想』（[[筑摩書房]]、1968年）より</ref>としている。
[[大石義雄]]は、『憲法二十年』<ref>大石義雄『憲法二十年』、246頁（[[有信堂]]、1966年） </ref>で葦津の『土民のことば』を称賛し、葦津を「今様北畠親房」と表現した。
戦後は『[[思想の科学]]』編集者でもあった[[市井三郎]]や[[鶴見俊輔]]、論客[[竹内好]]などとも思想的立場を超え交友があり、同誌にもしばしば寄稿していた。
また、[[朝鮮の文化]]にも造詣が深く、[[日本統治時代の朝鮮]]における独立運動家だった[[呂運亨]]とも交流があった。葦津は、[[朝鮮大学校 (日本)|朝鮮大学校]]を訪問した際、応接室の真ん中に[[金日成]]の肖像が飾られているのを見て感激し、「今の日本人にはこれがないからだめなんだ。かつては天皇陛下の『御真影』がどこの学校にも奉られていた、あの状況をまた取り戻さなきゃあ」と述べたという<ref>{{Cite book |和書 |author=[[梶村秀樹]] |title=排外主義克服のための朝鮮史 |publisher=平凡社ライブラリー |year=2014 |page=25}}</ref>。
== 親族 == [[ファイル:Iwasaki Seishichi and his friends.jpg|サムネイル|大三輪奈良太郎（後列中央）]] * 父・'''葦津耕次郎'''（1878-1940） - 神道家<ref name=nishiya>{{Cite journal|和書|author=西矢貴文 |title=葦津耕次郎 : 「国家神道」期における一神道人の軌跡 |volume=京都大学 博士論文 |issue=甲第14958号 |year=2009 |naid=500000489926 |url=https://hdl.handle.net/2433/126522}}</ref>。[[筥崎宮]]に奉職し神明奉仕に励み、後には鉱山業や社寺建築業を営む実業家として活躍する一方、[[朝鮮神宮]]御祭神論争など神道・神社の見地から時の政治・社会問題に発言する言論人としても活躍<ref>[http://kaeizyuku.com/2020/01/14/visit-graves/ 令和2年1月14日 「葦津家之墓」墓参]花瑛塾</ref>。民間の神道人として活動しつつ、[[玄洋社]]の[[頭山満]]と親交を結ぶなど独自の活動をした<ref>[https://www.chugainippoh.co.jp/info/ruikotu/ruikotu015-02-001.html 第15回「涙骨賞」受賞論文 奨励賞 葦津珍彦の思想について―戦後における天皇論・神道論を中心に 今西宏之氏]中外日報</ref>。著書に『日支事変の解決法』<ref>[https://dl.ndl.go.jp/info:ndljp/pid/1270782日支事変の解決法] (葦津耕次郎, 1938)、国立国会図書館デジタルコレクション</ref>。耕次郎の兄・葦津洗造は官幣中社筥崎宮宮司<ref>[https://dl.ndl.go.jp/info:ndljp/pid/2951432/17 官報. 1910年05月31日]</ref> * 祖父・'''葦津磯夫'''（-1903） - 筥崎宮宮司<ref name=asia>[http://asiarestoration.com/wp-content/uploads/2016/06/daiajia1st.pdf 王道を貫いた大三輪朝兵衛]浦辺登、『大亜細亜』創刊号、平成28年6月30日、大アジア研究会</ref>。一時、官幣大社[[香椎宮]]宮司も兼任<ref>[https://dl.ndl.go.jp/info:ndljp/pid/2947481/9 官報. 1897年06月26日]</ref>。[[福岡県立福岡農業高等学校|福岡農学校]]校長として[[横井時敬]]を招いて日本の農業改革に関わった<ref>『大三輪長兵衛の生涯』p208</ref>。 *大伯父・'''[[大三輪長兵衛]]'''（1835-1908） -祖父の兄。大阪の油屋、米屋の手代を経て、松前問屋を開き<ref>[https://dl.ndl.go.jp/info:ndljp/pid/778835/97 大三輪長兵衛]『明治富豪致富時代 : 付・日本全国五十万円以上の資産家』墨堤隠士 著 (大学館, 1902) </ref>、のちに綿貿易で財を成し、第五十八国立銀行頭取、大阪手形交換所会頭、大阪府会議長、同市会議長等を歴任、1898年に衆議院議員<ref name=sangyo/><ref>[https://www.ndl.go.jp/portrait/datas/36/ 大三輪長兵衛 おおみわ ちょうべえ]近代日本人の肖像、国立国会図書館</ref><ref>[https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%A4%A7%E4%B8%89%E8%BC%AA%E9%95%B7%E5%85%B5%E8%A1%9B-1061443 大三輪長兵衛]コトバンク</ref><ref>[http://www.pref.osaka.lg.jp/attach/3059/00006976/seifuku-itiran_20200522.pdf 大阪府議会歴代議長・副議長一覧]大阪府、[[令和]]2年5月</ref>。[[五代友厚]]とも親しかった<ref>{{Cite journal|和書|author=毛利敏彦 |title=大阪商業講習所の誕生と福澤諭吉 : 大阪市立大学事始め |journal=近代日本研究 |issn=0911-4181 |publisher=慶應義塾福澤研究センター |year=1985 |volume=2 |pages=207-236 |naid=120005349221 |url=https://koara.lib.keio.ac.jp/xoonips/modules/xoonips/detail.php?koara_id=AN10005325-19850000-0207}}</ref>。朝鮮国王・[[高宗 (朝鮮)|高宗]]とも通じ、朝鮮政府[[典圜局]]の新貨幣発行事業の顧問となり、交換局会弁なる総裁職を任じられた<ref name=sangyo>『近代日本の伸銅業: 水車から生まれた金属加工』産業新聞社、2008/12/12 p226-234</ref>。長兵衛の長男・'''大三輪奈良太郎''' (1867年生)は、米国遊学後、[[日本銀行]]を経て名古屋明治銀行頭取<ref>[http://jahis.law.nagoya-u.ac.jp/who/docs/who4-3189 大三輪奈良太郎]『人事興信録』第4版 [大正4(1915)年1月]</ref><ref>[https://dl.ndl.go.jp/info:ndljp/pid/913714/12 大三輪奈良太郎君]『名古屋百紳士』馬場籍生 著 (名古屋百紳士発行所, 1917) </ref>。その前妻きよは[[岩村高俊]]の娘、後妻の美弥は[[山内豊盈]]([[山内容堂]]弟)の娘<ref name=asia/>。 * 曽祖父・'''大神嘉納''' - 神職。筥崎宮の権大宮司・大神一貞の次男<ref>『大三輪長兵衛の生涯』葦津泰国（葦津事務所、2008年）p36</ref>。妻は[[福岡藩]]士・井上弥七（弥八）の娘<ref name=asia/><ref>『大三輪長兵衛の生涯』p40</ref>。兄（一貞の長男）の大神多門は、[[神仏習合]]で仏教の下に置かれていた神道の復活のため、[[神仏分離]]を求めて京の[[一条家]]を頼りにしたところ、福岡藩[[寺社奉行]]の怒りを買い、[[玄海島]]に遠島され、その地で没した<ref name=asia/><ref name=nishiya/>。その影響で大神家は筥崎宮を追われ、大三輪長兵衛が商人として成功するまで極貧生活を強いられたという<ref name=asia/>。 * 長男・'''葦津泰国'''（1937 - 2024） - [[東京都立大学 (1949-2011)|東京都立大学]]卒。神社新報社・元社長ほか。著書に『大三輪長兵衛の生涯』（葦津事務所、2008年）などがある。

神社新報社「神社新報ブックス」、平成元年（1989年） * 『一神道人の生涯 [[高山昇]]先生を回想して』 [[東伏見稲荷神社]]、平成4年（1992年）。非売品 * 『明治憲法の制定史話』神社新報社、平成30年（2018年）
=== 著作集 === * 『葦津珍彦選集』 同・編集委員会編<ref>各・解題は[[鈴木満男 (人類学者)|鈴木満男]]（門下生）</ref>／神社新報社、平成8年（1996年） # 天皇・神道・憲法 #維新の継承者として #時局・人物論 * 『「昭和を読もう」葦津珍彦の主張シリーズ』 葦津事務所、平成17年（2005年）- 平成19年（2007年） # 日本の君主制 天皇制の研究 # 永遠の維新者 # 近代民主主義の終末 日本思想の復活 # 土民のことば 信頼と忠誠との情理 # 大アジア主義と頭山満 # 昭和史を生きて 神国の民の心
== 回想 == *葦津大成『父、兄、私と大東亜戦争 次代への伝言』（神社新報ブックス、1999年）、親族の回想 *『次代へつなぐ葦津珍彦の精神と思想 生誕百年・歿後二十年を記念して』（神社新報ブックス、2012年）、追想集

{{Normdaten}} {{Writer-stub}} {{DEFAULTSORT:あしつ うすひこ}} [[Category:神道に関連する人物]] [[Category:20世紀日本の評論家]] [[Category:日本のナショナリスト]] [[Category:日本の保守思想家]] [[Category:東京都立小石川中等教育学校出身の人物]] [[Category:福岡市出身の人物]] [[Category:1909年生]] [[Category:1992年没]]

'''Ashizu Ujihiko''' (葦津 珍彦, July 17, 1909 – June 10, 1992) was a Japanese {{ill|Shinto|ja|神道|qq=draft|qq=draft}} scholar. He was also renowned as a commentator of the {{ill|Nationalist|ja|民族派|qq=draft|qq=draft}} movement.<ref>Yukio Hori, ''Postwar Right-Wing Forces'', Keiso Shobō, 1993, Expanded Edition.</ref>
== Career ==
He was born as the eldest son of {{ill|Ashizu Kojiro|ja|葦津耕次郎|qq=draft|qq=draft}} in Hakozaki, Fukuoka Prefecture (now Hakozaki, {{ill|Higashi-ku, Fukuoka|ja|福岡市東区|qq=draft|qq=draft}}). The Ashizu family is a descendant of the Ogami family, one of the shrine families of {{ill|Hakozaki Shrine|ja|筥崎宮|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, and his uncle, {{ill|Ashizu Seizō|ja|葦津洗造|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, was the chief priest of the same shrine.<ref name="#1">Foreword by {{ill|Ashizu Yasukuni|ja|葦津泰國|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, from ''Living the Shōwa Era: The Claims of Ujihiko Ashizu Series IV''</ref>
In 1922 (Taishō 11), he entered {{ill|Tokyo Metropolitan Koishikawa High School|ja|東京都立小石川高等学校|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (then Tokyo Prefectural Fifth Junior High School). Around this time, he yearned for socialism, participated in the Social Science Study Group, and avidly read related books.<ref name="#1"/>
Those around him were concerned, and after graduation, he went on to {{ill|Kokugakuin University|ja|國學院大學|qq=draft|qq=draft}}.{{Citation needed|date=July 5, 2017}} He also enrolled in the {{ill|Tokyo School of Foreign Languages|ja|東京外国語学校 (旧制)|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, but dropped out of both.{{Citation needed|date=July 5, 2017}} In 1928 (Shōwa 3), he entered {{ill|Fukushima University|ja|福島大学|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (then Fukushima Higher Commercial School), but dropped out the following year.
In 1931, at the recommendation of a friend who belonged to the {{ill|Japanese Communist Party|ja|日本共産党|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, he wrote a leading article for the newspaper {{ill|Akahata|ja|しんぶん赤旗|qq=draft|qq=draft}}.<ref name=ashidu>{{Cite journal|author=Konno Nobuyuki|title=The Thought of Ashizu Ujihiko during and after the War—Focusing on His View of Shinto|journal=Interdisciplinary Research on 'Narratives' in Japan from 1890 to the 1950s|page=63|year=2012}}</ref> However, in February of the following year, under the influence of his father, he is said to have converted to traditional conservative imperial {{ill|Shinto|ja|神道|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, which aimed for the realization of "Japan, the Emperor's Nation with Unity of Rites and Government," like his father.<ref name=ashidu />
In 1932 (Shōwa 7), he succeeded his father's business and engaged in the construction of shrines and temples as the representative of a shrine and temple construction office.<ref>"Fifty Years of the Association of Shinto Shrines" (Non-commercial publication), May 1996, Publisher: {{ill|Association of Shinto Shrines|ja|神社本庁|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, p.74—Obituary of Ashizu Ujihiko, associate of {{ill|Jinja Shinpo Sha|ja|神社新報社|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (June 10)</ref> During this period, he interacted with {{ill|Toyama Mitsuru|ja|頭山満|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Teisuke Imaizumi|ja|今泉定助|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Takayama Noboru|ja|高山昇|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Ogata Taketora|ja|緒方竹虎|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Ryōhei Uchida|ja|内田良平|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Inoue Tadamaro|ja|井上孚麿|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, and others. Before the outbreak of the {{ill|Pacific War|ja|太平洋戦争|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, he opposed the war, viewing it as an "unwinnable battle," and after the outbreak, he engaged in unique political activities, such as insisting on adhering only to the war objectives shown in the Imperial Rescript on the outbreak of war, which was "to secure the survival of an independent nation as unavoidable."{{Citation needed|date=July 5, 2017}}
During the war, some of his critical articles on {{ill|Nazi Germany|ja|ナチス・ドイツ|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, Japan's ally, and the policies of the {{ill|Hideki Tōjō|ja|東條英機|qq=draft|qq=draft}} Cabinet were banned, and he was arrested at times.{{Citation needed|date=July 5, 2017}}
Immediately after the war, he dissolved his company and assisted {{ill|Shigeru Yoshida (Home Ministry official)|ja|吉田茂 (内務官僚)|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Munenori Miyagawa|ja|宮川宗徳|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (later Secretary-General of the main office), and {{ill|Tokugawa Munetake|ja|徳川宗敬|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (later Chief of the main office), dedicating himself to the establishment of the {{ill|Association of Shinto Shrines|ja|神社本庁|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. Furthermore, when the '{{ill|Jinja Shinpo|ja|神社新報|qq=draft|qq=draft}}' was launched as part of the main office's educational public relations, he took charge of the management of {{ill|Jinja Shinpo Sha|ja|神社新報社|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, the publishing body, along with being the chief editor.
Even after his retirement in 1968 (Shōwa 43), he continued his writing activities and the guidance of future generations, being at the forefront of movements to protect the {{ill|Kokutai|ja|国体|qq=draft|qq=draft}} and {{ill|Shinto shrines|ja|神社|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. He continued to have a significant influence on the Shinto world (through the {{ill|National Council to Defend Japan|ja|日本を守る国民会議|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, now {{ill|Nippon Kaigi|ja|日本会議|qq=draft|qq=draft}}) and {{ill|Nationalist student movements|ja|民族派学生組織|qq=draft|qq=draft}} ({{ill|Japan Youth Council|ja|日本青年協議会|qq=draft|qq=draft}}), among others.
On the other hand, he questioned the enshrinement of {{ill|Class A war criminals|ja|A級戦犯|qq=draft|qq=draft}} like Tōjō, whom he had criticized during the war, due to their responsibility for the defeat.<ref>"Yasukuni: Where to Go from 'Postwar' 3—Article by Eminent Scholar of Shinto History Raises Doubts about 'Class A Enshrinement'," ''Mainichi Shimbun'' Tokyo edition morning edition: August 8, 2006</ref> (Note: {{ill|Yasukuni Shrine|ja|靖国神社|qq=draft|qq=draft}} is not a {{ill|Bekkaku Kanpeisha|ja|別表神社|qq=draft|qq=draft}} of the Association of Shinto Shrines.)
In 1992 (Heisei 4), he passed away at his home in {{ill|Kamakura|ja|鎌倉市|qq=draft|qq=draft}} at the age of 82.
{{ill|Bunzō Hashikawa|ja|橋川文三|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, who had experiences of debates with Ashizu, praised him as "an advanced polemicist among conservatives," noting that his theory of the Emperor system "should be noted as surpassing the level of traditional right-wingers." He pointed out that Ashizu tried to grasp the problem of the Emperor system from a comparative institutional perspective, based on research in European political thought history, pursuing an open form of "Kokutai theory" as his characteristic.<ref>From ''Postwar Japanese Thought Series 7: Conservative Thought,'' edited by Bunzō Hashikawa, Chikuma Shobō, 1968</ref>
{{ill|Yoshio Ōishi|ja|大石義雄|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, in ''Twenty Years of the Constitution'', praised Ashizu's ''Words of a Native'' and described Ashizu as "a modern-day Kitabatake Chikafusa."
After the war, he had friendships that transcended ideological positions with {{ill|Saburō Ichi'i|ja|市井三郎|qq=draft|qq=draft}} and {{ill|Shunsuke Tsurumi|ja|鶴見俊輔|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, who were editors of ''{{ill|Shisō no Kagaku|ja|思想の科学|qq=draft|qq=draft}}'', and the critic {{ill|Yoshihiko Takeuchi|ja|竹内好|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, and he often contributed to the same magazine.
He was also well-versed in Korean culture and had interactions with {{ill|Lyuh Woon-hyung|ja|呂運亨|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, an independence activist in Korea during the Japanese occupation. It is said that when Ashizu visited the {{ill|Korea University (Japan)|ja|朝鮮大学校|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (in Japan), he was moved upon seeing a portrait of {{ill|Kim Il-sung|ja|金日成|qq=draft|qq=draft}} in the middle of the reception room, and remarked, "This is what current Japanese people lack. In the past, the 'Imperial Portrait' of the Emperor was enshrined in every school; we need to restore that situation."<ref>{{Cite book |author=Hideki Kajimura |title=Overcoming Exclusionism: A History of Korea |publisher=Heibonsha Library |year=2014 |page=25}}</ref>
== Family ==
[[File:Iwasaki Seishichi and his friends.jpg|thumb|Nara Tarō Ōmiwa (center of back row)]]
* Father: '''{{ill|Ashizu Kojiro|ja|葦津耕次郎|qq=draft|qq=draft}}''' (1878–1940) – Shintoist.<ref name=nishiya>{{Cite journal|author=Takafumi Nishiya |title=Ashizu Kojiro: The Trajectory of a Shinto Practitioner during the 'State Shinto' Period |volume=Kyoto University Doctoral Thesis |issue=No.14958 |year=2009 |naid=500000489926 |url=https://hdl.handle.net/2433/126522}}</ref> He served at {{ill|Hakozaki Shrine|ja|筥崎宮|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, devoted himself to Shinto service, and later became active as a businessman running mining and shrine construction businesses, while also engaging as a commentator expressing views on political and social issues from the perspective of Shinto and shrines, such as the {{ill|Korean Shrine Deity Controversy|ja|朝鮮神宮御祭神論争|qq=draft|qq=draft}}.<ref>[http://kaeizyuku.com/2020/01/14/visit-graves/ January 14, 2020 'Visit to the Ashizu Family Grave'] Kaei-juku</ref>
* Grandfather: '''Ashizu Isoo''' (–1903) – Chief priest of Hakozaki Shrine.<ref name=asia>Uraube Noboru, [http://asiarestoration.com/wp-content/uploads/2016/06/daiajia1st.pdf 'Daijia wo Tsuranuita Ōmiwa Asahei'], ''Dai Ajia'' inaugural issue, June 30, 2016, Dai Ajia Kenkyūkai</ref> For a time, he also concurrently served as chief priest of the {{ill|Kashii Shrine|ja|香椎宮|qq=draft|qq=draft}}.<ref>[https://dl.ndl.go.jp/info:ndljp/pid/2947481/9 Official Gazette. June 26, 1897]</ref>
* Great-uncle: '''{{ill|Chōbei Ōmiwa|ja|大三輪長兵衛|qq=draft|qq=draft}}''' (1835–1908) – Brother of his grandfather. After working at an oil shop and rice shop in Osaka, he opened a Matsumae merchant house,<ref>[https://dl.ndl.go.jp/info:ndljp/pid/778835/97 'Chōbei Ōmiwa', ''Meiji Fugo Chifujidai'', Sumitei Inshi (Daigakukan, 1902)]</ref> and later made a fortune in cotton trade, serving as president of the 58th National Bank, chairman of the Osaka Clearing House, Osaka Prefectural Assembly chairman, and Osaka City Council chairman, among other positions. In 1898, he became a member of the {{ill|House of Representatives|ja|衆議院|qq=draft|qq=draft}}.<ref name=sangyo/><ref>[https://www.ndl.go.jp/portrait/datas/36/ 'Chōbei Ōmiwa'], Portraits of Modern Japanese Historical Figures, National Diet Library</ref><ref>[https://kotobank.jp/word/大三輪長兵衛-1061443 'Chōbei Ōmiwa'], Kotobank</ref><ref>[http://www.pref.osaka.lg.jp/attach/3059/00006976/seifuku-itiran_20200522.pdf 'List of Former Chairmen and Vice-Chairmen of Osaka Prefectural Assembly'], Osaka Prefecture, 2020</ref>
* Son: '''{{ill|Ashizu Yasukuni|ja|葦津泰國|qq=draft|qq=draft}}''' (1937–2024) – Graduate of Tokyo Metropolitan University. Former president of Jinja Shinpo Sha. Author of ''The Life of Chōbei Ōmiwa'' (Ashizu Office, 2008), among others.
== Works ==
[Full list of his publications, including major works and collected editions.]
=== Collected Works ===
* ''Ashizu Ujihiko Selected Works'' – Edited by the same Editorial Committee<ref>Each with commentary by {{ill|Mitsuo Suzuki|ja|鈴木満男|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (a disciple)</ref>, Jinja Shinpo Sha, 1996 1. ''Emperor, Shinto, Constitution'' 2. ''As Successors of the Restoration'' 3. ''Current Affairs and Character Critiques''
* ''"Let's Read the Shōwa Era": Ashizu Ujihiko's Claims Series'' – Ashizu Office, 2005–2007 1. ''Japan's Monarchy: Studies on the Emperor System'' 2. ''Eternal Restorationist'' 3. ''The End of Modern Democracy: Revival of Japanese Thought'' 4. ''Words of a Native: The Logic of Trust and Loyalty'' 5. ''Greater Asianism and Toyama Mitsuru'' 6. ''Living the Shōwa Era: The Spirit and Thoughts of the People of the Divine Land''

'''Ashizu Ujihiko''' (葦津 珍彦, July 17, 1909 – June 10, 1992) was a Japanese {{ill|Shinto|ja|神道|qq=draft|qq=draft}} scholar. He was also renowned as a commentator of the {{ill|Nationalist|ja|民族派|qq=draft|qq=draft}} movement.<ref>Yukio Hori, ''Postwar Right-Wing Forces'', Keiso Shobō, 1993, Expanded Edition.</ref>

In 1992 (Heisei 4), he passed away at his home in {{ill|Kamakura|ja|鎌倉市|qq=draft|qq=draft}} at the age of 82.
{{ill|Bunzō Hashikawa|ja|橋川文三|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, who had experiences of debates with Ashizu, praised him as "an advanced polemicist among conservatives," noting that his theory of the Emperor system "should be noted as surpassing the level of traditional right-wingers." He pointed out that Ashizu tried to grasp the problem of the Emperor system from a comparative institutional perspective, based on research in European political thought history, pursuing an open form of "Kokutai theory" as his characteristic.<ref>From ''Postwar Japanese Thought Series 7: Conservative Thought,'' edited by Bunzō Hashikawa, Chikuma Shobō, 1968</ref>
{{ill|Yoshio Ōishi|ja|大石義雄|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, in ''Twenty Years of the Constitution'', praised Ashizu's ''Words of a Native'' and described Ashizu as "a modern-day Kitabatake Chikafusa."
After the war, he had friendships that transcended ideological positions with {{ill|Saburō Ichi'i|ja|市井三郎|qq=draft|qq=draft}} and {{ill|Shunsuke Tsurumi|ja|鶴見俊輔|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, who were editors of ''{{ill|Shisō no Kagaku|ja|思想の科学|qq=draft|qq=draft}}'', and the critic {{ill|Yoshihiko Takeuchi|ja|竹内好|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, and he often contributed to the same magazine.
He was also well-versed in Korean culture and had interactions with {{ill|Lyuh Woon-hyung|ja|呂運亨|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, an independence activist in Korea during the Japanese occupation. It is said that when Ashizu visited the {{ill|Korea University (Japan)|ja|朝鮮大学校|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (in Japan), he was moved upon seeing a portrait of {{ill|Kim Il-sung|ja|金日成|qq=draft|qq=draft}} in the middle of the reception room, and remarked, "This is what current Japanese people lack. In the past, the 'Imperial Portrait' of the Emperor was enshrined in every school; we need to restore that situation."<ref>{{Cite book |author=Hideki Kajimura |title=Overcoming Exclusionism: A History of Korea |publisher=Heibonsha Library |year=2014 |page=25}}</ref>
== Family ==
[[File:Iwasaki Seishichi and his friends.jpg|thumb|Nara Tarō Ōmiwa (center of back row)]]
* Father: '''{{ill|Ashizu Kojiro|ja|葦津耕次郎|qq=draft|qq=draft}}''' (1878–1940) – Shintoist.<ref name=nishiya>{{Cite journal|author=Takafumi Nishiya |title=Ashizu Kojiro: The Trajectory of a Shinto Practitioner during the 'State Shinto' Period |volume=Kyoto University Doctoral Thesis |issue=No.14958 |year=2009 |naid=500000489926 |url=https://hdl.handle.net/2433/126522}}</ref> He served at {{ill|Hakozaki Shrine|ja|筥崎宮|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, devoted himself to Shinto service, and later became active as a businessman running mining and shrine construction businesses, while also engaging as a commentator expressing views on political and social issues from the perspective of Shinto and shrines, such as the {{ill|Korean Shrine Deity Controversy|ja|朝鮮神宮御祭神論争|qq=draft|qq=draft}}.<ref>[http://kaeizyuku.com/2020/01/14/visit-graves/ January 14, 2020 'Visit to the Ashizu Family Grave'] Kaei-juku</ref>
* Grandfather: '''Ashizu Isoo''' (–1903) – Chief priest of Hakozaki Shrine.<ref name=asia>Uraube Noboru, [http://asiarestoration.com/wp-content/uploads/2016/06/daiajia1st.pdf 'Daijia wo Tsuranuita Ōmiwa Asahei'], ''Dai Ajia'' inaugural issue, June 30, 2016, Dai Ajia Kenkyūkai</ref> For a time, he also concurrently served as chief priest of the {{ill|Kashii Shrine|ja|香椎宮|qq=draft|qq=draft}}.<ref>[https://dl.ndl.go.jp/info:ndljp/pid/2947481/9 Official Gazette. June 26, 1897]</ref>
* Great-uncle: '''{{ill|Chōbei Ōmiwa|ja|大三輪長兵衛|qq=draft|qq=draft}}''' (1835–1908) – Brother of his grandfather. After working at an oil shop and rice shop in Osaka, he opened a Matsumae merchant house,<ref>[https://dl.ndl.go.jp/info:ndljp/pid/778835/97 'Chōbei Ōmiwa', ''Meiji Fugo Chifujidai'', Sumitei Inshi (Daigakukan, 1902)]</ref> and later made a fortune in cotton trade, serving as president of the 58th National Bank, chairman of the Osaka Clearing House, Osaka Prefectural Assembly chairman, and Osaka City Council chairman, among other positions. In 1898, he became a member of the {{ill|House of Representatives|ja|衆議院|qq=draft|qq=draft}}.<ref name=sangyo/><ref>[https://www.ndl.go.jp/portrait/datas/36/ 'Chōbei Ōmiwa'], Portraits of Modern Japanese Historical Figures, National Diet Library</ref><ref>[https://kotobank.jp/word/大三輪長兵衛-1061443 'Chōbei Ōmiwa'], Kotobank</ref><ref>[http://www.pref.osaka.lg.jp/attach/3059/00006976/seifuku-itiran_20200522.pdf 'List of Former Chairmen and Vice-Chairmen of Osaka Prefectural Assembly'], Osaka Prefecture, 2020</ref>
* Son: '''{{ill|Ashizu Yasukuni|ja|葦津泰國|qq=draft|qq=draft}}''' (1937–2024) – Graduate of Tokyo Metropolitan University. Former president of Jinja Shinpo Sha. Author of ''The Life of Chōbei Ōmiwa'' (Ashizu Office, 2008), among others.
== Works ==
[Full list of his publications, including major works and collected editions.]
=== Collected Works ===
* ''Ashizu Ujihiko Selected Works'' – Edited by the same Editorial Committee<ref>Each with commentary by {{ill|Mitsuo Suzuki|ja|鈴木満男|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (a disciple)</ref>, Jinja Shinpo Sha, 1996 1. ''Emperor, Shinto, Constitution'' 2. ''As Successors of the Restoration'' 3. ''Current Affairs and Character Critiques''
* ''"Let's Read the Shōwa Era": Ashizu Ujihiko's Claims Series'' – Ashizu Office, 2005–2007 1. ''Japan's Monarchy: Studies on the Emperor System'' 2. ''Eternal Restorationist'' 3. ''The End of Modern Democracy: Revival of Japanese Thought'' 4. ''Words of a Native: The Logic of Trust and Loyalty'' 5. ''Greater Asianism and Toyama Mitsuru'' 6. ''Living the Shōwa Era: The Spirit and Thoughts of the People of the Divine Land''
== Footnotes ==

== References ==

revision.comment: Text replacement - "|別表神社|" to "|別表神社|1=Beppyo Shrine|2=ja|3=別表神社|4=simple|5=Beppyo shrine|6=zh|7=別表神社|8=mr|9=बेप्प्यो तीर्थ|"
revision.text:
'''Ashizu Ujihiko''' (葦津 珍彦, July 17, 1909 – June 10, 1992) was a Japanese {{ill|Shinto|ja|神道|qq=draft|qq=draft}} scholar. He was also renowned as a commentator of the {{ill|Nationalist|ja|民族派|qq=draft|qq=draft}} movement.<ref>Yukio Hori, ''Postwar Right-Wing Forces'', Keiso Shobō, 1993, Expanded Edition.</ref>
== Career ==
He was born as the eldest son of {{ill|Ashizu Kojiro|ja|葦津耕次郎|qq=draft|qq=draft}} in Hakozaki, Fukuoka Prefecture (now Hakozaki, {{ill|Higashi-ku, Fukuoka|ja|福岡市東区|qq=draft|qq=draft}}). The Ashizu family is a descendant of the Ogami family, one of the shrine families of {{ill|Hakozaki Shrine|ja|筥崎宮|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, and his uncle, {{ill|Ashizu Seizō|ja|葦津洗造|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, was the chief priest of the same shrine.<ref name="#1">Foreword by {{ill|Ashizu Yasukuni|ja|葦津泰國|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, from ''Living the Shōwa Era: The Claims of Ujihiko Ashizu Series IV''</ref>
In 1922 (Taishō 11), he entered {{ill|Tokyo Metropolitan Koishikawa High School|ja|東京都立小石川高等学校|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (then Tokyo Prefectural Fifth Junior High School). Around this time, he yearned for socialism, participated in the Social Science Study Group, and avidly read related books.<ref name="#1"/>
Those around him were concerned, and after graduation, he went on to {{ill|Kokugakuin University|ja|國學院大學|qq=draft|qq=draft}}.{{Citation needed|date=July 5, 2017}} He also enrolled in the {{ill|Tokyo School of Foreign Languages|ja|東京外国語学校 (旧制)|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, but dropped out of both.{{Citation needed|date=July 5, 2017}} In 1928 (Shōwa 3), he entered {{ill|Fukushima University|ja|福島大学|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (then Fukushima Higher Commercial School), but dropped out the following year.
In 1931, at the recommendation of a friend who belonged to the {{ill|Japanese Communist Party|ja|日本共産党|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, he wrote a leading article for the newspaper {{ill|Akahata|ja|しんぶん赤旗|qq=draft|qq=draft}}.<ref name=ashidu>{{Cite journal|author=Konno Nobuyuki|title=The Thought of Ashizu Ujihiko during and after the War—Focusing on His View of Shinto|journal=Interdisciplinary Research on 'Narratives' in Japan from 1890 to the 1950s|page=63|year=2012}}</ref> However, in February of the following year, under the influence of his father, he is said to have converted to traditional conservative imperial {{ill|Shinto|ja|神道|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, which aimed for the realization of "Japan, the Emperor's Nation with Unity of Rites and Government," like his father.<ref name=ashidu />
In 1932 (Shōwa 7), he succeeded his father's business and engaged in the construction of shrines and temples as the representative of a shrine and temple construction office.<ref>"Fifty Years of the Association of Shinto Shrines" (Non-commercial publication), May 1996, Publisher: {{ill|Association of Shinto Shrines|ja|神社本庁|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, p.74—Obituary of Ashizu Ujihiko, associate of {{ill|Jinja Shinpo Sha|ja|神社新報社|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (June 10)</ref> During this period, he interacted with {{ill|Toyama Mitsuru|ja|頭山満|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Teisuke Imaizumi|ja|今泉定助|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Takayama Noboru|ja|高山昇|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Ogata Taketora|ja|緒方竹虎|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Ryōhei Uchida|ja|内田良平|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Inoue Tadamaro|ja|井上孚麿|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, and others. Before the outbreak of the {{ill|Pacific War|ja|太平洋戦争|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, he opposed the war, viewing it as an "unwinnable battle," and after the outbreak, he engaged in unique political activities, such as insisting on adhering only to the war objectives shown in the Imperial Rescript on the outbreak of war, which was "to secure the survival of an independent nation as unavoidable."{{Citation needed|date=July 5, 2017}}
During the war, some of his critical articles on {{ill|Nazi Germany|ja|ナチス・ドイツ|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, Japan's ally, and the policies of the {{ill|Hideki Tōjō|ja|東條英機|qq=draft|qq=draft}} Cabinet were banned, and he was arrested at times.{{Citation needed|date=July 5, 2017}}
Immediately after the war, he dissolved his company and assisted {{ill|Shigeru Yoshida (Home Ministry official)|ja|吉田茂 (内務官僚)|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Munenori Miyagawa|ja|宮川宗徳|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (later Secretary-General of the main office), and {{ill|Tokugawa Munetake|ja|徳川宗敬|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (later Chief of the main office), dedicating himself to the establishment of the {{ill|Association of Shinto Shrines|ja|神社本庁|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. Furthermore, when the '{{ill|Jinja Shinpo|ja|神社新報|qq=draft|qq=draft}}' was launched as part of the main office's educational public relations, he took charge of the management of {{ill|Jinja Shinpo Sha|ja|神社新報社|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, the publishing body, along with being the chief editor.
Even after his retirement in 1968 (Shōwa 43), he continued his writing activities and the guidance of future generations, being at the forefront of movements to protect the {{ill|Kokutai|ja|国体|qq=draft|qq=draft}} and {{ill|Shinto shrines|ja|神社|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. He continued to have a significant influence on the Shinto world (through the {{ill|National Council to Defend Japan|ja|日本を守る国民会議|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, now {{ill|Nippon Kaigi|ja|日本会議|qq=draft|qq=draft}}) and {{ill|Nationalist student movements|ja|民族派学生組織|qq=draft|qq=draft}} ({{ill|Japan Youth Council|ja|日本青年協議会|qq=draft|qq=draft}}), among others.
On the other hand, he questioned the enshrinement of {{ill|Class A war criminals|ja|A級戦犯|qq=draft|qq=draft}} like Tōjō, whom he had criticized during the war, due to their responsibility for the defeat.<ref>"Yasukuni: Where to Go from 'Postwar' 3—Article by Eminent Scholar of Shinto History Raises Doubts about 'Class A Enshrinement'," ''Mainichi Shimbun'' Tokyo edition morning edition: August 8, 2006</ref> (Note: {{ill|Yasukuni Shrine|ja|靖国神社|qq=draft|qq=draft}} is not a {{ill|Bekkaku Kanpeisha|ja|別表神社|1=Beppyo Shrine|2=ja|3=別表神社|4=simple|5=Beppyo shrine|6=zh|7=別表神社|8=mr|9=बेप्प्यो तीर्थ|qq=draft|qq=draft}} of the Association of Shinto Shrines.)
In 1992 (Heisei 4), he passed away at his home in {{ill|Kamakura|ja|鎌倉市|qq=draft|qq=draft}} at the age of 82.
{{ill|Bunzō Hashikawa|ja|橋川文三|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, who had experiences of debates with Ashizu, praised him as "an advanced polemicist among conservatives," noting that his theory of the Emperor system "should be noted as surpassing the level of traditional right-wingers." He pointed out that Ashizu tried to grasp the problem of the Emperor system from a comparative institutional perspective, based on research in European political thought history, pursuing an open form of "Kokutai theory" as his characteristic.<ref>From ''Postwar Japanese Thought Series 7: Conservative Thought,'' edited by Bunzō Hashikawa, Chikuma Shobō, 1968</ref>
{{ill|Yoshio Ōishi|ja|大石義雄|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, in ''Twenty Years of the Constitution'', praised Ashizu's ''Words of a Native'' and described Ashizu as "a modern-day Kitabatake Chikafusa."
After the war, he had friendships that transcended ideological positions with {{ill|Saburō Ichi'i|ja|市井三郎|qq=draft|qq=draft}} and {{ill|Shunsuke Tsurumi|ja|鶴見俊輔|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, who were editors of ''{{ill|Shisō no Kagaku|ja|思想の科学|qq=draft|qq=draft}}'', and the critic {{ill|Yoshihiko Takeuchi|ja|竹内好|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, and he often contributed to the same magazine.
He was also well-versed in Korean culture and had interactions with {{ill|Lyuh Woon-hyung|ja|呂運亨|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, an independence activist in Korea during the Japanese occupation. It is said that when Ashizu visited the {{ill|Korea University (Japan)|ja|朝鮮大学校|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (in Japan), he was moved upon seeing a portrait of {{ill|Kim Il-sung|ja|金日成|qq=draft|qq=draft}} in the middle of the reception room, and remarked, "This is what current Japanese people lack. In the past, the 'Imperial Portrait' of the Emperor was enshrined in every school; we need to restore that situation."<ref>{{Cite book |author=Hideki Kajimura |title=Overcoming Exclusionism: A History of Korea |publisher=Heibonsha Library |year=2014 |page=25}}</ref>
== Family ==
[[File:Iwasaki Seishichi and his friends.jpg|thumb|Nara Tarō Ōmiwa (center of back row)]]
* Father: '''{{ill|Ashizu Kojiro|ja|葦津耕次郎|qq=draft|qq=draft}}''' (1878–1940) – Shintoist.<ref name=nishiya>{{Cite journal|author=Takafumi Nishiya |title=Ashizu Kojiro: The Trajectory of a Shinto Practitioner during the 'State Shinto' Period |volume=Kyoto University Doctoral Thesis |issue=No.14958 |year=2009 |naid=500000489926 |url=https://hdl.handle.net/2433/126522}}</ref> He served at {{ill|Hakozaki Shrine|ja|筥崎宮|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, devoted himself to Shinto service, and later became active as a businessman running mining and shrine construction businesses, while also engaging as a commentator expressing views on political and social issues from the perspective of Shinto and shrines, such as the {{ill|Korean Shrine Deity Controversy|ja|朝鮮神宮御祭神論争|qq=draft|qq=draft}}.<ref>[http://kaeizyuku.com/2020/01/14/visit-graves/ January 14, 2020 'Visit to the Ashizu Family Grave'] Kaei-juku</ref>
* Grandfather: '''Ashizu Isoo''' (–1903) – Chief priest of Hakozaki Shrine.<ref name=asia>Uraube Noboru, [http://asiarestoration.com/wp-content/uploads/2016/06/daiajia1st.pdf 'Daijia wo Tsuranuita Ōmiwa Asahei'], ''Dai Ajia'' inaugural issue, June 30, 2016, Dai Ajia Kenkyūkai</ref> For a time, he also concurrently served as chief priest of the {{ill|Kashii Shrine|ja|香椎宮|qq=draft|qq=draft}}.<ref>[https://dl.ndl.go.jp/info:ndljp/pid/2947481/9 Official Gazette. June 26, 1897]</ref>
* Great-uncle: '''{{ill|Chōbei Ōmiwa|ja|大三輪長兵衛|qq=draft|qq=draft}}''' (1835–1908) – Brother of his grandfather. After working at an oil shop and rice shop in Osaka, he opened a Matsumae merchant house,<ref>[https://dl.ndl.go.jp/info:ndljp/pid/778835/97 'Chōbei Ōmiwa', ''Meiji Fugo Chifujidai'', Sumitei Inshi (Daigakukan, 1902)]</ref> and later made a fortune in cotton trade, serving as president of the 58th National Bank, chairman of the Osaka Clearing House, Osaka Prefectural Assembly chairman, and Osaka City Council chairman, among other positions. In 1898, he became a member of the {{ill|House of Representatives|ja|衆議院|qq=draft|qq=draft}}.<ref name=sangyo/><ref>[https://www.ndl.go.jp/portrait/datas/36/ 'Chōbei Ōmiwa'], Portraits of Modern Japanese Historical Figures, National Diet Library</ref><ref>[https://kotobank.jp/word/大三輪長兵衛-1061443 'Chōbei Ōmiwa'], Kotobank</ref><ref>[http://www.pref.osaka.lg.jp/attach/3059/00006976/seifuku-itiran_20200522.pdf 'List of Former Chairmen and Vice-Chairmen of Osaka Prefectural Assembly'], Osaka Prefecture, 2020</ref>
* Son: '''{{ill|Ashizu Yasukuni|ja|葦津泰國|qq=draft|qq=draft}}''' (1937–2024) – Graduate of Tokyo Metropolitan University. Former president of Jinja Shinpo Sha. Author of ''The Life of Chōbei Ōmiwa'' (Ashizu Office, 2008), among others.
== Works ==
[Full list of his publications, including major works and collected editions.]
=== Collected Works ===
* ''Ashizu Ujihiko Selected Works'' – Edited by the same Editorial Committee<ref>Each with commentary by {{ill|Mitsuo Suzuki|ja|鈴木満男|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (a disciple)</ref>, Jinja Shinpo Sha, 1996 1. ''Emperor, Shinto, Constitution'' 2. ''As Successors of the Restoration'' 3. ''Current Affairs and Character Critiques''
* ''"Let's Read the Shōwa Era": Ashizu Ujihiko's Claims Series'' – Ashizu Office, 2005–2007 1. ''Japan's Monarchy: Studies on the Emperor System'' 2. ''Eternal Restorationist'' 3. ''The End of Modern Democracy: Revival of Japanese Thought'' 4. ''Words of a Native: The Logic of Trust and Loyalty'' 5. ''Greater Asianism and Toyama Mitsuru'' 6. ''Living the Shōwa Era: The Spirit and Thoughts of the People of the Divine Land''

'''Ashizu Ujihiko''' (葦津 珍彦, July 17, 1909 – June 10, 1992) was a Japanese {{ill|Shinto|ja|神道|qq=draft|qq=draft}} scholar. He was also renowned as a commentator of the {{ill|Nationalist|ja|民族派|qq=draft|qq=draft}} movement.<ref>Yukio Hori, ''Postwar Right-Wing Forces'', Keiso Shobō, 1993, Expanded Edition.</ref>
== Career ==
He was born as the eldest son of {{ill|Ashizu Kojiro|ja|葦津耕次郎|qq=draft|qq=draft}} in Hakozaki, Fukuoka Prefecture (now Hakozaki, {{ill|Higashi-ku, Fukuoka|ja|福岡市東区|qq=draft|qq=draft}}). The Ashizu family is a descendant of the Ogami family, one of the shrine families of {{ill|Hakozaki Shrine|ja|筥崎宮|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, and his uncle, {{ill|Ashizu Seizō|ja|葦津洗造|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, was the chief priest of the same shrine.<ref name="#1">Foreword by {{ill|Ashizu Yasukuni|ja|葦津泰國|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, from ''Living the Shōwa Era: The Claims of Ujihiko Ashizu Series IV''</ref>
In 1922 (Taishō 11), he entered {{ill|Tokyo Metropolitan Koishikawa High School|ja|東京都立小石川高等学校|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (then Tokyo Prefectural Fifth Junior High School). Around this time, he yearned for socialism, participated in the Social Science Study Group, and avidly read related books.<ref name="#1"/>
Those around him were concerned, and after graduation, he went on to {{ill|Kokugakuin University|ja|國學院大學|qq=draft|qq=draft}}.{{Citation needed|date=July 5, 2017}} He also enrolled in the {{ill|Tokyo School of Foreign Languages|ja|東京外国語学校 (旧制)|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, but dropped out of both.{{Citation needed|date=July 5, 2017}} In 1928 (Shōwa 3), he entered {{ill|Fukushima University|ja|福島大学|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (then Fukushima Higher Commercial School), but dropped out the following year.
In 1931, at the recommendation of a friend who belonged to the {{ill|Japanese Communist Party|ja|日本共産党|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, he wrote a leading article for the newspaper {{ill|Akahata|ja|しんぶん赤旗|qq=draft|qq=draft}}.<ref name=ashidu>{{Cite journal|author=Konno Nobuyuki|title=The Thought of Ashizu Ujihiko during and after the War—Focusing on His View of Shinto|journal=Interdisciplinary Research on 'Narratives' in Japan from 1890 to the 1950s|page=63|year=2012}}</ref> However, in February of the following year, under the influence of his father, he is said to have converted to traditional conservative imperial {{ill|Shinto|ja|神道|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, which aimed for the realization of "Japan, the Emperor's Nation with Unity of Rites and Government," like his father.<ref name=ashidu />
In 1932 (Shōwa 7), he succeeded his father's business and engaged in the construction of shrines and temples as the representative of a shrine and temple construction office.<ref>"Fifty Years of the Association of Shinto Shrines" (Non-commercial publication), May 1996, Publisher: {{ill|Association of Shinto Shrines|ja|神社本庁|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, p.74—Obituary of Ashizu Ujihiko, associate of {{ill|Jinja Shinpo Sha|ja|神社新報社|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (June 10)</ref> During this period, he interacted with {{ill|Toyama Mitsuru|ja|頭山満|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Teisuke Imaizumi|ja|今泉定助|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Takayama Noboru|ja|高山昇|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Ogata Taketora|ja|緒方竹虎|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Ryōhei Uchida|ja|内田良平|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Inoue Tadamaro|ja|井上孚麿|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, and others. Before the outbreak of the {{ill|Pacific War|ja|太平洋戦争|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, he opposed the war, viewing it as an "unwinnable battle," and after the outbreak, he engaged in unique political activities, such as insisting on adhering only to the war objectives shown in the Imperial Rescript on the outbreak of war, which was "to secure the survival of an independent nation as unavoidable."{{Citation needed|date=July 5, 2017}}
During the war, some of his critical articles on {{ill|Nazi Germany|ja|ナチス・ドイツ|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, Japan's ally, and the policies of the {{ill|Hideki Tōjō|ja|東條英機|qq=draft|qq=draft}} Cabinet were banned, and he was arrested at times.{{Citation needed|date=July 5, 2017}}
Immediately after the war, he dissolved his company and assisted {{ill|Shigeru Yoshida (Home Ministry official)|ja|吉田茂 (内務官僚)|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Munenori Miyagawa|ja|宮川宗徳|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (later Secretary-General of the main office), and {{ill|Tokugawa Munetake|ja|徳川宗敬|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (later Chief of the main office), dedicating himself to the establishment of the {{ill|Association of Shinto Shrines|ja|神社本庁|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. Furthermore, when the '{{ill|Jinja Shinpo|ja|神社新報|qq=draft|qq=draft}}' was launched as part of the main office's educational public relations, he took charge of the management of {{ill|Jinja Shinpo Sha|ja|神社新報社|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, the publishing body, along with being the chief editor.
Even after his retirement in 1968 (Shōwa 43), he continued his writing activities and the guidance of future generations, being at the forefront of movements to protect the {{ill|Kokutai|ja|国体|qq=draft|qq=draft}} and {{ill|Shinto shrines|ja|神社|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. He continued to have a significant influence on the Shinto world (through the {{ill|National Council to Defend Japan|ja|日本を守る国民会議|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, now {{ill|Nippon Kaigi|ja|日本会議|qq=draft|qq=draft}}) and {{ill|Nationalist student movements|ja|民族派学生組織|qq=draft|qq=draft}} ({{ill|Japan Youth Council|ja|日本青年協議会|qq=draft|qq=draft}}), among others.
On the other hand, he questioned the enshrinement of {{ill|Class A war criminals|ja|A級戦犯|qq=draft|qq=draft}} like Tōjō, whom he had criticized during the war, due to their responsibility for the defeat.<ref>"Yasukuni: Where to Go from 'Postwar' 3—Article by Eminent Scholar of Shinto History Raises Doubts about 'Class A Enshrinement'," ''Mainichi Shimbun'' Tokyo edition morning edition: August 8, 2006</ref> (Note: {{ill|Yasukuni Shrine|ja|靖国神社|qq=draft|qq=draft}} is not a {{ill|Bekkaku Kanpeisha|ja|別表神社|1=Beppyo Shrine|2=ja|3=別表神社|4=simple|5=Beppyo shrine|6=zh|7=別表神社|8=mr|9=बेप्प्यो तीर्थ|qq=draft|qq=draft}} of the Association of Shinto Shrines.)
In 1992 (Heisei 4), he passed away at his home in {{ill|Kamakura|ja|鎌倉市|qq=draft|qq=draft}} at the age of 82.
{{ill|Bunzō Hashikawa|ja|橋川文三|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, who had experiences of debates with Ashizu, praised him as "an advanced polemicist among conservatives," noting that his theory of the Emperor system "should be noted as surpassing the level of traditional right-wingers." He pointed out that Ashizu tried to grasp the problem of the Emperor system from a comparative institutional perspective, based on research in European political thought history, pursuing an open form of "Kokutai theory" as his characteristic.<ref>From ''Postwar Japanese Thought Series 7: Conservative Thought,'' edited by Bunzō Hashikawa, Chikuma Shobō, 1968</ref>
{{ill|Yoshio Ōishi|ja|大石義雄|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, in ''Twenty Years of the Constitution'', praised Ashizu's ''Words of a Native'' and described Ashizu as "a modern-day Kitabatake Chikafusa."
After the war, he had friendships that transcended ideological positions with {{ill|Saburō Ichi'i|ja|市井三郎|qq=draft|qq=draft}} and {{ill|Shunsuke Tsurumi|ja|鶴見俊輔|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, who were editors of ''{{ill|Shisō no Kagaku|ja|思想の科学|qq=draft|qq=draft}}'', and the critic {{ill|Yoshihiko Takeuchi|ja|竹内好|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, and he often contributed to the same magazine.
He was also well-versed in Korean culture and had interactions with {{ill|Lyuh Woon-hyung|ja|呂運亨|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, an independence activist in Korea during the Japanese occupation. It is said that when Ashizu visited the {{ill|Korea University (Japan)|ja|朝鮮大学校|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (in Japan), he was moved upon seeing a portrait of {{ill|Kim Il-sung|ja|金日成|qq=draft|qq=draft}} in the middle of the reception room, and remarked, "This is what current Japanese people lack. In the past, the 'Imperial Portrait' of the Emperor was enshrined in every school; we need to restore that situation."<ref>{{Cite book |author=Hideki Kajimura |title=Overcoming Exclusionism: A History of Korea |publisher=Heibonsha Library |year=2014 |page=25}}</ref>
== Family ==
[[File:Iwasaki Seishichi and his friends.jpg|thumb|Nara Tarō Ōmiwa (center of back row)]]
* Father: '''{{ill|Ashizu Kojiro|ja|葦津耕次郎|qq=draft|qq=draft}}''' (1878–1940) – Shintoist.<ref name=nishiya>{{Cite journal|author=Takafumi Nishiya |title=Ashizu Kojiro: The Trajectory of a Shinto Practitioner during the 'State Shinto' Period |volume=Kyoto University Doctoral Thesis |issue=No.14958 |year=2009 |naid=500000489926 |url=https://hdl.handle.net/2433/126522}}</ref> He served at {{ill|Hakozaki Shrine|ja|筥崎宮|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, devoted himself to Shinto service, and later became active as a businessman running mining and shrine construction businesses, while also engaging as a commentator expressing views on political and social issues from the perspective of Shinto and shrines, such as the {{ill|Korean Shrine Deity Controversy|ja|朝鮮神宮御祭神論争|qq=draft|qq=draft}}.<ref>[http://kaeizyuku.com/2020/01/14/visit-graves/ January 14, 2020 'Visit to the Ashizu Family Grave'] Kaei-juku</ref>
* Grandfather: '''Ashizu Isoo''' (–1903) – Chief priest of Hakozaki Shrine.<ref name=asia>Uraube Noboru, [http://asiarestoration.com/wp-content/uploads/2016/06/daiajia1st.pdf 'Daijia wo Tsuranuita Ōmiwa Asahei'], ''Dai Ajia'' inaugural issue, June 30, 2016, Dai Ajia Kenkyūkai</ref> For a time, he also concurrently served as chief priest of the {{ill|Kashii Shrine|ja|香椎宮|qq=draft|qq=draft}}.<ref>[https://dl.ndl.go.jp/info:ndljp/pid/2947481/9 Official Gazette. June 26, 1897]</ref>
* Great-uncle: '''{{ill|Chōbei Ōmiwa|ja|大三輪長兵衛|qq=draft|qq=draft}}''' (1835–1908) – Brother of his grandfather. After working at an oil shop and rice shop in Osaka, he opened a Matsumae merchant house,<ref>[https://dl.ndl.go.jp/info:ndljp/pid/778835/97 'Chōbei Ōmiwa', ''Meiji Fugo Chifujidai'', Sumitei Inshi (Daigakukan, 1902)]</ref> and later made a fortune in cotton trade, serving as president of the 58th National Bank, chairman of the Osaka Clearing House, Osaka Prefectural Assembly chairman, and Osaka City Council chairman, among other positions. In 1898, he became a member of the {{ill|House of Representatives|ja|衆議院|qq=draft|qq=draft}}.<ref name=sangyo/><ref>[https://www.ndl.go.jp/portrait/datas/36/ 'Chōbei Ōmiwa'], Portraits of Modern Japanese Historical Figures, National Diet Library</ref><ref>[https://kotobank.jp/word/大三輪長兵衛-1061443 'Chōbei Ōmiwa'], Kotobank</ref><ref>[http://www.pref.osaka.lg.jp/attach/3059/00006976/seifuku-itiran_20200522.pdf 'List of Former Chairmen and Vice-Chairmen of Osaka Prefectural Assembly'], Osaka Prefecture, 2020</ref>
* Son: '''{{ill|Ashizu Yasukuni|ja|葦津泰國|qq=draft|qq=draft}}''' (1937–2024) – Graduate of Tokyo Metropolitan University. Former president of Jinja Shinpo Sha. Author of ''The Life of Chōbei Ōmiwa'' (Ashizu Office, 2008), among others.

'''Ashizu Ujihiko''' (葦津 珍彦, July 17, 1909 – June 10, 1992) was a Japanese {{ill|Shinto|ja|神道|qq=draft|qq=draft}} scholar. He was also renowned as a commentator of the {{ill|Nationalist|ja|民族派|qq=draft|qq=draft}} movement.<ref>Yukio Hori, ''Postwar Right-Wing Forces'', Keiso Shobō, 1993, Expanded Edition.</ref>
== Career ==
He was born as the eldest son of {{ill|Ashizu Kojiro|ja|葦津耕次郎|qq=draft|qq=draft}} in Hakozaki, Fukuoka Prefecture (now Hakozaki, {{ill|Higashi-ku, Fukuoka|ja|福岡市東区|qq=draft|qq=draft}}). The Ashizu family is a descendant of the Ogami family, one of the shrine families of {{ill|Hakozaki Shrine|ja|筥崎宮|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, and his uncle, {{ill|Ashizu Seizō|ja|葦津洗造|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, was the chief priest of the same shrine.<ref name="#1">Foreword by {{ill|Ashizu Yasukuni|ja|葦津泰國|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, from ''Living the Shōwa Era: The Claims of Ujihiko Ashizu Series IV''</ref>
In 1922 (Taishō 11), he entered {{ill|Tokyo Metropolitan Koishikawa High School|ja|東京都立小石川高等学校|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (then Tokyo Prefectural Fifth Junior High School). Around this time, he yearned for socialism, participated in the Social Science Study Group, and avidly read related books.<ref name="#1"/>
Those around him were concerned, and after graduation, he went on to {{ill|Kokugakuin University|ja|國學院大學|qq=draft|qq=draft}}.{{Citation needed|date=July 5, 2017}} He also enrolled in the {{ill|Tokyo School of Foreign Languages|ja|東京外国語学校 (旧制)|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, but dropped out of both.{{Citation needed|date=July 5, 2017}} In 1928 (Shōwa 3), he entered {{ill|Fukushima University|ja|福島大学|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (then Fukushima Higher Commercial School), but dropped out the following year.
In 1931, at the recommendation of a friend who belonged to the {{ill|Japanese Communist Party|ja|日本共産党|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, he wrote a leading article for the newspaper {{ill|Akahata|ja|しんぶん赤旗|qq=draft|qq=draft}}.<ref name=ashidu>{{Cite journal|author=Konno Nobuyuki|title=The Thought of Ashizu Ujihiko during and after the War—Focusing on His View of Shinto|journal=Interdisciplinary Research on 'Narratives' in Japan from 1890 to the 1950s|page=63|year=2012}}</ref> However, in February of the following year, under the influence of his father, he is said to have converted to traditional conservative imperial {{ill|Shinto|ja|神道|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, which aimed for the realization of "Japan, the Emperor's Nation with Unity of Rites and Government," like his father.<ref name=ashidu />
In 1932 (Shōwa 7), he succeeded his father's business and engaged in the construction of shrines and temples as the representative of a shrine and temple construction office.<ref>"Fifty Years of the Association of Shinto Shrines" (Non-commercial publication), May 1996, Publisher: {{ill|Association of Shinto Shrines|ja|神社本庁|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, p.74—Obituary of Ashizu Ujihiko, associate of {{ill|Jinja Shinpo Sha|ja|神社新報社|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (June 10)</ref> During this period, he interacted with {{ill|Toyama Mitsuru|ja|頭山満|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Teisuke Imaizumi|ja|今泉定助|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Takayama Noboru|ja|高山昇|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Ogata Taketora|ja|緒方竹虎|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Ryōhei Uchida|ja|内田良平|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Inoue Tadamaro|ja|井上孚麿|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, and others. Before the outbreak of the {{ill|Pacific War|ja|太平洋戦争|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, he opposed the war, viewing it as an "unwinnable battle," and after the outbreak, he engaged in unique political activities, such as insisting on adhering only to the war objectives shown in the Imperial Rescript on the outbreak of war, which was "to secure the survival of an independent nation as unavoidable."{{Citation needed|date=July 5, 2017}}
During the war, some of his critical articles on {{ill|Nazi Germany|ja|ナチス・ドイツ|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, Japan's ally, and the policies of the {{ill|Hideki Tōjō|ja|東條英機|qq=draft|qq=draft}} Cabinet were banned, and he was arrested at times.{{Citation needed|date=July 5, 2017}}
Immediately after the war, he dissolved his company and assisted {{ill|Shigeru Yoshida (Home Ministry official)|ja|吉田茂 (内務官僚)|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Munenori Miyagawa|ja|宮川宗徳|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (later Secretary-General of the main office), and {{ill|Tokugawa Munetake|ja|徳川宗敬|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (later Chief of the main office), dedicating himself to the establishment of the {{ill|Association of Shinto Shrines|ja|神社本庁|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. Furthermore, when the '{{ill|Jinja Shinpo|ja|神社新報|qq=draft|qq=draft}}' was launched as part of the main office's educational public relations, he took charge of the management of {{ill|Jinja Shinpo Sha|ja|神社新報社|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, the publishing body, along with being the chief editor.
Even after his retirement in 1968 (Shōwa 43), he continued his writing activities and the guidance of future generations, being at the forefront of movements to protect the {{ill|Kokutai|ja|国体|qq=draft|qq=draft}} and {{ill|Shinto shrines|ja|神社|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. He continued to have a significant influence on the Shinto world (through the {{ill|National Council to Defend Japan|ja|日本を守る国民会議|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, now {{ill|Nippon Kaigi|ja|日本会議|qq=draft|qq=draft}}) and {{ill|Nationalist student movements|ja|民族派学生組織|qq=draft|qq=draft}} ({{ill|Japan Youth Council|ja|日本青年協議会|qq=draft|qq=draft}}), among others.
On the other hand, he questioned the enshrinement of {{ill|Class A war criminals|ja|A級戦犯|qq=draft|qq=draft}} like Tōjō, whom he had criticized during the war, due to their responsibility for the defeat.<ref>"Yasukuni: Where to Go from 'Postwar' 3—Article by Eminent Scholar of Shinto History Raises Doubts about 'Class A Enshrinement'," ''Mainichi Shimbun'' Tokyo edition morning edition: August 8, 2006</ref> (Note: {{ill|Yasukuni Shrine|ja|靖国神社|qq=draft|qq=draft}} is not a {{ill|Bekkaku Kanpeisha|ja|別表神社|1=Beppyo Shrine|2=ja|3=別表神社|4=simple|5=Beppyo shrine|6=zh|7=別表神社|8=mr|9=बेप्प्यो तीर्थ|qq=draft|qq=draft}} of the Association of Shinto Shrines.)
In 1992 (Heisei 4), he passed away at his home in {{ill|Kamakura|ja|鎌倉市|qq=draft|qq=draft}} at the age of 82.
{{ill|Bunzō Hashikawa|ja|橋川文三|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, who had experiences of debates with Ashizu, praised him as "an advanced polemicist among conservatives," noting that his theory of the Emperor system "should be noted as surpassing the level of traditional right-wingers." He pointed out that Ashizu tried to grasp the problem of the Emperor system from a comparative institutional perspective, based on research in European political thought history, pursuing an open form of "Kokutai theory" as his characteristic.<ref>From ''Postwar Japanese Thought Series 7: Conservative Thought,'' edited by Bunzō Hashikawa, Chikuma Shobō, 1968</ref>
{{ill|Yoshio Ōishi|ja|大石義雄|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, in ''Twenty Years of the Constitution'', praised Ashizu's ''Words of a Native'' and described Ashizu as "a modern-day Kitabatake Chikafusa."

He was also well-versed in Korean culture and had interactions with {{ill|Lyuh Woon-hyung|ja|呂運亨|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, an independence activist in Korea during the Japanese occupation. It is said that when Ashizu visited the {{ill|Korea University (Japan)|ja|朝鮮大学校|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (in Japan), he was moved upon seeing a portrait of {{ill|Kim Il-sung|ja|金日成|qq=draft|qq=draft}} in the middle of the reception room, and remarked, "This is what current Japanese people lack. In the past, the 'Imperial Portrait' of the Emperor was enshrined in every school; we need to restore that situation."<ref>{{Cite book |author=Hideki Kajimura |title=Overcoming Exclusionism: A History of Korea |publisher=Heibonsha Library |year=2014 |page=25}}</ref>

After working at an oil shop and rice shop in Osaka, he opened a Matsumae merchant house,<ref>[https://dl.ndl.go.jp/info:ndljp/pid/778835/97 'Chōbei Ōmiwa', ''Meiji Fugo Chifujidai'', Sumitei Inshi (Daigakukan, 1902)]</ref> and later made a fortune in cotton trade, serving as president of the 58th National Bank, chairman of the Osaka Clearing House, Osaka Prefectural Assembly chairman, and Osaka City Council chairman, among other positions. In 1898, he became a member of the {{ill|House of Representatives|ja|衆議院|qq=draft|qq=draft}}.<ref name=sangyo/><ref>[https://www.ndl.go.jp/portrait/datas/36/ 'Chōbei Ōmiwa'], Portraits of Modern Japanese Historical Figures, National Diet Library</ref><ref>[https://kotobank.jp/word/大三輪長兵衛-1061443 'Chōbei Ōmiwa'], Kotobank</ref><ref>[http://www.pref.osaka.lg.jp/attach/3059/00006976/seifuku-itiran_20200522.pdf 'List of Former Chairmen and Vice-Chairmen of Osaka Prefectural Assembly'], Osaka Prefecture, 2020</ref>
* Son: '''{{ill|Ashizu Yasukuni|ja|葦津泰國|qq=draft|qq=draft}}''' (1937–2024) – Graduate of Tokyo Metropolitan University. Former president of Jinja Shinpo Sha. Author of ''The Life of Chōbei Ōmiwa'' (Ashizu Office, 2008), among others.
== Works ==
[Full list of his publications, including major works and collected editions.]
=== Collected Works ===
* ''Ashizu Ujihiko Selected Works'' – Edited by the same Editorial Committee<ref>Each with commentary by {{ill|Mitsuo Suzuki|ja|鈴木満男|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (a disciple)</ref>, Jinja Shinpo Sha, 1996 1. ''Emperor, Shinto, Constitution'' 2. ''As Successors of the Restoration'' 3. ''Current Affairs and Character Critiques''
* ''"Let's Read the Shōwa Era": Ashizu Ujihiko's Claims Series'' – Ashizu Office, 2005–2007 1. ''Japan's Monarchy: Studies on the Emperor System'' 2. ''Eternal Restorationist'' 3. ''The End of Modern Democracy: Revival of Japanese Thought'' 4. ''Words of a Native: The Logic of Trust and Loyalty'' 5. ''Greater Asianism and Toyama Mitsuru'' 6. ''Living the Shōwa Era: The Spirit and Thoughts of the People of the Divine Land''
== Footnotes ==
{{Reflist|30em}}
== References ==
* ''Meiji Restoration and the Liberation of the East'', "Preface (Kazuma Shindō)", Shin Seiryoku Sha, 1964 * ''Ashizu Ujihiko Memorial Record'', Konihon Sha, 1993 * ''Ashizu Ujihiko Selected Works Volume 3'', Jinja Shinpo Sha, 1996, commentary and chronology at the end * Yukio Hori, ''Postwar Right-Wing Forces'', "Chapter 3: Ideology of the Postwar Right Wing, Section 2: Thoughts of Ashizu Ujihiko—Ideology of the Association of Shinto Shrines", Keiso Shobō, 1993, Expanded Edition
== External Links ==
* [https://dl.ndl.go.jp/info:ndljp/pid/1271056 'Words of Japanese People: Enlightening the Nazi'], Ashizu Ujihiko (Kyōdai Kai, 1940) * [https://web.archive.org/web/20201125060116/https://www.chugainippoh.co.jp/info/ruikotu/ruikotu015-02-001.html 'On the Thought of Ashizu Ujihiko—Focusing on His Postwar Theories on the Emperor and Shinto'], Hiroyuki Imanishi, ''Chūgai Nippo'', 15th 'Ruikotsu Award' Winning Essay, Encouragement Prize * [http://ymatsuda.kill.jp/Ashizu-mokuroku.pdf 'List of Works by Ashizu Ujihiko']
