He was also renowned as a commentator of the [[:en:Minzoku-ha|Nationalist]] movement.<ref>Yukio Hori, ''Postwar Right-Wing Forces'', Keiso Shobō, 1993, Expanded Edition.</ref>
== Career ==
He was born as the eldest son of {{ill|Ashizu Kojiro|ja|葦津耕次郎|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ashizu Kojiro|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in Hakozaki, Fukuoka Prefecture (now Hakozaki, {{ill|Higashi-ku, Fukuoka|ja|福岡市東区|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Higashi-ku, Fukuoka|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}). The Ashizu family is a descendant of the Ogami family, one of the shrine families of [[:en:Hakozaki Shrine|Hakozaki Shrine]], and his uncle, {{ill|Ashizu Seizō|ja|葦津洗造|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ashizu Seizō|qq=|1=Ashizu Seizō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q115531037}}, was the chief priest of the same shrine.<ref name="#1">Foreword by {{ill|Ashizu Yasukuni|ja|葦津泰國|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ashizu Yasukuni|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, from ''Living the Shōwa Era: The Claims of Ujihiko Ashizu Series IV''</ref>
In 1922 (Taishō 11), he entered {{ill|Tokyo Metropolitan Koishikawa High School|ja|東京都立小石川高等学校|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokyo Metropolitan Koishikawa High School|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 東京都立小石川中等教育学校|1=Koishikawa Secondary Education School|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11525469}} (then Tokyo Prefectural Fifth Junior High School). Around this time, he yearned for socialism, participated in the Social Science Study Group, and avidly read related books.<ref name="#1"/>
Those around him were concerned, and after graduation, he went on to [[:en:Kokugakuin University|Kokugakuin University]].{{Citation needed|date=July 5, 2017}} He also enrolled in the {{ill|Tokyo School of Foreign Languages|ja|東京外国語学校 (旧制)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokyo School of Foreign Languages|qq=|1=Tokyo School of Foreign Languages|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11524652}}, but dropped out of both.{{Citation needed|date=July 5, 2017}} In 1928 (Shōwa 3), he entered [[:en:Fukushima University|Fukushima University]] (then Fukushima Higher Commercial School), but dropped out the following year.
In 1931, at the recommendation of a friend who belonged to the [[:en:Japanese Communist Party|Japanese Communist Party]], he wrote a leading article for the newspaper [[:en:Shimbun Akahata|Akahata]].<ref name=ashidu>{{Cite journal|author=Konno Nobuyuki|title=The Thought of Ashizu Ujihiko during and after the War—Focusing on His View of Shinto|journal=Interdisciplinary Research on 'Narratives' in Japan from 1890 to the 1950s|page=63|year=2012}}</ref> However, in February of the following year, under the influence of his father, he is said to have converted to traditional conservative imperial [[:en:Shinto|Shinto]], which aimed for the realization of "Japan, the Emperor's Nation with Unity of Rites and Government," like his father.<ref name=ashidu />
In 1932 (Shōwa 7), he succeeded his father's business and engaged in the construction of shrines and temples as the representative of a shrine and temple construction office.<ref>"Fifty Years of the Association of Shinto Shrines" (Non-commercial publication), May 1996, Publisher: [[:en:Association of Shinto Shrines|Association of Shinto Shrines]], p.74—Obituary of Ashizu Ujihiko, associate of {{ill|Jinja Shinpo Sha|ja|神社新報社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jinja Shinpo Sha|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 神社新報|1=Jinja Shinpo Sha|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11590705}} (June 10)</ref> During this period, he interacted with [[:en:Tōyama Mitsuru|Toyama Mitsuru]], {{ill|Teisuke Imaizumi|ja|今泉定助|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Teisuke Imaizumi|qq=|1=Sadasuke Imaizumi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11377248}}, {{ill|Takayama Noboru|ja|高山昇|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takayama Noboru|qq=|1=Noboru Takayama|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11669373}}, [[:en:Taketora Ogata|Ogata Taketora]], [[:en:Ryohei Uchida|Ryōhei Uchida]], {{ill|Inoue Tadamaro|ja|井上孚麿|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Inoue Tadamaro|qq=|1=Takamaro Inoue|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11372944}}, and others. Before the outbreak of the [[:en:Pacific War|Pacific War]], he opposed the war, viewing it as an "unwinnable battle," and after the outbreak, he engaged in unique political activities, such as insisting on adhering only to the war objectives shown in the Imperial Rescript on the outbreak of war, which was "to secure the survival of an independent nation as unavoidable."{{Citation needed|date=July 5, 2017}}
During the war, some of his critical articles on [[:en:Nazi Germany|Nazi Germany]], Japan's ally, and the policies of the [[:en:Hideki Tojo|Hideki Tōjō]] Cabinet were banned, and he was arrested at times.{{Citation needed|date=July 5, 2017}}
Immediately after the war, he dissolved his company and assisted [[:en:Shigeru Yoshida (civil servant)|Shigeru Yoshida (Home Ministry official)]], {{ill|Munenori Miyagawa|ja|宮川宗徳|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Munenori Miyagawa|qq=|1=Munenori Miyagawa|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q27921105}} (later Secretary-General of the main office), and [[:en:Muneyoshi Tokugawa|Tokugawa Munetake]] (later Chief of the main office), dedicating himself to the establishment of the [[:en:Association of Shinto Shrines|Association of Shinto Shrines]]. Furthermore, when the '{{ill|Jinja Shinpo|ja|神社新報|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jinja Shinpo|qq=|1=Jinja Shinpo|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11590705}}' was launched as part of the main office's educational public relations, he took charge of the management of {{ill|Jinja Shinpo Sha|ja|神社新報社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jinja Shinpo Sha|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 神社新報|1=Jinja Shinpo Sha|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11590705}}, the publishing body, along with being the chief editor.
Even after his retirement in 1968 (Shōwa 43), he continued his writing activities and the guidance of future generations, being at the forefront of movements to protect the [[:en:Kokutai|Kokutai]] and [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrines]]. He continued to have a significant influence on the Shinto world (through the {{ill|National Council to Defend Japan|ja|日本を守る国民会議|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/National Council to Defend Japan|qq=|1=National Council to Defend Japan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11506199}}, now [[:en:Nippon Kaigi|Nippon Kaigi]]) and {{ill|Nationalist student movements|ja|民族派学生組織|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nationalist student movements|qq=|1=Nationalist student movements|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11547922}} ({{ill|Japan Youth Council|ja|日本青年協議会|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Japan Youth Council|qq=|1=Japan Youth Council|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11509401}}), among others.
On the other hand, he questioned the enshrinement of {{ill|Class A war criminals|ja|A級戦犯|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Class A war criminals|qq=|1=Class A war criminal|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q5369948}} like Tōjō, whom he had criticized during the war, due to their responsibility for the defeat.<ref>"Yasukuni: Where to Go from 'Postwar' 3—Article by Eminent Scholar of Shinto History Raises Doubts about 'Class A Enshrinement'," ''Mainichi Shimbun'' Tokyo edition morning edition: August 8, 2006</ref> (Note: [[:en:Yasukuni Shrine|Yasukuni Shrine]] is not a [[:en:Beppyo shrine|Beppyo Shrine]] of the Association of Shinto Shrines.)
In 1992 (Heisei 4), he passed away at his home in [[:en:Kamakura|Kamakura]] at the age of 82.
{{ill|Bunzō Hashikawa|ja|橋川文三|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Bunzō Hashikawa|qq=|1=Bunsō Hashikawa|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1588426}}, who had experiences of debates with Ashizu, praised him as "an advanced polemicist among conservatives," noting that his theory of the Emperor system "should be noted as surpassing the level of traditional right-wingers." He pointed out that Ashizu tried to grasp the problem of the Emperor system from a comparative institutional perspective, based on research in European political thought history, pursuing an open form of "Kokutai theory" as his characteristic.<ref>From ''Postwar Japanese Thought Series 7: Conservative Thought,'' edited by Bunzō Hashikawa, Chikuma Shobō, 1968</ref>
{{ill|Yoshio Ōishi|ja|大石義雄|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yoshio Ōishi|qq=|1=Yoshio Ōishi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11438252}}, in ''Twenty Years of the Constitution'', praised Ashizu's ''Words of a Native'' and described Ashizu as "a modern-day Kitabatake Chikafusa."
After the war, he had friendships that transcended ideological positions with {{ill|Saburō Ichi'i|ja|市井三郎|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saburō Ichi'i|qq=|1=Saburō Ichii|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11479994}} and [[:en:Shunsuke Tsurumi|Shunsuke Tsurumi]], who were editors of ''{{ill|Shisō no Kagaku|ja|思想の科学|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shisō no Kagaku|qq=|1=Shisō no Kagaku|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11491520}}'', and the critic [[:en:Yoshimi Takeuchi|Yoshihiko Takeuchi]], and he often contributed to the same magazine.
He was also well-versed in Korean culture and had interactions with [[:en:Lyuh Woon-hyung|Lyuh Woon-hyung]], an independence activist in Korea during the Japanese occupation. It is said that when Ashizu visited the [[:en:Korea University (disambiguation)|Korea University (Japan)]] (in Japan), he was moved upon seeing a portrait of [[:en:Kim Il Sung|Kim Il-sung]] in the middle of the reception room, and remarked, "This is what current Japanese people lack. In the past, the 'Imperial Portrait' of the Emperor was enshrined in every school; we need to restore that situation."<ref>{{Cite book |author=Hideki Kajimura |title=Overcoming Exclusionism: A History of Korea |publisher=Heibonsha Library |year=2014 |page=25}}</ref>
== Family ==
[[File:Iwasaki Seishichi and his friends.jpg|thumb|Nara Tarō Ōmiwa (center of back row)]]
* Father: '''{{ill|Ashizu Kojiro|ja|葦津耕次郎|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ashizu Kojiro|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}''' (1878–1940) – Shintoist.<ref name=nishiya>{{Cite journal|author=Takafumi Nishiya |title=Ashizu Kojiro: The Trajectory of a Shinto Practitioner during the 'State Shinto' Period |volume=Kyoto University Doctoral Thesis |issue=No.14958 |year=2009 |naid=500000489926 |url=https://hdl.handle.net/2433/126522}}</ref> He served at [[:en:Hakozaki Shrine|Hakozaki Shrine]], devoted himself to Shinto service, and later became active as a businessman running mining and shrine construction businesses, while also engaging as a commentator expressing views on political and social issues from the perspective of Shinto and shrines, such as the {{ill|Korean Shrine Deity Controversy|ja|朝鮮神宮御祭神論争|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Korean Shrine Deity Controversy|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.<ref>[http://kaeizyuku.com/2020/01/14/visit-graves/ January 14, 2020 'Visit to the Ashizu Family Grave'] Kaei-juku</ref>
* Grandfather: '''Ashizu Isoo''' (–1903) – Chief priest of Hakozaki Shrine.<ref name=asia>Uraube Noboru, [http://asiarestoration.com/wp-content/uploads/2016/06/daiajia1st.pdf 'Daijia wo Tsuranuita Ōmiwa Asahei'], ''Dai Ajia'' inaugural issue, June 30, 2016, Dai Ajia Kenkyūkai</ref> For a time, he also concurrently served as chief priest of the [[:en:Kashii-gū|Kashii Shrine]].<ref>[https://dl.ndl.go.jp/info:ndljp/pid/2947481/9 Official Gazette. June 26, 1897]</ref>
* Great-uncle: '''{{ill|Chōbei Ōmiwa|ja|大三輪長兵衛|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chōbei Ōmiwa|qq=|1=Ōmiwa Chōbē|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q124483411}}''' (1835–1908) – Brother of his grandfather. After working at an oil shop and rice shop in Osaka, he opened a Matsumae merchant house,<ref>[https://dl.ndl.go.jp/info:ndljp/pid/778835/97 'Chōbei Ōmiwa', ''Meiji Fugo Chifujidai'', Sumitei Inshi (Daigakukan, 1902)]</ref> and later made a fortune in cotton trade, serving as president of the 58th National Bank, chairman of the Osaka Clearing House, Osaka Prefectural Assembly chairman, and Osaka City Council chairman, among other positions. In 1898, he became a member of the [[:en:House of Representatives (Japan)|House of Representatives]].<ref name=sangyo/><ref>[https://www.ndl.go.jp/portrait/datas/36/ 'Chōbei Ōmiwa'], Portraits of Modern Japanese Historical Figures, National Diet Library</ref><ref>[https://kotobank.jp/word/大三輪長兵衛-1061443 'Chōbei Ōmiwa'], Kotobank</ref><ref>[http://www.pref.osaka.lg.jp/attach/3059/00006976/seifuku-itiran_20200522.pdf 'List of Former Chairmen and Vice-Chairmen of Osaka Prefectural Assembly'], Osaka Prefecture, 2020</ref>
* Son: '''{{ill|Ashizu Yasukuni|ja|葦津泰國|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ashizu Yasukuni|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}''' (1937–2024) – Graduate of Tokyo Metropolitan University. Former president of Jinja Shinpo Sha. Author of ''The Life of Chōbei Ōmiwa'' (Ashizu Office, 2008), among others.
== Works ==
[Full list of his publications, including major works and collected editions.]
=== Collected Works ===
* ''Ashizu Ujihiko Selected Works'' – Edited by the same Editorial Committee<ref>Each with commentary by {{ill|Mitsuo Suzuki|ja|鈴木満男|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mitsuo Suzuki|qq=|1=Mitsuo Suzuki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q24872776}} (a disciple)</ref>, Jinja Shinpo Sha, 1996 1. ''Emperor, Shinto, Constitution'' 2. ''As Successors of the Restoration'' 3. ''Current Affairs and Character Critiques''
* ''"Let's Read the Shōwa Era": Ashizu Ujihiko's Claims Series'' – Ashizu Office, 2005–2007 1. ''Japan's Monarchy: Studies on the Emperor System'' 2. ''Eternal Restorationist'' 3. ''The End of Modern Democracy: Revival of Japanese Thought'' 4. ''Words of a Native: The Logic of Trust and Loyalty'' 5. ''Greater Asianism and Toyama Mitsuru'' 6. ''Living the Shōwa Era: The Spirit and Thoughts of the People of the Divine Land''
== Footnotes ==
{{Reflist|30em}}
== References ==
* ''Meiji Restoration and the Liberation of the East'', "Preface (Kazuma Shindō)", Shin Seiryoku Sha, 1964 * ''Ashizu Ujihiko Memorial Record'', Konihon Sha, 1993 * ''Ashizu Ujihiko Selected Works Volume 3'', Jinja Shinpo Sha, 1996, commentary and chronology at the end * Yukio Hori, ''Postwar Right-Wing Forces'', "Chapter 3: Ideology of the Postwar Right Wing, Section 2: Thoughts of Ashizu Ujihiko—Ideology of the Association of Shinto Shrines", Keiso Shobō, 1993, Expanded Edition
== External Links ==
* [https://dl.ndl.go.jp/info:ndljp/pid/1271056 'Words of Japanese People: Enlightening the Nazi'], Ashizu Ujihiko (Kyōdai Kai, 1940) * [https://web.archive.org/web/20201125060116/https://www.chugainippoh.co.jp/info/ruikotu/ruikotu015-02-001.html 'On the Thought of Ashizu Ujihiko—Focusing on His Postwar Theories on the Emperor and Shinto'], Hiroyuki Imanishi, ''Chūgai Nippo'', 15th 'Ruikotsu Award' Winning Essay, Encouragement Prize * [http://ymatsuda.kill.jp/Ashizu-mokuroku.pdf 'List of Works by Ashizu Ujihiko']

revision.text:
{{Infobox person|birth_date=1909; {{birth date|1909|7|17}}|death_date={{death date|1992|6|10}}|birth_place={{ill|Fukuoka Prefecture|qid=Q123258}}|gender={{ill|male|qid=Q6581097}}|nationality={{ill|Empire of Japan|qid=Q188712}}; {{ill|Japan|qid=Q17}}|occupation={{ill|critic|qid=Q6430706}}}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11256585]] Wikidata name: Uzuhiko Ashizu [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:19, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Ashizu Ujihiko''' (葦津 珍彦, July 17, 1909 – June 10, 1992) was a Japanese [[:en:Shinto|Shinto]] scholar. He was also renowned as a commentator of the [[:en:Minzoku-ha|Nationalist]] movement.<ref>Yukio Hori, ''Postwar Right-Wing Forces'', Keiso Shobō, 1993, Expanded Edition.</ref>
== Career ==
He was born as the eldest son of {{ill|Ashizu Kojiro|ja|葦津耕次郎|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ashizu Kojiro|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in Hakozaki, Fukuoka Prefecture (now Hakozaki, {{ill|Higashi-ku, Fukuoka|ja|福岡市東区|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Higashi-ku, Fukuoka|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}). The Ashizu family is a descendant of the Ogami family, one of the shrine families of [[:en:Hakozaki Shrine|Hakozaki Shrine]], and his uncle, {{ill|Ashizu Seizō|ja|葦津洗造|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ashizu Seizō|qq=|1=Ashizu Seizō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q115531037}}, was the chief priest of the same shrine.<ref name="#1">Foreword by {{ill|Ashizu Yasukuni|ja|葦津泰國|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ashizu Yasukuni|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, from ''Living the Shōwa Era: The Claims of Ujihiko Ashizu Series IV''</ref>
In 1922 (Taishō 11), he entered {{ill|Tokyo Metropolitan Koishikawa High School|ja|東京都立小石川高等学校|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokyo Metropolitan Koishikawa High School|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 東京都立小石川中等教育学校|1=Koishikawa Secondary Education School|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11525469}} (then Tokyo Prefectural Fifth Junior High School). Around this time, he yearned for socialism, participated in the Social Science Study Group, and avidly read related books.<ref name="#1"/>
Those around him were concerned, and after graduation, he went on to [[:en:Kokugakuin University|Kokugakuin University]].{{Citation needed|date=July 5, 2017}} He also enrolled in the {{ill|Tokyo School of Foreign Languages|ja|東京外国語学校 (旧制)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokyo School of Foreign Languages|qq=|1=Tokyo School of Foreign Languages|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11524652}}, but dropped out of both.{{Citation needed|date=July 5, 2017}} In 1928 (Shōwa 3), he entered [[:en:Fukushima University|Fukushima University]] (then Fukushima Higher Commercial School), but dropped out the following year.
In 1931, at the recommendation of a friend who belonged to the [[:en:Japanese Communist Party|Japanese Communist Party]], he wrote a leading article for the newspaper [[:en:Shimbun Akahata|Akahata]].<ref name=ashidu>{{Cite journal|author=Konno Nobuyuki|title=The Thought of Ashizu Ujihiko during and after the War—Focusing on His View of Shinto|journal=Interdisciplinary Research on 'Narratives' in Japan from 1890 to the 1950s|page=63|year=2012}}</ref> However, in February of the following year, under the influence of his father, he is said to have converted to traditional conservative imperial [[:en:Shinto|Shinto]], which aimed for the realization of "Japan, the Emperor's Nation with Unity of Rites and Government," like his father.<ref name=ashidu />
In 1932 (Shōwa 7), he succeeded his father's business and engaged in the construction of shrines and temples as the representative of a shrine and temple construction office.<ref>"Fifty Years of the Association of Shinto Shrines" (Non-commercial publication), May 1996, Publisher: [[:en:Association of Shinto Shrines|Association of Shinto Shrines]], p.74—Obituary of Ashizu Ujihiko, associate of {{ill|Jinja Shinpo Sha|ja|神社新報社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jinja Shinpo Sha|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 神社新報|1=Jinja Shinpo Sha|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11590705}} (June 10)</ref> During this period, he interacted with [[:en:Tōyama Mitsuru|Toyama Mitsuru]], {{ill|Teisuke Imaizumi|ja|今泉定助|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Teisuke Imaizumi|qq=|1=Sadasuke Imaizumi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11377248}}, {{ill|Takayama Noboru|ja|高山昇|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takayama Noboru|qq=|1=Noboru Takayama|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11669373}}, [[:en:Taketora Ogata|Ogata Taketora]], [[:en:Ryohei Uchida|Ryōhei Uchida]], {{ill|Inoue Tadamaro|ja|井上孚麿|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Inoue Tadamaro|qq=|1=Takamaro Inoue|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11372944}}, and others. Before the outbreak of the [[:en:Pacific War|Pacific War]], he opposed the war, viewing it as an "unwinnable battle," and after the outbreak, he engaged in unique political activities, such as insisting on adhering only to the war objectives shown in the Imperial Rescript on the outbreak of war, which was "to secure the survival of an independent nation as unavoidable."{{Citation needed|date=July 5, 2017}}
During the war, some of his critical articles on [[:en:Nazi Germany|Nazi Germany]], Japan's ally, and the policies of the [[:en:Hideki Tojo|Hideki Tōjō]] Cabinet were banned, and he was arrested at times.{{Citation needed|date=July 5, 2017}}
Immediately after the war, he dissolved his company and assisted [[:en:Shigeru Yoshida (civil servant)|Shigeru Yoshida (Home Ministry official)]], {{ill|Munenori Miyagawa|ja|宮川宗徳|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Munenori Miyagawa|qq=|1=Munenori Miyagawa|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q27921105}} (later Secretary-General of the main office), and [[:en:Muneyoshi Tokugawa|Tokugawa Munetake]] (later Chief of the main office), dedicating himself to the establishment of the [[:en:Association of Shinto Shrines|Association of Shinto Shrines]]. Furthermore, when the '{{ill|Jinja Shinpo|ja|神社新報|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jinja Shinpo|qq=|1=Jinja Shinpo|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11590705}}' was launched as part of the main office's educational public relations, he took charge of the management of {{ill|Jinja Shinpo Sha|ja|神社新報社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jinja Shinpo Sha|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 神社新報|1=Jinja Shinpo Sha|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11590705}}, the publishing body, along with being the chief editor.
Even after his retirement in 1968 (Shōwa 43), he continued his writing activities and the guidance of future generations, being at the forefront of movements to protect the [[:en:Kokutai|Kokutai]] and [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrines]]. He continued to have a significant influence on the Shinto world (through the {{ill|National Council to Defend Japan|ja|日本を守る国民会議|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/National Council to Defend Japan|qq=|1=National Council to Defend Japan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11506199}}, now [[:en:Nippon Kaigi|Nippon Kaigi]]) and {{ill|Nationalist student movements|ja|民族派学生組織|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nationalist student movements|qq=|1=Nationalist student movements|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11547922}} ({{ill|Japan Youth Council|ja|日本青年協議会|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Japan Youth Council|qq=|1=Japan Youth Council|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11509401}}), among others.
On the other hand, he questioned the enshrinement of {{ill|Class A war criminals|ja|A級戦犯|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Class A war criminals|qq=|1=Class A war criminal|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q5369948}} like Tōjō, whom he had criticized during the war, due to their responsibility for the defeat.<ref>"Yasukuni: Where to Go from 'Postwar' 3—Article by Eminent Scholar of Shinto History Raises Doubts about 'Class A Enshrinement'," ''Mainichi Shimbun'' Tokyo edition morning edition: August 8, 2006</ref> (Note: [[:en:Yasukuni Shrine|Yasukuni Shrine]] is not a [[:en:Beppyo shrine|Beppyo Shrine]] of the Association of Shinto Shrines.)
In 1992 (Heisei 4), he passed away at his home in [[:en:Kamakura|Kamakura]] at the age of 82.
{{ill|Bunzō Hashikawa|ja|橋川文三|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Bunzō Hashikawa|qq=|1=Bunsō Hashikawa|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1588426}}, who had experiences of debates with Ashizu, praised him as "an advanced polemicist among conservatives," noting that his theory of the Emperor system "should be noted as surpassing the level of traditional right-wingers." He pointed out that Ashizu tried to grasp the problem of the Emperor system from a comparative institutional perspective, based on research in European political thought history, pursuing an open form of "Kokutai theory" as his characteristic.<ref>From ''Postwar Japanese Thought Series 7: Conservative Thought,'' edited by Bunzō Hashikawa, Chikuma Shobō, 1968</ref>
{{ill|Yoshio Ōishi|ja|大石義雄|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yoshio Ōishi|qq=|1=Yoshio Ōishi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11438252}}, in ''Twenty Years of the Constitution'', praised Ashizu's ''Words of a Native'' and described Ashizu as "a modern-day Kitabatake Chikafusa."
After the war, he had friendships that transcended ideological positions with {{ill|Saburō Ichi'i|ja|市井三郎|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saburō Ichi'i|qq=|1=Saburō Ichii|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11479994}} and [[:en:Shunsuke Tsurumi|Shunsuke Tsurumi]], who were editors of ''{{ill|Shisō no Kagaku|ja|思想の科学|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shisō no Kagaku|qq=|1=Shisō no Kagaku|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11491520}}'', and the critic [[:en:Yoshimi Takeuchi|Yoshihiko Takeuchi]], and he often contributed to the same magazine.
He was also well-versed in Korean culture and had interactions with [[:en:Lyuh Woon-hyung|Lyuh Woon-hyung]], an independence activist in Korea during the Japanese occupation. It is said that when Ashizu visited the [[:en:Korea University (disambiguation)|Korea University (Japan)]] (in Japan), he was moved upon seeing a portrait of [[:en:Kim Il Sung|Kim Il-sung]] in the middle of the reception room, and remarked, "This is what current Japanese people lack. In the past, the 'Imperial Portrait' of the Emperor was enshrined in every school; we need to restore that situation."<ref>{{Cite book |author=Hideki Kajimura |title=Overcoming Exclusionism: A History of Korea |publisher=Heibonsha Library |year=2014 |page=25}}</ref>
== Family ==
[[File:Iwasaki Seishichi and his friends.jpg|thumb|Nara Tarō Ōmiwa (center of back row)]]
* Father: '''{{ill|Ashizu Kojiro|ja|葦津耕次郎|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ashizu Kojiro|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}''' (1878–1940) – Shintoist.<ref name=nishiya>{{Cite journal|author=Takafumi Nishiya |title=Ashizu Kojiro: The Trajectory of a Shinto Practitioner during the 'State Shinto' Period |volume=Kyoto University Doctoral Thesis |issue=No.14958 |year=2009 |naid=500000489926 |url=https://hdl.handle.net/2433/126522}}</ref> He served at [[:en:Hakozaki Shrine|Hakozaki Shrine]], devoted himself to Shinto service, and later became active as a businessman running mining and shrine construction businesses, while also engaging as a commentator expressing views on political and social issues from the perspective of Shinto and shrines, such as the {{ill|Korean Shrine Deity Controversy|ja|朝鮮神宮御祭神論争|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Korean Shrine Deity Controversy|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.<ref>[http://kaeizyuku.com/2020/01/14/visit-graves/ January 14, 2020 'Visit to the Ashizu Family Grave'] Kaei-juku</ref>
* Grandfather: '''Ashizu Isoo''' (–1903) – Chief priest of Hakozaki Shrine.<ref name=asia>Uraube Noboru, [http://asiarestoration.com/wp-content/uploads/2016/06/daiajia1st.pdf 'Daijia wo Tsuranuita Ōmiwa Asahei'], ''Dai Ajia'' inaugural issue, June 30, 2016, Dai Ajia Kenkyūkai</ref> For a time, he also concurrently served as chief priest of the [[:en:Kashii-gū|Kashii Shrine]].<ref>[https://dl.ndl.go.jp/info:ndljp/pid/2947481/9 Official Gazette. June 26, 1897]</ref>
* Great-uncle: '''{{ill|Chōbei Ōmiwa|ja|大三輪長兵衛|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chōbei Ōmiwa|qq=|1=Ōmiwa Chōbē|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q124483411}}''' (1835–1908) – Brother of his grandfather. After working at an oil shop and rice shop in Osaka, he opened a Matsumae merchant house,<ref>[https://dl.ndl.go.jp/info:ndljp/pid/778835/97 'Chōbei Ōmiwa', ''Meiji Fugo Chifujidai'', Sumitei Inshi (Daigakukan, 1902)]</ref> and later made a fortune in cotton trade, serving as president of the 58th National Bank, chairman of the Osaka Clearing House, Osaka Prefectural Assembly chairman, and Osaka City Council chairman, among other positions. In 1898, he became a member of the [[:en:House of Representatives (Japan)|House of Representatives]].<ref name=sangyo/><ref>[https://www.ndl.go.jp/portrait/datas/36/ 'Chōbei Ōmiwa'], Portraits of Modern Japanese Historical Figures, National Diet Library</ref><ref>[https://kotobank.jp/word/大三輪長兵衛-1061443 'Chōbei Ōmiwa'], Kotobank</ref><ref>[http://www.pref.osaka.lg.jp/attach/3059/00006976/seifuku-itiran_20200522.pdf 'List of Former Chairmen and Vice-Chairmen of Osaka Prefectural Assembly'], Osaka Prefecture, 2020</ref>
* Son: '''{{ill|Ashizu Yasukuni|ja|葦津泰國|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ashizu Yasukuni|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}''' (1937–2024) – Graduate of Tokyo Metropolitan University. Former president of Jinja Shinpo Sha. Author of ''The Life of Chōbei Ōmiwa'' (Ashizu Office, 2008), among others.

<!--Uzuhiko Ashizu--><!--Uzuhiko Ashizu--><!--Uzuhiko Ashizu--><!--Uzuhiko Ashizu--><!--Uzuhiko Ashizu--><!--Uzuhiko Ashizu--><!--Uzuhiko Ashizu--><!--Uzuhiko Ashizu--><!--Uzuhiko Ashizu--><!--Uzuhiko Ashizu--><!--Uzuhiko Ashizu--><!--Uzuhiko Ashizu--><!--Uzuhiko Ashizu--><!--Uzuhiko Ashizu--><!--Uzuhiko Ashizu--><!--Uzuhiko Ashizu--> [[Category:20th Century Japanese Critics]] [[Category:Japanese conservative thinkers]] [[Category:People from Tokyo Metropolitan Koishikawa Middle High School]] [[Category:People from Fukuoka]] <!--jawiki derived wikidata interwikis--> [[ko:아시즈 우즈히코]] {{wikidata link|Q11256585}}

{{Infobox person|birth_date=1909; {{birth date|1909|7|17}}|death_date={{death date|1992|6|10}}|birth_place={{ill|Fukuoka Prefecture|qid=Q123258}}|gender={{ill|male|qid=Q6581097}}|nationality={{ill|Empire of Japan|qid=Q188712}}; {{ill|Japan|qid=Q17}}|occupation={{ill|critic|qid=Q6430706}}}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11256585]] Wikidata name: Uzuhiko Ashizu [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:19, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Ashizu Ujihiko''' (葦津 珍彦, July 17, 1909 – June 10, 1992) was a Japanese [[:en:Shinto|Shinto]] scholar. He was also renowned as a commentator of the [[:en:Minzoku-ha|Nationalist]] movement.<ref>Yukio Hori, ''Postwar Right-Wing Forces'', Keiso Shobō, 1993, Expanded Edition.</ref>
== Career ==
He was born as the eldest son of {{ill|Ashizu Kojiro|ja|葦津耕次郎|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ashizu Kojiro|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in Hakozaki, Fukuoka Prefecture (now Hakozaki, {{ill|Higashi-ku, Fukuoka|ja|福岡市東区|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Higashi-ku, Fukuoka|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}). The Ashizu family is a descendant of the Ogami family, one of the shrine families of [[:en:Hakozaki Shrine|Hakozaki Shrine]], and his uncle, {{ill|Ashizu Seizō|ja|葦津洗造|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ashizu Seizō|qq=|1=Ashizu Seizō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q115531037}}, was the chief priest of the same shrine.<ref name="#1">Foreword by {{ill|Ashizu Yasukuni|ja|葦津泰國|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ashizu Yasukuni|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, from ''Living the Shōwa Era: The Claims of Ujihiko Ashizu Series IV''</ref>
In 1922 (Taishō 11), he entered {{ill|Tokyo Metropolitan Koishikawa High School|ja|東京都立小石川高等学校|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokyo Metropolitan Koishikawa High School|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 東京都立小石川中等教育学校|1=Koishikawa Secondary Education School|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11525469}} (then Tokyo Prefectural Fifth Junior High School). Around this time, he yearned for socialism, participated in the Social Science Study Group, and avidly read related books.<ref name="#1"/>
Those around him were concerned, and after graduation, he went on to [[:en:Kokugakuin University|Kokugakuin University]].{{Citation needed|date=July 5, 2017}} He also enrolled in the {{ill|Tokyo School of Foreign Languages|ja|東京外国語学校 (旧制)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokyo School of Foreign Languages|qq=|1=Tokyo School of Foreign Languages|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11524652}}, but dropped out of both.{{Citation needed|date=July 5, 2017}} In 1928 (Shōwa 3), he entered [[:en:Fukushima University|Fukushima University]] (then Fukushima Higher Commercial School), but dropped out the following year.
In 1931, at the recommendation of a friend who belonged to the [[:en:Japanese Communist Party|Japanese Communist Party]], he wrote a leading article for the newspaper [[:en:Shimbun Akahata|Akahata]].<ref name=ashidu>{{Cite journal|author=Konno Nobuyuki|title=The Thought of Ashizu Ujihiko during and after the War—Focusing on His View of Shinto|journal=Interdisciplinary Research on 'Narratives' in Japan from 1890 to the 1950s|page=63|year=2012}}</ref> However, in February of the following year, under the influence of his father, he is said to have converted to traditional conservative imperial [[:en:Shinto|Shinto]], which aimed for the realization of "Japan, the Emperor's Nation with Unity of Rites and Government," like his father.<ref name=ashidu />
In 1932 (Shōwa 7), he succeeded his father's business and engaged in the construction of shrines and temples as the representative of a shrine and temple construction office.<ref>"Fifty Years of the Association of Shinto Shrines" (Non-commercial publication), May 1996, Publisher: [[:en:Association of Shinto Shrines|Association of Shinto Shrines]], p.74—Obituary of Ashizu Ujihiko, associate of {{ill|Jinja Shinpo Sha|ja|神社新報社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jinja Shinpo Sha|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 神社新報|1=Jinja Shinpo Sha|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11590705}} (June 10)</ref> During this period, he interacted with [[:en:Tōyama Mitsuru|Toyama Mitsuru]], {{ill|Teisuke Imaizumi|ja|今泉定助|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Teisuke Imaizumi|qq=|1=Sadasuke Imaizumi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11377248}}, {{ill|Takayama Noboru|ja|高山昇|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takayama Noboru|qq=|1=Noboru Takayama|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11669373}}, [[:en:Taketora Ogata|Ogata Taketora]], [[:en:Ryohei Uchida|Ryōhei Uchida]], {{ill|Inoue Tadamaro|ja|井上孚麿|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Inoue Tadamaro|qq=|1=Takamaro Inoue|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11372944}}, and others. Before the outbreak of the [[:en:Pacific War|Pacific War]], he opposed the war, viewing it as an "unwinnable battle," and after the outbreak, he engaged in unique political activities, such as insisting on adhering only to the war objectives shown in the Imperial Rescript on the outbreak of war, which was "to secure the survival of an independent nation as unavoidable."{{Citation needed|date=July 5, 2017}}
During the war, some of his critical articles on [[:en:Nazi Germany|Nazi Germany]], Japan's ally, and the policies of the [[:en:Hideki Tojo|Hideki Tōjō]] Cabinet were banned, and he was arrested at times.{{Citation needed|date=July 5, 2017}}
Immediately after the war, he dissolved his company and assisted [[:en:Shigeru Yoshida (civil servant)|Shigeru Yoshida (Home Ministry official)]], {{ill|Munenori Miyagawa|ja|宮川宗徳|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Munenori Miyagawa|qq=|1=Munenori Miyagawa|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q27921105}} (later Secretary-General of the main office), and [[:en:Muneyoshi Tokugawa|Tokugawa Munetake]] (later Chief of the main office), dedicating himself to the establishment of the [[:en:Association of Shinto Shrines|Association of Shinto Shrines]]. Furthermore, when the '{{ill|Jinja Shinpo|ja|神社新報|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jinja Shinpo|qq=|1=Jinja Shinpo|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11590705}}' was launched as part of the main office's educational public relations, he took charge of the management of {{ill|Jinja Shinpo Sha|ja|神社新報社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jinja Shinpo Sha|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 神社新報|1=Jinja Shinpo Sha|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11590705}}, the publishing body, along with being the chief editor.
Even after his retirement in 1968 (Shōwa 43), he continued his writing activities and the guidance of future generations, being at the forefront of movements to protect the [[:en:Kokutai|Kokutai]] and [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrines]]. He continued to have a significant influence on the Shinto world (through the {{ill|National Council to Defend Japan|ja|日本を守る国民会議|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/National Council to Defend Japan|qq=|1=National Council to Defend Japan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11506199}}, now [[:en:Nippon Kaigi|Nippon Kaigi]]) and {{ill|Nationalist student movements|ja|民族派学生組織|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nationalist student movements|qq=|1=Nationalist student movements|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11547922}} ({{ill|Japan Youth Council|ja|日本青年協議会|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Japan Youth Council|qq=|1=Japan Youth Council|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11509401}}), among others.
On the other hand, he questioned the enshrinement of {{ill|Class A war criminals|ja|A級戦犯|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Class A war criminals|qq=|1=Class A war criminal|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q5369948}} like Tōjō, whom he had criticized during the war, due to their responsibility for the defeat.<ref>"Yasukuni: Where to Go from 'Postwar' 3—Article by Eminent Scholar of Shinto History Raises Doubts about 'Class A Enshrinement'," ''Mainichi Shimbun'' Tokyo edition morning edition: August 8, 2006</ref> (Note: [[:en:Yasukuni Shrine|Yasukuni Shrine]] is not a [[:en:Beppyo shrine|Beppyo Shrine]] of the Association of Shinto Shrines.)
In 1992 (Heisei 4), he passed away at his home in [[:en:Kamakura|Kamakura]] at the age of 82.
{{ill|Bunzō Hashikawa|ja|橋川文三|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Bunzō Hashikawa|qq=|1=Bunsō Hashikawa|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1588426}}, who had experiences of debates with Ashizu, praised him as "an advanced polemicist among conservatives," noting that his theory of the Emperor system "should be noted as surpassing the level of traditional right-wingers." He pointed out that Ashizu tried to grasp the problem of the Emperor system from a comparative institutional perspective, based on research in European political thought history, pursuing an open form of "Kokutai theory" as his characteristic.<ref>From ''Postwar Japanese Thought Series 7: Conservative Thought,'' edited by Bunzō Hashikawa, Chikuma Shobō, 1968</ref>
{{ill|Yoshio Ōishi|ja|大石義雄|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yoshio Ōishi|qq=|1=Yoshio Ōishi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11438252}}, in ''Twenty Years of the Constitution'', praised Ashizu's ''Words of a Native'' and described Ashizu as "a modern-day Kitabatake Chikafusa."
After the war, he had friendships that transcended ideological positions with {{ill|Saburō Ichi'i|ja|市井三郎|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saburō Ichi'i|qq=|1=Saburō Ichii|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11479994}} and [[:en:Shunsuke Tsurumi|Shunsuke Tsurumi]], who were editors of ''{{ill|Shisō no Kagaku|ja|思想の科学|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shisō no Kagaku|qq=|1=Shisō no Kagaku|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11491520}}'', and the critic [[:en:Yoshimi Takeuchi|Yoshihiko Takeuchi]], and he often contributed to the same magazine.
He was also well-versed in Korean culture and had interactions with [[:en:Lyuh Woon-hyung|Lyuh Woon-hyung]], an independence activist in Korea during the Japanese occupation. It is said that when Ashizu visited the [[:en:Korea University (disambiguation)|Korea University (Japan)]] (in Japan), he was moved upon seeing a portrait of [[:en:Kim Il Sung|Kim Il-sung]] in the middle of the reception room, and remarked, "This is what current Japanese people lack. In the past, the 'Imperial Portrait' of the Emperor was enshrined in every school; we need to restore that situation."<ref>{{Cite book |author=Hideki Kajimura |title=Overcoming Exclusionism: A History of Korea |publisher=Heibonsha Library |year=2014 |page=25}}</ref>

June 26, 1897]</ref>
* Great-uncle: '''{{ill|Chōbei Ōmiwa|ja|大三輪長兵衛|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chōbei Ōmiwa|qq=|1=Ōmiwa Chōbē|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q124483411}}''' (1835–1908) – Brother of his grandfather. After working at an oil shop and rice shop in Osaka, he opened a Matsumae merchant house,<ref>[https://dl.ndl.go.jp/info:ndljp/pid/778835/97 'Chōbei Ōmiwa', ''Meiji Fugo Chifujidai'', Sumitei Inshi (Daigakukan, 1902)]</ref> and later made a fortune in cotton trade, serving as president of the 58th National Bank, chairman of the Osaka Clearing House, Osaka Prefectural Assembly chairman, and Osaka City Council chairman, among other positions. In 1898, he became a member of the [[:en:House of Representatives (Japan)|House of Representatives]].<ref name=sangyo/><ref>[https://www.ndl.go.jp/portrait/datas/36/ 'Chōbei Ōmiwa'], Portraits of Modern Japanese Historical Figures, National Diet Library</ref><ref>[https://kotobank.jp/word/大三輪長兵衛-1061443 'Chōbei Ōmiwa'], Kotobank</ref><ref>[http://www.pref.osaka.lg.jp/attach/3059/00006976/seifuku-itiran_20200522.pdf 'List of Former Chairmen and Vice-Chairmen of Osaka Prefectural Assembly'], Osaka Prefecture, 2020</ref>
* Son: '''{{ill|Ashizu Yasukuni|ja|葦津泰國|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ashizu Yasukuni|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}''' (1937–2024) – Graduate of Tokyo Metropolitan University. Former president of Jinja Shinpo Sha. Author of ''The Life of Chōbei Ōmiwa'' (Ashizu Office, 2008), among others.

{{Infobox person|birth_date=1909; {{birth date|1909|7|17}}|death_date={{death date|1992|6|10}}|birth_place={{ill|Fukuoka Prefecture|qid=Q123258}}|gender={{ill|male|qid=Q6581097}}|nationality={{ill|Empire of Japan|qid=Q188712}}; {{ill|Japan|qid=Q17}}|occupation={{ill|critic|qid=Q6430706}}}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11256585]] Wikidata name: Uzuhiko Ashizu [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:19, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Ashizu Ujihiko''' (葦津 珍彦, July 17, 1909 – June 10, 1992) was a Japanese [[:en:Shinto|Shinto]] scholar. He was also renowned as a commentator of the [[:en:Minzoku-ha|Nationalist]] movement.<ref>Yukio Hori, ''Postwar Right-Wing Forces'', Keiso Shobō, 1993, Expanded Edition.</ref>
== Career ==
He was born as the eldest son of {{ill|Ashizu Kojiro|ja|葦津耕次郎|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ashizu Kojiro|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in Hakozaki, Fukuoka Prefecture (now Hakozaki, {{ill|Higashi-ku, Fukuoka|ja|福岡市東区|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Higashi-ku, Fukuoka|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}). The Ashizu family is a descendant of the Ogami family, one of the shrine families of [[:en:Hakozaki Shrine|Hakozaki Shrine]], and his uncle, {{ill|Ashizu Seizō|ja|葦津洗造|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ashizu Seizō|qq=|1=Ashizu Seizō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q115531037}}, was the chief priest of the same shrine.<ref name="#1">Foreword by {{ill|Ashizu Yasukuni|ja|葦津泰國|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ashizu Yasukuni|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, from ''Living the Shōwa Era: The Claims of Ujihiko Ashizu Series IV''</ref>
In 1922 (Taishō 11), he entered {{ill|Tokyo Metropolitan Koishikawa High School|ja|東京都立小石川高等学校|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokyo Metropolitan Koishikawa High School|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 東京都立小石川中等教育学校|1=Koishikawa Secondary Education School|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11525469}} (then Tokyo Prefectural Fifth Junior High School). Around this time, he yearned for socialism, participated in the Social Science Study Group, and avidly read related books.<ref name="#1"/>
Those around him were concerned, and after graduation, he went on to [[:en:Kokugakuin University|Kokugakuin University]].{{Citation needed|date=July 5, 2017}} He also enrolled in the {{ill|Tokyo School of Foreign Languages|ja|東京外国語学校 (旧制)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokyo School of Foreign Languages|qq=|1=Tokyo School of Foreign Languages|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11524652}}, but dropped out of both.{{Citation needed|date=July 5, 2017}} In 1928 (Shōwa 3), he entered [[:en:Fukushima University|Fukushima University]] (then Fukushima Higher Commercial School), but dropped out the following year.
In 1931, at the recommendation of a friend who belonged to the [[:en:Japanese Communist Party|Japanese Communist Party]], he wrote a leading article for the newspaper [[:en:Shimbun Akahata|Akahata]].<ref name=ashidu>{{Cite journal|author=Konno Nobuyuki|title=The Thought of Ashizu Ujihiko during and after the War—Focusing on His View of Shinto|journal=Interdisciplinary Research on 'Narratives' in Japan from 1890 to the 1950s|page=63|year=2012}}</ref> However, in February of the following year, under the influence of his father, he is said to have converted to traditional conservative imperial [[:en:Shinto|Shinto]], which aimed for the realization of "Japan, the Emperor's Nation with Unity of Rites and Government," like his father.<ref name=ashidu />
In 1932 (Shōwa 7), he succeeded his father's business and engaged in the construction of shrines and temples as the representative of a shrine and temple construction office.<ref>"Fifty Years of the Association of Shinto Shrines" (Non-commercial publication), May 1996, Publisher: [[:en:Association of Shinto Shrines|Association of Shinto Shrines]], p.74—Obituary of Ashizu Ujihiko, associate of {{ill|Jinja Shinpo Sha|ja|神社新報社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jinja Shinpo Sha|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 神社新報|1=Jinja Shinpo Sha|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11590705}} (June 10)</ref> During this period, he interacted with [[:en:Tōyama Mitsuru|Toyama Mitsuru]], {{ill|Teisuke Imaizumi|ja|今泉定助|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Teisuke Imaizumi|qq=|1=Sadasuke Imaizumi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11377248}}, {{ill|Takayama Noboru|ja|高山昇|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takayama Noboru|qq=|1=Noboru Takayama|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11669373}}, [[:en:Taketora Ogata|Ogata Taketora]], [[:en:Ryohei Uchida|Ryōhei Uchida]], {{ill|Inoue Tadamaro|ja|井上孚麿|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Inoue Tadamaro|qq=|1=Takamaro Inoue|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11372944}}, and others. Before the outbreak of the [[:en:Pacific War|Pacific War]], he opposed the war, viewing it as an "unwinnable battle," and after the outbreak, he engaged in unique political activities, such as insisting on adhering only to the war objectives shown in the Imperial Rescript on the outbreak of war, which was "to secure the survival of an independent nation as unavoidable."{{Citation needed|date=July 5, 2017}}
During the war, some of his critical articles on [[:en:Nazi Germany|Nazi Germany]], Japan's ally, and the policies of the [[:en:Hideki Tojo|Hideki Tōjō]] Cabinet were banned, and he was arrested at times.{{Citation needed|date=July 5, 2017}}
Immediately after the war, he dissolved his company and assisted [[:en:Shigeru Yoshida (civil servant)|Shigeru Yoshida (Home Ministry official)]], {{ill|Munenori Miyagawa|ja|宮川宗徳|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Munenori Miyagawa|qq=|1=Munenori Miyagawa|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q27921105}} (later Secretary-General of the main office), and [[:en:Muneyoshi Tokugawa|Tokugawa Munetake]] (later Chief of the main office), dedicating himself to the establishment of the [[:en:Association of Shinto Shrines|Association of Shinto Shrines]]. Furthermore, when the '{{ill|Jinja Shinpo|ja|神社新報|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jinja Shinpo|qq=|1=Jinja Shinpo|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11590705}}' was launched as part of the main office's educational public relations, he took charge of the management of {{ill|Jinja Shinpo Sha|ja|神社新報社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jinja Shinpo Sha|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 神社新報|1=Jinja Shinpo Sha|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11590705}}, the publishing body, along with being the chief editor.
Even after his retirement in 1968 (Shōwa 43), he continued his writing activities and the guidance of future generations, being at the forefront of movements to protect the [[:en:Kokutai|Kokutai]] and [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrines]]. He continued to have a significant influence on the Shinto world (through the {{ill|National Council to Defend Japan|ja|日本を守る国民会議|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/National Council to Defend Japan|qq=|1=National Council to Defend Japan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11506199}}, now [[:en:Nippon Kaigi|Nippon Kaigi]]) and {{ill|Nationalist student movements|ja|民族派学生組織|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nationalist student movements|qq=|1=Nationalist student movements|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11547922}} ({{ill|Japan Youth Council|ja|日本青年協議会|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Japan Youth Council|qq=|1=Japan Youth Council|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11509401}}), among others.
On the other hand, he questioned the enshrinement of {{ill|Class A war criminals|ja|A級戦犯|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Class A war criminals|qq=|1=Class A war criminal|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q5369948}} like Tōjō, whom he had criticized during the war, due to their responsibility for the defeat.<ref>"Yasukuni: Where to Go from 'Postwar' 3—Article by Eminent Scholar of Shinto History Raises Doubts about 'Class A Enshrinement'," ''Mainichi Shimbun'' Tokyo edition morning edition: August 8, 2006</ref> (Note: [[:en:Yasukuni Shrine|Yasukuni Shrine]] is not a [[:en:Beppyo shrine|Beppyo Shrine]] of the Association of Shinto Shrines.)
In 1992 (Heisei 4), he passed away at his home in [[:en:Kamakura|Kamakura]] at the age of 82.
{{ill|Bunzō Hashikawa|ja|橋川文三|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Bunzō Hashikawa|qq=|1=Bunsō Hashikawa|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1588426}}, who had experiences of debates with Ashizu, praised him as "an advanced polemicist among conservatives," noting that his theory of the Emperor system "should be noted as surpassing the level of traditional right-wingers." He pointed out that Ashizu tried to grasp the problem of the Emperor system from a comparative institutional perspective, based on research in European political thought history, pursuing an open form of "Kokutai theory" as his characteristic.<ref>From ''Postwar Japanese Thought Series 7: Conservative Thought,'' edited by Bunzō Hashikawa, Chikuma Shobō, 1968</ref>
{{ill|Yoshio Ōishi|ja|大石義雄|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yoshio Ōishi|qq=|1=Yoshio Ōishi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11438252}}, in ''Twenty Years of the Constitution'', praised Ashizu's ''Words of a Native'' and described Ashizu as "a modern-day Kitabatake Chikafusa."
After the war, he had friendships that transcended ideological positions with {{ill|Saburō Ichi'i|ja|市井三郎|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saburō Ichi'i|qq=|1=Saburō Ichii|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11479994}} and [[:en:Shunsuke Tsurumi|Shunsuke Tsurumi]], who were editors of ''{{ill|Shisō no Kagaku|ja|思想の科学|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shisō no Kagaku|qq=|1=Shisō no Kagaku|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11491520}}'', and the critic [[:en:Yoshimi Takeuchi|Yoshihiko Takeuchi]], and he often contributed to the same magazine.
He was also well-versed in Korean culture and had interactions with [[:en:Lyuh Woon-hyung|Lyuh Woon-hyung]], an independence activist in Korea during the Japanese occupation. It is said that when Ashizu visited the [[:en:Korea University (disambiguation)|Korea University (Japan)]] (in Japan), he was moved upon seeing a portrait of [[:en:Kim Il Sung|Kim Il-sung]] in the middle of the reception room, and remarked, "This is what current Japanese people lack. In the past, the 'Imperial Portrait' of the Emperor was enshrined in every school; we need to restore that situation."<ref>{{Cite book |author=Hideki Kajimura |title=Overcoming Exclusionism: A History of Korea |publisher=Heibonsha Library |year=2014 |page=25}}</ref>
== Family ==
[[File:Iwasaki Seishichi and his friends.jpg|thumb|Nara Tarō Ōmiwa (center of back row)]]
* Father: '''{{ill|Ashizu Kojiro|ja|葦津耕次郎|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ashizu Kojiro|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}''' (1878–1940) – Shintoist.<ref name=nishiya>{{Cite journal|author=Takafumi Nishiya |title=Ashizu Kojiro: The Trajectory of a Shinto Practitioner during the 'State Shinto' Period |volume=Kyoto University Doctoral Thesis |issue=No.14958 |year=2009 |naid=500000489926 |url=https://hdl.handle.net/2433/126522}}</ref> He served at [[:en:Hakozaki Shrine|Hakozaki Shrine]], devoted himself to Shinto service, and later became active as a businessman running mining and shrine construction businesses, while also engaging as a commentator expressing views on political and social issues from the perspective of Shinto and shrines, such as the {{ill|Korean Shrine Deity Controversy|ja|朝鮮神宮御祭神論争|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Korean Shrine Deity Controversy|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.<ref>[http://kaeizyuku.com/2020/01/14/visit-graves/ January 14, 2020 'Visit to the Ashizu Family Grave'] Kaei-juku</ref>
* Grandfather: '''Ashizu Isoo''' (–1903) – Chief priest of Hakozaki Shrine.<ref name=asia>Uraube Noboru, [http://asiarestoration.com/wp-content/uploads/2016/06/daiajia1st.pdf 'Daijia wo Tsuranuita Ōmiwa Asahei'], ''Dai Ajia'' inaugural issue, June 30, 2016, Dai Ajia Kenkyūkai</ref> For a time, he also concurrently served as chief priest of the [[:en:Kashii-gū|Kashii Shrine]].<ref>[https://dl.ndl.go.jp/info:ndljp/pid/2947481/9 Official Gazette. June 26, 1897]</ref>
* Great-uncle: '''{{ill|Chōbei Ōmiwa|ja|大三輪長兵衛|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chōbei Ōmiwa|qq=|1=Ōmiwa Chōbē|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q124483411}}''' (1835–1908) – Brother of his grandfather. After working at an oil shop and rice shop in Osaka, he opened a Matsumae merchant house,<ref>[https://dl.ndl.go.jp/info:ndljp/pid/778835/97 'Chōbei Ōmiwa', ''Meiji Fugo Chifujidai'', Sumitei Inshi (Daigakukan, 1902)]</ref> and later made a fortune in cotton trade, serving as president of the 58th National Bank, chairman of the Osaka Clearing House, Osaka Prefectural Assembly chairman, and Osaka City Council chairman, among other positions. In 1898, he became a member of the [[:en:House of Representatives (Japan)|House of Representatives]].<ref name=sangyo/><ref>[https://www.ndl.go.jp/portrait/datas/36/ 'Chōbei Ōmiwa'], Portraits of Modern Japanese Historical Figures, National Diet Library</ref><ref>[https://kotobank.jp/word/大三輪長兵衛-1061443 'Chōbei Ōmiwa'], Kotobank</ref><ref>[http://www.pref.osaka.lg.jp/attach/3059/00006976/seifuku-itiran_20200522.pdf 'List of Former Chairmen and Vice-Chairmen of Osaka Prefectural Assembly'], Osaka Prefecture, 2020</ref>
* Son: '''{{ill|Ashizu Yasukuni|ja|葦津泰國|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ashizu Yasukuni|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}''' (1937–2024) – Graduate of Tokyo Metropolitan University. Former president of Jinja Shinpo Sha. Author of ''The Life of Chōbei Ōmiwa'' (Ashizu Office, 2008), among others.
== Works ==
[Full list of his publications, including major works and collected editions.]
=== Collected Works ===
* ''Ashizu Ujihiko Selected Works'' – Edited by the same Editorial Committee<ref>Each with commentary by {{ill|Mitsuo Suzuki|ja|鈴木満男|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mitsuo Suzuki|qq=|1=Mitsuo Suzuki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q24872776}} (a disciple)</ref>, Jinja Shinpo Sha, 1996 1. ''Emperor, Shinto, Constitution'' 2. ''As Successors of the Restoration'' 3. ''Current Affairs and Character Critiques''
* ''"Let's Read the Shōwa Era": Ashizu Ujihiko's Claims Series'' – Ashizu Office, 2005–2007 1. ''Japan's Monarchy: Studies on the Emperor System'' 2. ''Eternal Restorationist'' 3. ''The End of Modern Democracy: Revival of Japanese Thought'' 4. ''Words of a Native: The Logic of Trust and Loyalty'' 5. ''Greater Asianism and Toyama Mitsuru'' 6. ''Living the Shōwa Era: The Spirit and Thoughts of the People of the Divine Land''

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:葦津珍彦]][[ko:아시즈 우즈히코]] {{Infobox person|birth_date=1909; {{birth date|1909|7|17}}|death_date={{death date|1992|6|10}}|birth_place={{ill|Fukuoka Prefecture|qid=Q123258}}|gender={{ill|male|qid=Q6581097}}|nationality={{ill|Empire of Japan|qid=Q188712}}; {{ill|Japan|qid=Q17}}|occupation={{ill|critic|qid=Q6430706}}}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11256585]] Wikidata name: Uzuhiko Ashizu [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:19, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Ashizu Ujihiko''' (葦津 珍彦, July 17, 1909 – June 10, 1992) was a Japanese [[:en:Shinto|Shinto]] scholar. He was also renowned as a commentator of the [[:en:Minzoku-ha|Nationalist]] movement.<ref>Yukio Hori, ''Postwar Right-Wing Forces'', Keiso Shobō, 1993, Expanded Edition.</ref>
== Career ==
He was born as the eldest son of {{ill|Ashizu Kojiro|ja|葦津耕次郎|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ashizu Kojiro|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in Hakozaki, Fukuoka Prefecture (now Hakozaki, {{ill|Higashi-ku, Fukuoka|ja|福岡市東区|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Higashi-ku, Fukuoka|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}). The Ashizu family is a descendant of the Ogami family, one of the shrine families of [[:en:Hakozaki Shrine|Hakozaki Shrine]], and his uncle, {{ill|Ashizu Seizō|ja|葦津洗造|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ashizu Seizō|qq=|1=Ashizu Seizō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q115531037}}, was the chief priest of the same shrine.<ref name="#1">Foreword by {{ill|Ashizu Yasukuni|ja|葦津泰國|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ashizu Yasukuni|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, from ''Living the Shōwa Era: The Claims of Ujihiko Ashizu Series IV''</ref>
In 1922 (Taishō 11), he entered {{ill|Tokyo Metropolitan Koishikawa High School|ja|東京都立小石川高等学校|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokyo Metropolitan Koishikawa High School|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 東京都立小石川中等教育学校|1=Koishikawa Secondary Education School|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11525469}} (then Tokyo Prefectural Fifth Junior High School). Around this time, he yearned for socialism, participated in the Social Science Study Group, and avidly read related books.<ref name="#1"/>

In 1931, at the recommendation of a friend who belonged to the [[:en:Japanese Communist Party|Japanese Communist Party]], he wrote a leading article for the newspaper [[:en:Shimbun Akahata|Akahata]].<ref name=ashidu>{{Cite journal|author=Konno Nobuyuki|title=The Thought of Ashizu Ujihiko during and after the War—Focusing on His View of Shinto|journal=Interdisciplinary Research on 'Narratives' in Japan from 1890 to the 1950s|page=63|year=2012}}</ref> However, in February of the following year, under the influence of his father, he is said to have converted to traditional conservative imperial [[:en:Shinto|Shinto]], which aimed for the realization of "Japan, the Emperor's Nation with Unity of Rites and Government," like his father.<ref name=ashidu />
In 1932 (Shōwa 7), he succeeded his father's business and engaged in the construction of shrines and temples as the representative of a shrine and temple construction office.<ref>"Fifty Years of the Association of Shinto Shrines" (Non-commercial publication), May 1996, Publisher: [[:en:Association of Shinto Shrines|Association of Shinto Shrines]], p.74—Obituary of Ashizu Ujihiko, associate of {{ill|Jinja Shinpo Sha|ja|神社新報社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jinja Shinpo Sha|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 神社新報|1=Jinja Shinpo Sha|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11590705}} (June 10)</ref> During this period, he interacted with [[:en:Tōyama Mitsuru|Toyama Mitsuru]], {{ill|Teisuke Imaizumi|ja|今泉定助|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Teisuke Imaizumi|qq=|1=Sadasuke Imaizumi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11377248}}, {{ill|Takayama Noboru|ja|高山昇|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takayama Noboru|qq=|1=Noboru Takayama|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11669373}}, [[:en:Taketora Ogata|Ogata Taketora]], [[:en:Ryohei Uchida|Ryōhei Uchida]], {{ill|Inoue Tadamaro|ja|井上孚麿|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Inoue Tadamaro|qq=|1=Takamaro Inoue|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11372944}}, and others. Before the outbreak of the [[:en:Pacific War|Pacific War]], he opposed the war, viewing it as an "unwinnable battle," and after the outbreak, he engaged in unique political activities, such as insisting on adhering only to the war objectives shown in the Imperial Rescript on the outbreak of war, which was "to secure the survival of an independent nation as unavoidable."{{Citation needed|date=July 5, 2017}}
During the war, some of his critical articles on [[:en:Nazi Germany|Nazi Germany]], Japan's ally, and the policies of the [[:en:Hideki Tojo|Hideki Tōjō]] Cabinet were banned, and he was arrested at times.{{Citation needed|date=July 5, 2017}}
Immediately after the war, he dissolved his company and assisted [[:en:Shigeru Yoshida (civil servant)|Shigeru Yoshida (Home Ministry official)]], {{ill|Munenori Miyagawa|ja|宮川宗徳|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Munenori Miyagawa|qq=|1=Munenori Miyagawa|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q27921105}} (later Secretary-General of the main office), and [[:en:Muneyoshi Tokugawa|Tokugawa Munetake]] (later Chief of the main office), dedicating himself to the establishment of the [[:en:Association of Shinto Shrines|Association of Shinto Shrines]]. Furthermore, when the '{{ill|Jinja Shinpo|ja|神社新報|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jinja Shinpo|qq=|1=Jinja Shinpo|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11590705}}' was launched as part of the main office's educational public relations, he took charge of the management of {{ill|Jinja Shinpo Sha|ja|神社新報社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jinja Shinpo Sha|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 神社新報|1=Jinja Shinpo Sha|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11590705}}, the publishing body, along with being the chief editor.
Even after his retirement in 1968 (Shōwa 43), he continued his writing activities and the guidance of future generations, being at the forefront of movements to protect the [[:en:Kokutai|Kokutai]] and [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrines]]. He continued to have a significant influence on the Shinto world (through the {{ill|National Council to Defend Japan|ja|日本を守る国民会議|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/National Council to Defend Japan|qq=|1=National Council to Defend Japan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11506199}}, now [[:en:Nippon Kaigi|Nippon Kaigi]]) and {{ill|Nationalist student movements|ja|民族派学生組織|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nationalist student movements|qq=|1=Nationalist student movements|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11547922}} ({{ill|Japan Youth Council|ja|日本青年協議会|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Japan Youth Council|qq=|1=Japan Youth Council|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11509401}}), among others.
On the other hand, he questioned the enshrinement of {{ill|Class A war criminals|ja|A級戦犯|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Class A war criminals|qq=|1=Class A war criminal|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q5369948}} like Tōjō, whom he had criticized during the war, due to their responsibility for the defeat.<ref>"Yasukuni: Where to Go from 'Postwar' 3—Article by Eminent Scholar of Shinto History Raises Doubts about 'Class A Enshrinement'," ''Mainichi Shimbun'' Tokyo edition morning edition: August 8, 2006</ref> (Note: [[:en:Yasukuni Shrine|Yasukuni Shrine]] is not a [[:en:Beppyo shrine|Beppyo Shrine]] of the Association of Shinto Shrines.)
In 1992 (Heisei 4), he passed away at his home in [[:en:Kamakura|Kamakura]] at the age of 82.
{{ill|Bunzō Hashikawa|ja|橋川文三|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Bunzō Hashikawa|qq=|1=Bunsō Hashikawa|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1588426}}, who had experiences of debates with Ashizu, praised him as "an advanced polemicist among conservatives," noting that his theory of the Emperor system "should be noted as surpassing the level of traditional right-wingers." He pointed out that Ashizu tried to grasp the problem of the Emperor system from a comparative institutional perspective, based on research in European political thought history, pursuing an open form of "Kokutai theory" as his characteristic.<ref>From ''Postwar Japanese Thought Series 7: Conservative Thought,'' edited by Bunzō Hashikawa, Chikuma Shobō, 1968</ref>
{{ill|Yoshio Ōishi|ja|大石義雄|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yoshio Ōishi|qq=|1=Yoshio Ōishi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11438252}}, in ''Twenty Years of the Constitution'', praised Ashizu's ''Words of a Native'' and described Ashizu as "a modern-day Kitabatake Chikafusa."
After the war, he had friendships that transcended ideological positions with {{ill|Saburō Ichi'i|ja|市井三郎|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saburō Ichi'i|qq=|1=Saburō Ichii|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11479994}} and [[:en:Shunsuke Tsurumi|Shunsuke Tsurumi]], who were editors of ''{{ill|Shisō no Kagaku|ja|思想の科学|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shisō no Kagaku|qq=|1=Shisō no Kagaku|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11491520}}'', and the critic [[:en:Yoshimi Takeuchi|Yoshihiko Takeuchi]], and he often contributed to the same magazine.
He was also well-versed in Korean culture and had interactions with [[:en:Lyuh Woon-hyung|Lyuh Woon-hyung]], an independence activist in Korea during the Japanese occupation. It is said that when Ashizu visited the [[:en:Korea University (disambiguation)|Korea University (Japan)]] (in Japan), he was moved upon seeing a portrait of [[:en:Kim Il Sung|Kim Il-sung]] in the middle of the reception room, and remarked, "This is what current Japanese people lack. In the past, the 'Imperial Portrait' of the Emperor was enshrined in every school; we need to restore that situation."<ref>{{Cite book |author=Hideki Kajimura |title=Overcoming Exclusionism: A History of Korea |publisher=Heibonsha Library |year=2014 |page=25}}</ref>
== Family ==
[[File:Iwasaki Seishichi and his friends.jpg|thumb|Nara Tarō Ōmiwa (center of back row)]]
* Father: '''{{ill|Ashizu Kojiro|ja|葦津耕次郎|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ashizu Kojiro|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}''' (1878–1940) – Shintoist.<ref name=nishiya>{{Cite journal|author=Takafumi Nishiya |title=Ashizu Kojiro: The Trajectory of a Shinto Practitioner during the 'State Shinto' Period |volume=Kyoto University Doctoral Thesis |issue=No.14958 |year=2009 |naid=500000489926 |url=https://hdl.handle.net/2433/126522}}</ref> He served at [[:en:Hakozaki Shrine|Hakozaki Shrine]], devoted himself to Shinto service, and later became active as a businessman running mining and shrine construction businesses, while also engaging as a commentator expressing views on political and social issues from the perspective of Shinto and shrines, such as the {{ill|Korean Shrine Deity Controversy|ja|朝鮮神宮御祭神論争|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Korean Shrine Deity Controversy|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.<ref>[http://kaeizyuku.com/2020/01/14/visit-graves/ January 14, 2020 'Visit to the Ashizu Family Grave'] Kaei-juku</ref>
* Grandfather: '''Ashizu Isoo''' (–1903) – Chief priest of Hakozaki Shrine.<ref name=asia>Uraube Noboru, [http://asiarestoration.com/wp-content/uploads/2016/06/daiajia1st.pdf 'Daijia wo Tsuranuita Ōmiwa Asahei'], ''Dai Ajia'' inaugural issue, June 30, 2016, Dai Ajia Kenkyūkai</ref> For a time, he also concurrently served as chief priest of the [[:en:Kashii-gū|Kashii Shrine]].<ref>[https://dl.ndl.go.jp/info:ndljp/pid/2947481/9 Official Gazette. June 26, 1897]</ref>
* Great-uncle: '''{{ill|Chōbei Ōmiwa|ja|大三輪長兵衛|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chōbei Ōmiwa|qq=|1=Ōmiwa Chōbē|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q124483411}}''' (1835–1908) – Brother of his grandfather. After working at an oil shop and rice shop in Osaka, he opened a Matsumae merchant house,<ref>[https://dl.ndl.go.jp/info:ndljp/pid/778835/97 'Chōbei Ōmiwa', ''Meiji Fugo Chifujidai'', Sumitei Inshi (Daigakukan, 1902)]</ref> and later made a fortune in cotton trade, serving as president of the 58th National Bank, chairman of the Osaka Clearing House, Osaka Prefectural Assembly chairman, and Osaka City Council chairman, among other positions. In 1898, he became a member of the [[:en:House of Representatives (Japan)|House of Representatives]].<ref name=sangyo/><ref>[https://www.ndl.go.jp/portrait/datas/36/ 'Chōbei Ōmiwa'], Portraits of Modern Japanese Historical Figures, National Diet Library</ref><ref>[https://kotobank.jp/word/大三輪長兵衛-1061443 'Chōbei Ōmiwa'], Kotobank</ref><ref>[http://www.pref.osaka.lg.jp/attach/3059/00006976/seifuku-itiran_20200522.pdf 'List of Former Chairmen and Vice-Chairmen of Osaka Prefectural Assembly'], Osaka Prefecture, 2020</ref>
* Son: '''{{ill|Ashizu Yasukuni|ja|葦津泰國|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ashizu Yasukuni|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}''' (1937–2024) – Graduate of Tokyo Metropolitan University. Former president of Jinja Shinpo Sha. Author of ''The Life of Chōbei Ōmiwa'' (Ashizu Office, 2008), among others.

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:葦津珍彦]][[ko:아시즈 우즈히코]] {{Infobox person|birth_date=1909; {{birth date|1909|7|17}}|death_date={{death date|1992|6|10}}|birth_place={{ill|Fukuoka Prefecture|WD=Q123258}}|gender={{ill|male|WD=Q6581097}}|nationality={{ill|Empire of Japan|WD=Q188712}}; {{ill|Japan|WD=Q17}}|occupation={{ill|critic|WD=Q6430706}}}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11256585]] Wikidata name: Uzuhiko Ashizu [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:19, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Ashizu Ujihiko''' (葦津 珍彦, July 17, 1909 – June 10, 1992) was a Japanese [[:en:Shinto|Shinto]] scholar. He was also renowned as a commentator of the [[:en:Minzoku-ha|Nationalist]] movement.<ref>Yukio Hori, ''Postwar Right-Wing Forces'', Keiso Shobō, 1993, Expanded Edition.</ref>
== Career ==
He was born as the eldest son of {{ill|Ashizu Kojiro|ja|葦津耕次郎|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ashizu Kojiro|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in Hakozaki, Fukuoka Prefecture (now Hakozaki, {{ill|Higashi-ku, Fukuoka|ja|福岡市東区|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Higashi-ku, Fukuoka|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}). The Ashizu family is a descendant of the Ogami family, one of the shrine families of [[:en:Hakozaki Shrine|Hakozaki Shrine]], and his uncle, {{ill|Ashizu Seizō|ja|葦津洗造|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ashizu Seizō|qq=|1=Ashizu Seizō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q115531037}}, was the chief priest of the same shrine.<ref name="#1">Foreword by {{ill|Ashizu Yasukuni|ja|葦津泰國|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ashizu Yasukuni|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, from ''Living the Shōwa Era: The Claims of Ujihiko Ashizu Series IV''</ref>
In 1922 (Taishō 11), he entered {{ill|Tokyo Metropolitan Koishikawa High School|ja|東京都立小石川高等学校|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokyo Metropolitan Koishikawa High School|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 東京都立小石川中等教育学校|1=Koishikawa Secondary Education School|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11525469}} (then Tokyo Prefectural Fifth Junior High School). Around this time, he yearned for socialism, participated in the Social Science Study Group, and avidly read related books.<ref name="#1"/>
Those around him were concerned, and after graduation, he went on to [[:en:Kokugakuin University|Kokugakuin University]].{{Citation needed|date=July 5, 2017}} He also enrolled in the {{ill|Tokyo School of Foreign Languages|ja|東京外国語学校 (旧制)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokyo School of Foreign Languages|qq=|1=Tokyo School of Foreign Languages|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11524652}}, but dropped out of both.{{Citation needed|date=July 5, 2017}} In 1928 (Shōwa 3), he entered [[:en:Fukushima University|Fukushima University]] (then Fukushima Higher Commercial School), but dropped out the following year.
In 1931, at the recommendation of a friend who belonged to the [[:en:Japanese Communist Party|Japanese Communist Party]], he wrote a leading article for the newspaper [[:en:Shimbun Akahata|Akahata]].<ref name=ashidu>{{Cite journal|author=Konno Nobuyuki|title=The Thought of Ashizu Ujihiko during and after the War—Focusing on His View of Shinto|journal=Interdisciplinary Research on 'Narratives' in Japan from 1890 to the 1950s|page=63|year=2012}}</ref> However, in February of the following year, under the influence of his father, he is said to have converted to traditional conservative imperial [[:en:Shinto|Shinto]], which aimed for the realization of "Japan, the Emperor's Nation with Unity of Rites and Government," like his father.<ref name=ashidu />
In 1932 (Shōwa 7), he succeeded his father's business and engaged in the construction of shrines and temples as the representative of a shrine and temple construction office.<ref>"Fifty Years of the Association of Shinto Shrines" (Non-commercial publication), May 1996, Publisher: [[:en:Association of Shinto Shrines|Association of Shinto Shrines]], p.74—Obituary of Ashizu Ujihiko, associate of {{ill|Jinja Shinpo Sha|ja|神社新報社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jinja Shinpo Sha|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 神社新報|1=Jinja Shinpo Sha|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11590705}} (June 10)</ref> During this period, he interacted with [[:en:Tōyama Mitsuru|Toyama Mitsuru]], {{ill|Teisuke Imaizumi|ja|今泉定助|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Teisuke Imaizumi|qq=|1=Sadasuke Imaizumi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11377248}}, {{ill|Takayama Noboru|ja|高山昇|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takayama Noboru|qq=|1=Noboru Takayama|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11669373}}, [[:en:Taketora Ogata|Ogata Taketora]], [[:en:Ryohei Uchida|Ryōhei Uchida]], {{ill|Inoue Tadamaro|ja|井上孚麿|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Inoue Tadamaro|qq=|1=Takamaro Inoue|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11372944}}, and others. Before the outbreak of the [[:en:Pacific War|Pacific War]], he opposed the war, viewing it as an "unwinnable battle," and after the outbreak, he engaged in unique political activities, such as insisting on adhering only to the war objectives shown in the Imperial Rescript on the outbreak of war, which was "to secure the survival of an independent nation as unavoidable."{{Citation needed|date=July 5, 2017}}
During the war, some of his critical articles on [[:en:Nazi Germany|Nazi Germany]], Japan's ally, and the policies of the [[:en:Hideki Tojo|Hideki Tōjō]] Cabinet were banned, and he was arrested at times.{{Citation needed|date=July 5, 2017}}
Immediately after the war, he dissolved his company and assisted [[:en:Shigeru Yoshida (civil servant)|Shigeru Yoshida (Home Ministry official)]], {{ill|Munenori Miyagawa|ja|宮川宗徳|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Munenori Miyagawa|qq=|1=Munenori Miyagawa|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q27921105}} (later Secretary-General of the main office), and [[:en:Muneyoshi Tokugawa|Tokugawa Munetake]] (later Chief of the main office), dedicating himself to the establishment of the [[:en:Association of Shinto Shrines|Association of Shinto Shrines]]. Furthermore, when the '{{ill|Jinja Shinpo|ja|神社新報|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jinja Shinpo|qq=|1=Jinja Shinpo|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11590705}}' was launched as part of the main office's educational public relations, he took charge of the management of {{ill|Jinja Shinpo Sha|ja|神社新報社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jinja Shinpo Sha|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 神社新報|1=Jinja Shinpo Sha|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11590705}}, the publishing body, along with being the chief editor.
Even after his retirement in 1968 (Shōwa 43), he continued his writing activities and the guidance of future generations, being at the forefront of movements to protect the [[:en:Kokutai|Kokutai]] and [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrines]]. He continued to have a significant influence on the Shinto world (through the {{ill|National Council to Defend Japan|ja|日本を守る国民会議|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/National Council to Defend Japan|qq=|1=National Council to Defend Japan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11506199}}, now [[:en:Nippon Kaigi|Nippon Kaigi]]) and {{ill|Nationalist student movements|ja|民族派学生組織|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nationalist student movements|qq=|1=Nationalist student movements|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11547922}} ({{ill|Japan Youth Council|ja|日本青年協議会|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Japan Youth Council|qq=|1=Japan Youth Council|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11509401}}), among others.
On the other hand, he questioned the enshrinement of {{ill|Class A war criminals|ja|A級戦犯|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Class A war criminals|qq=|1=Class A war criminal|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q5369948}} like Tōjō, whom he had criticized during the war, due to their responsibility for the defeat.<ref>"Yasukuni: Where to Go from 'Postwar' 3—Article by Eminent Scholar of Shinto History Raises Doubts about 'Class A Enshrinement'," ''Mainichi Shimbun'' Tokyo edition morning edition: August 8, 2006</ref> (Note: [[:en:Yasukuni Shrine|Yasukuni Shrine]] is not a [[:en:Beppyo shrine|Beppyo Shrine]] of the Association of Shinto Shrines.)
In 1992 (Heisei 4), he passed away at his home in [[:en:Kamakura|Kamakura]] at the age of 82.

He pointed out that Ashizu tried to grasp the problem of the Emperor system from a comparative institutional perspective, based on research in European political thought history, pursuing an open form of "Kokutai theory" as his characteristic.<ref>From ''Postwar Japanese Thought Series 7: Conservative Thought,'' edited by Bunzō Hashikawa, Chikuma Shobō, 1968</ref>
